items.title: AMD’s new X100 chip lineup puts Strix Halo into robots – APUs for physical AI bring Zen 5 CPU, RDNA 3.5 GPU cores to compete with Intel’s Panther Lake
items.description: Countering Intel’s recent moves, AMD is bringing its Strix Halo APUs to the realm of robots, and physical AI. Designed for 24/7 operation and a 10-year embedded lifecycle, X100 will also be offered as a Kria System on Module (SOM) robot developer platform.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter AMD is bringing its Strix Halo APUs into the world of physical AI. The new X100 series of processors come with similar specs as the various Ryzen AI Max models floating around in client devices, but they’re tailored for 24/7 operation, with a 10-year lifecycle in embedded applications like robotics.There are three SKUs that align with the three original Strix Halo models (not the updated versions with 40 CUs). The top-end X199 comes with 16 Zen 5 cores and 40 RDNA 3.5 CUs. The X188 steps down to 12 cores and 32 CUs, while the X168 comes with eight cores and the same 32 CUs. AMD hasn’t shared detailed specifications for each model, but the company says the range goes up to a 5.1 GHz boost clock and 128 GB of unified memory. They also include an XDNA 2 NPU with up to 50 TOPS, a configurable TDP between 45W and 120W, and operating temperature between -40 degrees Celsius up to 105 degrees. Image 1 of 3 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 3 View Original Image 2 of 3 View Original Image 3 of 3 View Original AMD’s range bites back at Intel, which launched a range of Panther Lake SoCs for physical AI at the beginning of the year. Both make an argument for SoCs in robotics, reducing latency when the CPU, AI accelerator, and memory are fragmented across separate chips. The X100 range is just physically larger than Panther Lake, packing much more silicon on the SoC for more powerful deployments.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: (Image credit: AMD)The company shared a range of benchmarks comparing the flagship X199 against Intel’s Core Ultra X7 358H, a 16-core chip with Intel’s Arc B390 iGPU that has 12 Xe3 cores. AMD claims a lead of 1.2X and 1.3X, respectively, in GeekBench 6.1 and PassMark, as well as 1.5X in an unofficial SPECrate 2017 run looking at integer workloads. In graphics, AMD unsurprisingly takes the edge with 1.4X faster Vulkan and 1.7X faster OpenGL performance (both measured with GFXBench 5 on Ubuntu), as well as a 1.6X lead in Unigine Heaven Extreme.On the physical AI front, AMD claims a 1.4X improvement in Time to First Token (TTFT) and 3.5X faster tokens per second in Llama-bench, with a Vulkan backend running at a 45W TDP. These results need a massive dash of salt, however. You may like AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 ‘Gorgon Halo’ packs up to 192GB of unified memory AMD executives react to Nvidia’s RTX Spark AMD Ryzen AI 5 435G APU goes head-to-head with the Ryzen 5 8600G in early benchmarks AMD tested the Ryzen AI Max 395+ “configured to reflect Ryzen AI Embedded X199 specifications.” It tested on the Maple reference board with a 5.1 GHz CPU clock, 2.9 GHz GPU clock, and sustained 45W TDP. The X7 358H, meanwhile, was tested in an MSI Prestige 16 Flip AI+ with an enforced TDP limit of 30W. AMD then “projected” 45W performance on the Intel chip “using scaling factors derived from public benchmark data.”It’s not exactly an apples-to-apples comparison, in other words. There’s some sort of proxy stand-in or extrapolation of data across all of the benchmarks here, so keep that in mind as we work through the rest of AMD’s X100 announcements.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsAMD X100 Kria SOM and robotics developer platform Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original utside of the chips themselves, AMD is offering X100 models as part of a Kria System on Module (SOM) or an integrated robotics developer platform. The Kria X100 board measures 120mm x 120mm and conforms to the standardized COM-HPC form factor. If you’re a developer that wants to develop for the board, AMD is offering its Kria AI robotics developer platform.It’s a fully-integrated box, leveraging the X100 Kria SOM alongside AMD’s Spartan UltraScale+ FPGA baseboard. AMD says it’s a “turnkey” solution for robotics development, including specialized connectivity for cameras and industrial networking, along with robotic sensors. The platform is available in early access now, and AMD says it’ll be in full production in Q4 of this year. . (Image credit: AMD)AMD shared some benchmarks for the X100 Kria, as well, comparing it to Nvidia’s Thor T5000. These benchmarks weren’t run internally at AMD. They were commissioned by AMD and ran by Open Navigation and Mimix. Critically, the benchmarks didn’t test an X100 Kria board, or at least, not exactly in the form it will take once it’s inside a robot or AMD’s developer box. What to read next AMD challenges Nvidia's DGX Spark with $3,999 Ryzen AI Halo with Windows 11 support AMD Ryzen AI Halo review: AMD builds a DGX Spark of its own Intel and AMD's new ACE CPU extensions bring an efficient AI-oriented instruction set to x86 Instead, AMD is comparing Nvidia’s Jetson AGX Thor developer kit to a GMKtech EVO-X2 AI mini PC with a Ryzen AI Max+ 395 “configured to reflect Ryzen AI embedded x199 specifications.” Naturally, the thermal and power environment of these chips will heavily influence performance. Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original AMD is continuing its attempt to siphon developers away from Nvidia’s CUDA platform for development, as well. It’s HIPIFY tool converts CUDA code to AMD’s HIP C++ portable code, and the company claims it can now handle 70-80% of the “effort” of porting on its own. AMD tested on a Ryzen AI Max+ 395, once again configured to match the X199, and it ported 15 CUDA applications, comprising 1,199 lines of code, to arrive at that 70% to 80% range.X100 Kria lives at the “brain” of the robotics platform, but AMD envisions an end-to-end solution for humanoid-style robots with its Spartan UltraScale+. Zynq UltraScale+, and Versal AI Edge Gen 2 FPGAs and SoCs Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Jake Roach
items.titleZh: AMD新款X100芯片系列将Strix Halo引入机器人——用于物理AI的APU带来Zen 5 CPU和RDNA 3.5 GPU核心，与英特尔的Panther Lake竞争
items.descriptionZh:
AMD近期将其Strix Halo APU产品线延伸至物理AI领域，正式推出面向嵌入式应用的X100系列处理器。该系列与消费级Ryzen AI Max系列在规格上高度一致，但专为7x24小时不间断运行设计，具备长达10年的生命周期，主要面向机器人等工业嵌入式场景。

**核心规格与技术架构**

X100系列包含三款SKU，对应原始Strix Halo的三款型号（非更新后的40 CU版本）。旗舰型号X199配备16个Zen 5 CPU核心和40个RDNA 3.5计算单元（CU）；X188降级为12核心和32 CU；X168则为8核心加32 CU。AMD未公布每款型号的详细参数，但表示全系列最高支持5.1 GHz加速频率、128 GB统一内存，并集成XDNA 2 NPU，AI算力可达50 TOPS。可配置TDP范围在45W至120W之间，工作温度覆盖-40°C到105°C，适应严苛工业环境。

**性能对比与争议数据**

AMD将X100系列直接对标Intel年初发布的Panther Lake SoC（同样面向物理AI）。AMD强调，将CPU、AI加速器和内存集成于单一SoC可显著降低延迟，而X100系列由于集成了更多硅片，在物理尺寸上大于Panther Lake，适用于更强大的部署场景。

在基准测试中，AMD将旗舰X199与Intel Core Ultra X7 358H（16核，配备12个Xe3核心的Arc B390 iGPU）进行对比，声称在GeekBench 6.1和PassMark中分别领先1.2倍和1.3倍，在SPECrate 2017整数负载中领先1.5倍。图形性能方面，X199在Vulkan（GFXBench 5，Ubuntu）下快1.4倍，OpenGL快1.7倍，Unigine Heaven Extreme快1.6倍。在物理AI场景中，AMD声称Llama-bench（45W TDP，Vulkan后端）的首Token延迟（TTFT）提升1.4倍，每秒Token数提升3.5倍。

然而，这些测试条件存在明显不对等。AMD使用的测试平台是Ryzen AI Max 395+（配置模拟X199规格），在Maple参考板上以5.1 GHz CPU、2.9 GHz GPU频率和持续45W TDP运行；而Intel芯片则在MSI Prestige 16 Flip AI+笔记本上测试，强制TDP限制为30W，随后AMD“根据公开基准数据推导”将其缩放至45W性能。AMD自己承认这不是“苹果对苹果”的比较，数据仅供参考，需谨慎解读。

**Kria SOM与机器人开发平台**

除芯片本身，AMD还提供基于X100的Kria系统级模块（SOM）和集成机器人开发平台。Kria X100板卡尺寸120mm x 120mm，符合标准COM-HPC规格。开发者可使用Kria AI机器人开发平台，这是一个“交钥匙”解决方案，包含X100 Kria SOM和AMD Spartan UltraScale+ FPGA基板，提供摄像头、工业网络和机器人传感器的专用连接。该平台现已开放早期访问，AMD计划2025年Q4全面量产。

AMD还公布了X100 Kria与Nvidia Thor T5000的对比基准测试，但这些测试并非AMD内部执行，而是由Open Navigation和Mimix受委托完成。关键问题是，测试并未直接使用X100 Kria板卡本身，而是用一台搭载Ryzen AI Max+ 395的GMKtech EVO-X2 AI迷你PC（配置模拟X199规格）与Nvidia Jetson AGX Thor开发者套件对比。显然，热设计和功率环境对性能影响巨大。

**CUDA生态迁移与端到端方案**

AMD继续推动开发者从Nvidia CUDA平台迁移至其ROCm生态。其HIPIFY工具可将CUDA代码转换为AMD HIP C++可移植代码，AMD称该工具可自动处理70%~80%的移植工作量（在15个CUDA应用、共计1199行代码的测试中测得）。测试同样基于Ryzen AI Max+ 395配置。

在更宏观的机器人方案中，AMD将X100 Kria定位为“大脑”，并搭配Spartan UltraScale+、Zynq UltraScale+和Versal AI Edge Gen 2 FPGA/SoC，形成面向人形机器人等复杂系统的端到端解决方案。

items.title: AMD takes the wraps off its Instinct MI455X AI accelerator — CDNA 5 and Helios rack-scale architecture combine to take the fight to Nvidia in the data center
items.description: AMD showed off its MI455X accelerator at its Advancing AI 2026 event, demonstrating its strong competitive performance, large HBM memory capacity, and Helios rack-scale architecture.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter At AMD’s Advancing AI event this week, the company revealed more details of its upcoming MI455X GPU and the Helios rack-scale architecture that will join 72 of those GPUs into a coherent accelerator—the largest such system that AMD has built so far and its first to truly compete with Nvidia’s NVL72 rack-scale design, as used in the Blackwell and Rubin generations.AMD calls the MI455X “by leaps and bounds the most advanced AI accelerator we’ve ever built,” and from what we’ve seen, it’s the most competitive product at both the chip level and at rack scale that AMD has ever put up against Nvidia’s thorough dominance of the AI compute race. (Image credit: AMD)The full MI455X GPU is a massive chip encompassing 320 billion transistors, and it’s built up using advanced packaging technologies. Four Accelerator Complex Dies (XCDs) are stacked on top of each Fabric and Cache Die (FCD) using hybrid bonding. In turn, the two FCDs are each joined to six stacks of HBM4, the two I/O dies, and to one another using TSMC’s CoWoS-L technology.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: (Image credit: AMD)This chiplet design lets AMD use the most advanced TSMC 2N gate-all-around (GAA) process technology on the XCDs, where it’s most beneficial for power and performance, while the FCDs and I/O dies, which contain elements that don’t benefit from the densest process technologies, are fabricated on TSMC N3P.CDNA 5 represents a large shift in the shape of the CDNA architecture. AMD now calls the fundamental building block of the CDNA 5 Accelerator Complex Die a “Work Group Processor” instead of a “Compute Unit,” but in practice, the basic layout of the rest of the Accelerated Complex Die (XCD) is largely similar. You may like AMD's Helios MI455X AI platform breaks cover, initial systems use UALink-over-Ethernet interconnects Microsoft will deploy AMD’s Helios rack-scale AI accelerator ‘at scale’ on Azure AMD announces MI350P PCIe AI accelerator card with 144GB of HBM3E — roughly 40% faster in FP16 and FP8 theoretical compute compared to Nvidia's H200 NVL competitor (Image credit: AMD)The number of WGPs on the MI455X remains the same as on the MI355X at 256. Because there hasn’t been a change to the number of fundamental compute resources on the chip, the per-WGP throughput on the CDNA 5 MI455X has to be much higher than on the MI355X to deliver its large performance boost.Among the many other changes for this generation, CDNA 5 marks a major shift for the programming model of an Instinct GPU. The width of a wavefront, or group of work items or threads that each workgroup processor addresses, is now 32 instead of 64, a choice AMD says improves instruction latency, branch divergence penalties, and register pressure. It further explains that a 32-wide approach increases the flexibility of the architecture for interacting with different tensor tile sizes and mapping compute kernels to the hardware. RDNA GPUs have used a native wavefront size of 32 since their introduction.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors (Image credit: AMD)CDNA 5 greatly enhances compute performance over CDNA 4, theoretically doubling and in some cases quadrupling the peak FLOPS possible from the chip. The MI455X especially benefits lower-precision floating-point formats now common for use in inference. OCP MXFP8 and MXFP4 formats are theoretically up to 4X faster than on the CDNA 4 MI355X.Here’s an overview of the MI455X’s theoretical peak FLOPS compared to both the previous-generation MI355X and Nvidia’s Rubin GPU. Swipe to scroll horizontally AMD Instinct MI455X Peak FLOPS Row 0 - Cell 0 Instinct MI355X Instinct MI455X Nvidia Rubin NVFP4 (dense) -- -- 35 PF OCP MXFP4 10 PF 40.26 PF -- OCP MXFP6 10 PF 20.13 PF 17.5 PF OCP MXFP8 10 PF 20.13 PF 17.5 PF Matrix FP16/BF16 2.5 PF 5.03 PF 4 PF Vector FP16 157.3 TF 315 TF -- Matrix FP32 157.3 TF 315 TF 400 TF Vector FP32 157.3 TF 315 TF 130 TF At least on paper, the MI455X offers peak performance that bests what Nvidia has shown for Rubin so far, and in combination with the Helios rack-scale architecture that finally gives AMD the same 72-GPU coherent domain as Nvidia’s NVL72 rack-scale design, AMD is more competitive in the AI data center capacity race than ever before. And that’s translating into real customer wins, as AMD has announced pivotal deals with Microsoft and Anthropic this week. What to read next AMD and Cerebras partner on low-latency, high-throughput AI inference AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 ‘Gorgon Halo’ packs up to 192GB of unified memory AMD to supply Anthropic with 2 gigawatts of Instinct MI450 GPUs But we’d be careful about drawing too many conclusions from these head-to-head peak FLOPS numbers, as the realized performance of these GPUs in the real world is likely to be much lower in practice, as AMD itself admitted in one of our sessions. The ongoing challenge will be to optimize software and applications to achieve as much of that theoretical performance as is possible.Beyond its general compute improvements, AMD is also touting improved transcendental math performance from the CDNA 5 Transcendental Unit, which has wide-reaching implications for performance on essential AI functions like softmax, neural network activations, and attention. AMD says that the CDNA 5 Transcendental Unit doubles throughput versus CDNA 4. The Transcendental Unit also adds support for an explicit tanh instruction, which is useful for certain operations on hidden layers within neural networks. (Image credit: AMD)AMD also deeply revised the cache and memory hierarchy on the MI455X. The large Infinity Cache on CDNA 4 has been ditched in favor of a smaller but higher-bandwidth shared L2 cache on each Fabric Compute Die. Each FCD has a 96MB L2 slice for 192MB in total, compared to just 32MB backed by the 256MB Infinity Cache on the MI355X. AMD says this cache offers 1.5X higher bandwidth per FCD compared to the CDNA 4 Infinity Cache, so in aggregate, the MI455X has 3X the L2 bandwidth compared to the MI355X.Within the WGP, larger, faster, and more flexible caches are now available. The local data store (LDS) or scratchpad memory has doubled in size versus CDNA 4, to 320 KB, and in total, there is 96 MB of LDS across the chip, twice that of the largest CDNA 4 implementation on the MI355X. AMD says the LDS SRAM offers higher read and write bandwidth per clock than in the previous generation, but it didn’t provide specifics.Critically, the main memory architecture of MI455X has moved to HBM4 for this generation. AMD uses six stacks of HBM4 per FCD for a total of 12 on the chip, offering a memory capacity of 432GB and memory bandwidth of 23.3 TB/s per GPU. High memory capacity and bandwidth are both crucial for keeping ever-expanding AI model weights and KV caches close to the GPU for the best performance.This HBM implementation is one of the MI455X’s strongest advantages over Nvidia’s Rubin in isolation. It offers both much higher capacity and slightly higher bandwidth than the first Rubin GPU that Nvidia detailed this week. In its initial configuration, Rubin only offers 288GB of HBM4 with up to 22 TB/s of bandwidth.At rack scale, the MI455X’s higher HBM capacity adds up to 31.1 TB of HBM across 72 GPUs, or a whopping 50% more than the 20.7 TB aggregate capacity of Vera Rubin. And the Helios rack-scale architecture is AMD’s first to join all of that GPU memory into a single coherent domain. Since AI inference performance is dominated by both data locality and memory bandwidth, AMD’s advantages in this regard are sure to attract plenty of attention. Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original Efficient data movement across the chip is a key consideration for reducing power consumption and improving performance on the MI455X.The CDNA 5 Tensor Data Mover is an improved version of the data movement engine in past CDNA generations. Like Nvidia’s Tensor Memory Accelerator, the TDM accelerates the production of certain tensor-specific memory addresses and facilitates moving the related data from memory without involving the shader engines. In the CDNA 5 generation, the TDM gains the ability to move data directly from DRAM to the WGP LDS cache without staging data at intermediate cache levels.AMD has also added multicast support for memory reads to the WGPs to take advantage of the fact that AI workloads often need to work on the same weights and activations across multiple WGPs at once. By using multicast, a single memory read can be amplified across many WGPs, increasing effective memory bandwidth, reducing duplicated traffic on the bus, and lowering power consumption. Nvidia has had a similar capability in its GPUs since Hopper, but given AMD’s intense focus on efficient data movement this generation, it’s perhaps unsurprising to see a version of it added here.All told, the MI455X is AMD’s most compelling Instinct product yet. It delivers a massive generational performance improvement, it puts up peak FLOPS on paper that are competitive with or even superior to Nvidia’s upcoming Rubin GPU, and it offers a massive 432GB pool of HBM4 memory that’s 1.5x larger and even slightly faster than Rubin’s 288GB complement.Combined with the Helios rack-scale architecture and its 72-accelerator scale-up domain—AMD’s first true answer to Nvidia’s NVL72 rack-scale design—we should expect the race for AI compute superiority to really heat up in the second half of this year as both Nvidia and AMD begin delivering their next-generation products to customers. Image 1 of 17 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 17 View Original Image 2 of 17 View Original Image 3 of 17 View Original Image 4 of 17 View Original Image 5 of 17 View Original Image 6 of 17 View Original Image 7 of 17 View Original Image 8 of 17 View Original Image 9 of 17 View Original Image 10 of 17 View Original Image 11 of 17 View Original Image 12 of 17 View Original Image 13 of 17 View Original Image 14 of 17 View Original Image 15 of 17 View Original Image 16 of 17 View Original Image 17 of 17 View Original Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Jeffrey Kampman
items.titleZh: AMD 发布 Instinct MI455X AI 加速器——CDNA 5 与 Helios 机架级架构联手，在数据中心领域向英伟达发起挑战
items.descriptionZh:
在AMD本周举办的“Advancing AI”活动上，该公司披露了其即将推出的MI455X GPU以及与之配套的Helios机架级架构的更多细节。Helios架构将72块MI455X GPU整合为一个统一的加速器——这是AMD迄今为止构建的最大规模此类系统，也是其首款真正能与Nvidia Blackwell和Rubin世代中使用的NVL72机架级设计相抗衡的产品。

AMD称MI455X是“迄今为止我们构建的最先进的AI加速器，且遥遥领先”。从已公布的信息来看，无论在芯片层面还是机架规模上，这都堪称AMD在AI计算赛道上对抗Nvidia全面统治地位时最具竞争力的产品。

### 芯片设计与封装技术

MI455X GPU是一颗巨大的芯片，集成了3200亿个晶体管，并采用了先进的封装技术。每个Fabric和Cache Die（FCD）上通过混合键合堆叠了四个加速器复合芯片（XCD）。两个FCD各自连接到六组HBM4内存、两个I/O芯片，并通过台积电的CoWoS-L技术彼此相连。

这种小芯片设计使得AMD能够在XCD上使用最先进的台积电2N环绕栅极（GAA）工艺，以最大化功耗和性能优势；而FCD和I/O芯片中那些无需最先进工艺的元件，则采用台积电N3P工艺制造。

### CDNA 5架构：核心技术原理与创新

CDNA 5代表了CDNA架构的重大变革。AMD现在将CDNA 5加速器复合芯片（XCD）的基本构建单元称为“工作组处理器”（Work Group Processor, WGP），而非之前的“计算单元”（Compute Unit）。不过，XCD其余部分的布局大致相似。MI455X上的WGP数量与MI355X相同，均为256个。由于芯片基本计算资源数量未变，CDNA 5的MI455X必须通过大幅提升每WGP的吞吐量来实现巨大的性能提升。

本代架构的另一个重大变化是编程模型：wavefront（每个工作组处理器处理的一组工作项或线程）的宽度从64变为32。AMD称，这一选择改善了指令延迟、分支分歧惩罚和寄存器压力，同时提高了架构在处理不同张量块大小以及将计算内核映射到硬件时的灵活性。RDNA GPU自推出以来一直使用原生32宽wavefront。

CDNA 5在计算性能上较CDNA 4大幅提升，理论峰值FLOPS在某些情况下翻倍，甚至达到四倍。MI455X尤其受益于低精度浮点格式（如今在推理中很常见）。OCP MXFP8和MXFP4格式的理论性能相比CDNA 4 MI355X最高提升4倍。

### 性能参数与对比数据

以下是MI455X与上一代MI355X以及Nvidia Rubin GPU的理论峰值FLOPS对比表（表格具体数值见原文）。至少在纸面上，MI455X的峰值性能超越了Nvidia目前公布的Rubin数据，再加上Helios机架级架构终于为AMD提供了与Nvidia NVL72相同的72-GPU一致域，AMD在AI数据中心容量竞赛中的竞争力前所未有。本周AMD已宣布与微软和Anthropic达成关键交易。

但需要谨慎的是，不应从这些直接的峰值FLOPS数据中得出过多结论，因为实际运行性能很可能远低于理论值——AMD自己也在某次会议中承认了这一点。持续的挑战在于优化软件和应用，以尽可能接近理论性能。

除通用计算改进外，AMD还强调了CDNA 5超越单元（Transcendental Unit）的提升。该单元对softmax、神经网络激活函数和注意力机制等关键AI功能的性能有着广泛影响。AMD称，CDNA 5超越单元的吞吐量是CDNA 4的两倍，并新增了对显式tanh指令的支持，这对神经网络隐藏层的某些运算很有用。

### 缓存与内存层级革新

AMD对MI455X的缓存和内存层级进行了深度修订。CDNA 4上的大容量Infinity Cache被取代，改为每个Fabric Compute Die上更小但带宽更高的共享L2缓存。每个FCD拥有96MB L2切片，总计192MB（MI355X仅为32MB L2加上256MB Infinity Cache）。AMD表示，每个FCD的缓存带宽相比CDNA 4 Infinity Cache提升1.5倍，因此MI455X的L2总带宽是MI355X的3倍。

在WGP内部，缓存更大、更快、更灵活。本地数据存储（LDS，即scratchpad内存）大小是CDNA 4的两倍，达到320KB，整个芯片的LDS总量为96MB，是MI355X最大配置的两倍。AMD称LDS SRAM每时钟周期的读写带宽均高于上一代，但未提供具体数值。

最关键的是，MI455X的主内存架构已升级至HBM4。每个FCD使用6组HBM4堆栈，总计12组，提供每GPU 432GB容量和23.3 TB/s带宽。高容量和高带宽对于将不断增长的AI模型权重和KV缓存保留在GPU附近以实现最佳性能至关重要。

在单芯片层面，HBM实现是MI455X相对于Nvidia Rubin的最强优势之一。它提供了比Nvidia本周详述的首款Rubin GPU（仅288GB HBM4，带宽最高22 TB/s）更高的容量和略高的带宽。在机架规模上，MI455X更高的HBM容量使72块GPU的总HBM达到31.1TB，比Vera Rubin的20.7TB总容量高出50%。由于AI推理性能主要由数据局部性和内存带宽决定，AMD在这方面的优势必将吸引大量关注。

### 高效数据移动与Helios机架级架构

高效的数据移动是降低功耗、提升MI455X性能的关键。CDNA 5张量数据移动器（Tensor Data Mover, TDM）是上一代数据移动引擎的改进版，类似于Nvidia的张量内存加速器。TDM加速特定张量内存地址的生成，并促进相关数据从内存中移动，而无需涉及着色器引擎。在CDNA 5中，TDM新增了将数据直接从DRAM移动到WGP LDS缓存的能力，无需在中间缓存层级暂存数据。

AMD还为WGP的内存读取新增了多播支持，以利用AI工作负载经常需要在多个WGP上同时处理相同权重和激活值的特性。通过多播，一次内存读取可以放大到多个WGP，从而增加有效内存带宽、减少总线上的重复流量并降低功耗。Nvidia自Hopper起就拥有类似功能，但考虑到AMD本代对高效数据移动的高度关注，看到类似功能的加入并不意外。

### 总结与应用前景

总体而言，MI455X是AMD迄今为止最引人注目的Instinct产品。它带来了巨大的代际性能提升，纸面峰值FLOPS与Nvidia即将推出的Rubin GPU相当甚至更优，并提供了432GB的海量HBM4内存池，比Rubin的288GB大50%且速度稍快。结合支持72加速器规模扩展域的Helios机架级架构——这是AMD首个真正能与Nvidia NVL72机架级设计抗衡的方案——我们可以预期，随着Nvidia和AMD均在今年下半年开始向客户交付下一代产品，AI计算优势的争夺将真正白热化。

items.title: AMD and Cerebras partner on low-latency, high-throughput AI inference — EPYC processors in Helios rack-scale infrastructure paired with Cerebras' Wafer-Scale Engine (WSE) solutions
items.description: When AMD's Helios meets giant wafers from Cerebras, it is not like when Odysseus meets with the Laestrygonian Giants, they collaborate to build an ultimate data center solution.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 1 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter AMD and Cerebras Systems on Thursday announced plans to develop a platform that would combine AMD's EPYC processors in Helios rack-scale infrastructure with Cerebras' Wafer-Scale Engine (WSE) solutions. Together, the new systems promise to combine low latency of AMD's CPUs and Instinct GPUs with high throughput of Cerebras's Wafer Scale Engines (WSE) processors.AMD and Cerebras expect the new inter-rack-scale platform — based on AMD Helios rack with EPYC CPUs and Instinct MI400-series accelerators inside — to be responsible for prompt processing and large context windows, whereas Cerebras' WSE will take care of the memory-bandwidth-intensive token-generation stage.AMD and Cerebras expect their disaggregated inference platform to deliver up to 5X higher tokens per second per watt (T/s/W) by assigning different portions of an inference workload to architectures optimized for them. Therefore, AMD Helios provides rack-scale compute capacity and large volumes of complex requests, whereas the Cerebras WSE handles latency-sensitive token generation. The two compute platforms will operate within a single inference workflow, although the companies have not disclosed additional performance data or explained how the systems will be interconnected.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: The underlying idea of the AMD + Cerebras platform is essentially the same as Nvidia's CPX concept, but AMD and Cerebras assign the specialized hardware to the opposite inference stage.Nvidia's disaggregated design separates inference into context/prefill and generation/decode. The cancelled Rubin CPX GPU with GDDR7 was optimized specifically for the compute-heavy context/prefill stage, while the regular HBM-equipped Rubin GPUs handle the memory-bandwidth-bound generation stage. You may like Microsoft will deploy AMD’s Helios rack-scale AI accelerator ‘at scale’ on Azure AMD takes the wraps off its Instinct MI455X AI accelerator AMD's Helios MI455X AI platform breaks cover, initial systems use UALink-over-Ethernet interconnects By contrast, the AMD and Cerebras platform follows the same disaggregation principle, but the specialization is inverted: AMD's Helios platform with Instinct GPUs handles the prefill stage and processes prompts and large context windows, while the Cerebras WSE takes over the decode stage and handles latency-sensitive token generation.Cerebras plans to install AMD Helios systems in its own data centers and integrate them with its WSE racks. The combined offering is scheduled to become available initially through Cerebras Cloud in the second half of 2026, according to the two companies.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Anton Shilov
items.titleZh: AMD与Cerebras合作，推出低延迟、高吞吐量AI推理——Helios机架级基础设施中的EPYC处理器与Cerebras晶圆级引擎（WSE）解决方案强强联合
items.descriptionZh:
AMD和Cerebras Systems于周四宣布联合开发一种新型推理平台，该平台将AMD基于Helios机架级基础设施的EPYC处理器与Cerebras的晶圆级引擎（WSE）解决方案相结合。这一合作旨在利用AMD CPU和Instinct GPU的低延迟特性，以及Cerebras WSE处理器的高吞吐量优势，实现更高效的AI推理工作流。

### 背景与上下文
随着大语言模型（LLM）的推理需求日益增长，传统的单一架构在处理不同阶段的推理任务时面临性能瓶颈。AI推理通常分为两个主要阶段：预填充（prefill）阶段，负责处理用户输入的提示文本和大型上下文窗口，计算密集度高；以及生成（decode）阶段，负责逐 token 生成输出，内存带宽需求极高且对延迟敏感。AMD和Cerebras的联合方案正是针对这一痛点，采用解耦推理架构，将不同阶段分配给最适合的硬件。

### 核心技术原理与创新点
该平台的核心思路是“工作负载分离”：AMD Helios机架（搭载EPYC CPU和Instinct MI400系列加速器）负责预填充阶段，处理复杂的提示词和大上下文窗口；而Cerebras的WSE则处理生成阶段，专注于高吞吐、低延迟的token生成。这种分工使得每种硬件可以专攻其擅长的任务——AMD侧擅长高计算密度的并行处理，Cerebras的晶圆级芯片则凭借巨大的片上存储和带宽优势，高效处理内存带宽受限的生成环节。

值得关注的是，这一设计与Nvidia的CPX概念类似，但分工恰好相反。Nvidia原本计划将CPX GPU（配备GDDR7）专门用于计算密集的预填充阶段，而常规HBM版的Rubin GPU处理生成阶段。AMD和Cerebras则反过来：用AMD的Instinct GPU处理预填充，用Cerebras WSE处理生成。这种反向优化可能源于两家公司对各自硬件特性的深度把握——Cerebras WSE拥有超大的片上SRAM和极高的内存带宽，尤其适合逐token生成时频繁的内存访问。

### 性能参数与对比数据
根据AMD和Cerebras的预期，这种解耦推理平台相比传统方案，每瓦每秒生成的token数（T/s/W）可提升高达5倍。这一提升来源于将推理工作负载的不同部分分配给专门优化的架构，避免了单一硬件的性能瓶颈。不过，两家公司并未公布更多详细性能数据，也未说明两个计算平台之间具体如何互连（通信机制未公开）。目前已知的是，AMD Helios机架和Cerebras WSE机架将协同工作于同一推理流程中，但延迟和带宽指标有待后续披露。

### 技术影响与应用场景
该平台的首个部署规划是：Cerebras将在其自有数据中心安装AMD Helios系统，并与自家的WSE机架集成。联合解决方案计划于2026年下半年通过Cerebras Cloud首次提供给客户。这意味着该平台主要瞄准云端AI推理服务，尤其适合需要同时处理大量复杂上下文（如长文档理解、多轮对话）和实时生成（如聊天机器人、代码生成）的场景。

从行业角度看，这一合作标志着AMD和Cerebras在AI推理领域直接挑战Nvidia的统治地位。通过解耦架构，他们提供了一种可能更高效、更灵活的选择——客户可以根据自身工作负载特性，灵活调整预填充和生成阶段的硬件配比。对于追求高能效比和低延迟的AI应用（如实时交互系统、大规模推理集群），该平台具有明显吸引力。

总之，AMD+Cerebras的联合推理平台通过创新的工作负载分离设计，有望在AI推理的能效和性能上实现显著突破，但具体的实际表现和生态系统支持仍需等待2026年产品上市后的验证。

items.title: New semiconductor firm breaks cover, backed by $43 million in early-stage funding — TYLsemi aims to deliver custom silicon to customers without breaking the bank
items.description: TYLsemi is set to offer pre-validated chiplets, along with custom ASIC design services, and build highly custom multi-tile processors at relatively low costs.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter A new semiconductor firm, TYLsemi (pronounced Tile Semi), publicly revealed itself this month, alongside $43 million in early-stage funding and an ambitious plan to simplify the development of custom processors for AI infrastructure.Dozens of contract chip designers can develop custom processors of different complexity. However, only a few companies can offer custom silicon design services using standard chiplets to speed up and derisk the development cycle. TYLsemi is aiming to join their ranks. We spoke to their founders to find out how the nascent business might pull it off.Emerging from stealthRather than compete solely as another custom ASIC design house, TYLsemi intends to offer reusable, standards-based connectivity, power delivery, and eventually memory chiplets that customers can combine with their own differentiating compute silicon to build a unique system-in-package. For companies that do not intend to conduct semiconductor development themselves, TYLsemi will also provide an end-to-end service that includes design and implementation of a differentiating chiplet, packaging, qualification, and high-volume production, essentially enabling companies without any silicon development skills to offer their own multi-chiplet processors.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: (Image credit: TYLsemi)TYLsemi was co-founded by Mohit Gupta and Sunil Bhardwaj, semiconductor veterans who have led global engineering, operations, and business teams at Alphawave, SiFive, Cadence, Rambus, and other chip companies, and who collectively have plenty of experience with both standard and custom silicon. Mohit Gupta, a co-founder and chief executive of TYLsemi, believes that the time to establish a company that specializes in pre-approved chiplets and custom ASIC design is right now."Chiplets have been discussed for seven or eight years, but several things have changed in the last three or four years," Gupta told Tom's Hardware Premium. "First, advanced packaging has matured significantly. There are now multiple 2.5D and 3D integration options in volume production. Customers are not limited to one packaging technology or supplier; there are options from foundries and OSATs, including TSMC, Intel, ASE, and Amkor. Second, die-to-die standards have arrived. In the past, most chiplet implementations relied on proprietary interfaces. UCIe is now moving into production deployments, including at hyperscalers, which makes heterogeneous integration much more practical. Third, supply-chain resilience has become critical. Customers increasingly want modular and potentially multi-source strategies rather than a single point of failure. Those factors have created an environment that did not exist four or five years ago." You may like The custom AI ASIC state of play (May 2026) — Broadcom deals, Google TPUs, Meta MTIA & beyond Analyzing TSMC's fab expansion roadmap — multi-fab N2 ramp, CoWoS, SoIC, and uncorking bottlenecks Google reportedly books Intel for packaging more than 3 million TPUs in 2028 AI accelerators will be among the primary applications to benefit from multi-chiplet design, as we have already learned from AMD and Nvidia."The AI accelerator market is on track to reach $604 billion by 2033, and custom silicon XPUs built for specific hyperscaler workloads are the fastest-growing segment," Gupta said. "At that scale, chiplet-based design is no longer optional, yet there is no pure-play chiplet company serving this market with a full portfolio. TYLsemi closes that gap with standards-based chiplets combined with UCIe-based die-to-die connectivity, XPU-aware design, packaging, and integration — giving customers a fast, proven path to AI-era silicon."Chiplet economicsThe vast majority of AI and HPC accelerators today feature large die sizes, in many cases approaching the size of a reticle. However, as modern process technologies are becoming more complex, foundries tend to increase their quotes for new nodes. A leading-edge wafer used to cost around $15,000 to process around five years ago, but today that price is around $30,000. As a result, large chips at a size close to the reticle limit implemented on a leading-edge node become an option for a select few chip designers who can afford it. For newcomers, multi-chiplet designs enabled by advanced packaging and standardized interconnects such as UCIe start to make a lot more sense. (Image credit: TYLsemi)"Once dies get into the 500 – 600 mm² range, the yield curve becomes increasingly difficult. Timing closure on a reticle-sized die is also challenging," Gupta explained. "I have worked on a reticle-sized accelerator, and getting from 99% to the final 1% can require disproportionately more engineering effort."TYLsemi estimates that its chiplet approach could reduce total cost of ownership by 57% at a volume of 100,000 devices, from $350 million for a monolithic 700 mm² 3nm-class chip to $150 million for a design combining a 500 mm² 3nm-class compute die with four 100 mm² I/O chiplets built on an N-1 process. TYLsemi believes that the unit price of a monolithic chip would be $3,000, whereas the cost of an SiP would be around $600. The company attributes the saving to higher yields, reusable I/O silicon, lower IP licensing and engineering costs, and substantially lower per-unit silicon costs. However, the company stresses that the figures are illustrative estimates rather than actual manufacturing costs. Additionally, multi-chiplet designs can enable faster product refreshes compared to large monolithic dies as they are faster to develop and yield. What to read next Tower Semiconductor revives shuttered Panasonic-era fab in $3 billion Japan photonics expansion — METI-backed plan targets $3.6 billion revenue by 2028 Leading-edge foundry roadmaps for TSMC, Intel and Samsung — outlining the path to 1.4nm nodes and beyond Jim Keller's startup is building a factory to mass-produce small semiconductor fabs "Compute may move to 2nm or A14, while high-speed I/O can remain on 3nm, since I/O does not scale in the same way as logic," Gupta said. "Our power-delivery chiplets can use an even less advanced process. Customers therefore do not have to use the most expensive silicon real estate for every function. […] There is no single answer for every design. You have to determine the right disaggregation points based on the architecture, thermal requirements, package, and how multiple accelerators communicate. […] The exact partitioning will vary by application, but you still get a better total cost of ownership."TYLsemi primarily targets AI infrastructure, so it generally envisions multi-chiplet designs to be used for AI accelerators, data-center CPUs, high-performance computing, networking and telecom silicon, and heterogeneous SoCs. However, TYLsemi has also ignored the fact that multi-chiplet designs are already widely used for consumer CPUs and GPUs.Foundation chipletsAt the core of TYLsemi's proposition are its foundation chiplets, which are reusable building blocks intended to handle common non-compute functions in custom AI and infrastructure processors and are implemented using various process technologies from TSMC. The foundation chiplets include the following: (Image credit: TYLsemi)TYL.IO — a family of connectivity chiplets that includes TYL. IO PCIe, a 32-lane PCIe 7.0/CXL chiplet connected to the compute die via UCIe; TYL.IO Scale, a 224G+ SerDes for ESUN/UALink scale-up connectivity; and TYL.IO EIC for co-packaged optics.TYL.Power — a 16nm in-package IVR chiplet with embedded passives, designed to provide power closer to compute dies and use closed-loop control and die telemetry to improve power delivery.TYL.Mem — a planned family of memory-connectivity chiplets. TYLsemi has not yet disclosed the architecture or specifications, though it is safe to assume they are talking about memory controllers and PHYs.Not all of these chiplets will be available immediately, as the company has certain priorities amid limited resources."The first TYL.IO product disaggregates the PCIe functionality that would normally sit on a large server processor, it is a 32-lane PCIe Gen7/CXL chiplet connected to the host compute die using UCIe," Gupta explained. "The idea is that the CPU cores can move to 2nm, A14, or another leading-edge process, while the I/O chiplet remains on 3nm. The next product in the family will address scale-up connectivity between XPUs within a rack using high-speed SerDes. That device will be considerably larger, with around 72 lanes and approximately 14 TB/s of bandwidth. We also have an EIC roadmap for co-packaged optical connectivity. We expect samples of our first I/O product in the second half of 2027."These chiplets can be used as standalone components or integrated with a customer's compute dies designed by the customer to TYLsemi through TYL.Forge, TYLsemi's end-to-end custom silicon platform.TYL.ForgeTYL.Forge is arguably one of the key enablers of TYLsemi's business, as the program is aimed at companies that have their own compute architecture or even a compute die, but cannot build their own SiP or manage the entire semiconductor supply chain."There are larger custom silicon companies in the market, but many of them focus on a relatively small number of customers that can generate billions of dollars in annual business," Gupta explained. "We see an opportunity among emerging AI companies and system companies that need advanced custom silicon but also need a partner capable of taking responsibility for the entire implementation and supply chain." (Image credit: TYLsemi)Such clients can provide their proprietary compute RTL, while TYLsemi handles physical implementation and integrates the resulting compute die with its pre-validated connectivity, power, and eventually memory chiplets. The company then manages tape-out, packaging, assembly, testing, qualification, and high-volume production."For example, a customer building a large accelerator can bring us its matrix-multiplication engine," Gupta said. "We can implement the custom compute die and integrate it with our chiplets, so the customer does not have to reinvent the I/O and other common functions. This reduces risk and time to market."In fact, TYL.Forge appears flexible about where the customer enters the development process. TYLsemi describes the platform as covering everything from architecture and front-end design through implementation, tape-out, assembly, qualification, and production. So instead of RTL, customers can come to TYLsemi with an architecture/concept, and then the company will help develop and implement the silicon. Nonetheless, TYLsemi does not intend to invent the customer's core compute architecture itself. In addition, customers can bring in an existing compute die, which TYLsemi can combine with its chiplets, package, test, and bring to production."We are also talking with companies developing Arm- and RISC-V-based server processors," Gupta said. "They can develop the architecture, while we implement the rest of the silicon and bring the product to production. That gives customers an economic and engineering advantage because they do not need to build teams for every part of the chip."The key advantage of TYL.Forge is the reuse of pre-validated components. Instead of developing common functions such as PCIe connectivity and power delivery for every new processor, customers can use TYLsemi's pre-validated foundation chiplets and focus engineering resources on differentiated compute architectures, software, and system design. In theory, TYLsemi could integrate third-party chiplets (not from a customer, but from a third-party chiplet provider). Still, the company's focus remains on offering its own pre-validated chiplets and custom silicon with SiPs it builds."Potentially, [we could integrate third-party UCIe chiplets into a TYLsemi-based system], UCIe has done a very good job defining the electrical interface, but the ecosystem is still maturing at the protocol level," Gupta explained. "In some cases, if we provide a chiplet to a customer, we may also need to provide or enable the UCIe IP on the other side of the connection. We are committed to UCIe and industry standards because standardization ultimately wins. […] We can consider customization for a large strategic customer or hyperscaler, but we do not want those projects to derail our standard product roadmap. […] Even when customers buy our standalone chiplets, I expect many of them will ask us to handle packaging and testing because heterogeneous integration and supply-chain management are difficult "TYLsemi estimates that its approach can cut development time and cost by up to 50% compared with traditional custom silicon programs. In the best-case scenario, TYLsemi envisions that the development cycle can shrink considerably compared to today's cycles that can be two, three, or more years long. According to TYLsemi, once a customer provides sufficiently mature RTL or a netlist, the company can take a custom compute die to tape-out in around six to nine months or so, which includes fabrication, assembly, testing, and qualification."If a customer provides mature final RTL or a netlist and uses our standardized I/O chiplet, we believe we can take the custom compute die to tape-out in approximately six months in some cases," Gupta explained. "More generally, our target is six to nine months from a mature design to tape-out. The architecture and front-end phase is more customer-dependent. For a first-generation product, that can take around six months; for a more mature second- or third-generation design, it could be closer to three months, and some of that work can overlap with implementation. After tape-out, fabrication can take roughly four to five months depending on the process, followed by perhaps another two months for assembly, testing, and qualification. If the architecture is already mature, it may therefore be possible to reach production samples in about a year."Still, the company stresses that architecture development and implementation typically include feedback loops, which greatly slow the development process. This is why the company provides the relatively conservative '50%' figure.TYL.IO and TYL.Power samples will be available to qualified customers in 2027, in partnership with TSMC, and the company is looking forward to designing processors for its clients in time for them to reach the market in 2029 – 2030.Speaking of TSMC, TYLsemi will initially only offer designs and services adhered to the TSMC ecosystem, though eventually it may offer other options for packaging technologies, such as Intel's EMIB and Foveros, or Amkor's packaging methods."We are initially focused on the TSMC ecosystem, but we also intend to explore other advanced-packaging supply chains," Gupta said. "We do not want to limit ourselves to one packaging option. Over time, that could include other OSATs and packaging technologies. […] That could include Intel, ASE, Amkor, or others. Amkor, for example, is building significant packaging capacity in Arizona."
items.author: Anton Shilov
items.titleZh: New semiconductor firm breaks cover, backed by $43 million in early-stage funding — TYLsemi aims to deliver custom silicon to customers without breaking the bank
items.descriptionZh:
一家名为TYLsemi（发音为“Tile Semi”）的新兴半导体公司于本月从隐身模式中走出，公开发布了其融资计划——初期获得了4300万美元的融资——以及一个雄心勃勃的蓝图：简化AI基础设施领域定制处理器的开发流程。该公司由Mohit Gupta和Sunil Bhardwaj共同创立，两人都是半导体行业的资深人士，曾在Alphawave、SiFive、Cadence、Rambus等公司领导全球工程、运营和业务团队，在标准和定制芯片领域拥有丰富的经验。他们相信，如今正是成立一家专注于预验证小芯片（chiplet）和定制ASIC设计的专业公司的时机。

### 市场背景与小芯片经济学的重新计算

当前，AI加速器市场预计到2033年将达到6040亿美元，其中针对超大规模数据中心工作负载的定制芯片（XPU）是增长最快的细分市场。然而，传统的大面积单晶片（monolithic die）设计正面临严峻挑战。随着先进制程（如3nm）变得越来越复杂，代工厂的报价也在飙升：五年前一片顶级制程晶圆的加工成本约为15000美元，如今已接近30000美元。这使得接近光罩极限尺寸的大型芯片（如700平方毫米）在成本上变得难以承受，只有极少数能负担得起的设计者才敢尝试。

TYLsemi的联合创始人兼CEO Mohit Gupta指出，过去三四年间，几个关键因素已经发生了变化，为小芯片设计创造了前所未有的土壤：
1.  **先进封装技术已成熟**：目前已有多种2.5D和3D集成方案进入量产，客户不再局限于单一封装技术或供应商。
2.  **Die-to-Die（芯片间互联）标准已到来**：UCIe（Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express）标准正从专有接口转向量产部署，使得异构集成变得非常实用。
3.  **供应链韧性成为关键**：客户越来越倾向于采用模块化和多源策略，以避免单一失效点。

#### 对比数据：从经济学角度看单晶片与小芯片

TYLsemi估计，在10万颗器件的产量下，其小芯片方法相比传统的单晶片设计，可以将总拥有成本（TCO）降低57%。

| 对比项 | 传统单晶片方案 | TYLsemi小芯片方案（SiP） |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **设计构成** | 700平方毫米的3nm制程单晶片 | 500平方毫米的3nm制程计算晶片 + 4个100平方毫米的I/O小芯片（采用N-1制程） |
| **单位成本** | 约3000美元 | 约600美元 |
| **总开发/制造成本** | 3.5亿美元 | 1.5亿美元 |
| **成本节省来源** | - | 更高的良率、可复用的I/O小芯片、更低的IP许可和工程成本、以及显著降低的单位芯片成本。 |

Gupta解释道，当单晶片面积达到500-600平方毫米范围时，良率曲线会变得非常陡峭。在他的经验中，从99%的时序收敛做到最后的1%，需要的工程工作量不成比例地增加。采用小芯片设计，计算部分可以移动到2nm或A14节点，而高速I/O可以继续留在3nm甚至更成熟的节点上，因为I/O不随逻辑芯片同步微缩。这样客户就不必为所有功能使用最昂贵的芯片面积。

### 核心技术：可复用的基础小芯片与UCIe互联

TYLsemi的核心竞争力在于提供一系列被称为“基础小芯片（Foundation Chiplets）”的标准化、可复用的构建模块。它们负责处理AI处理器中常见的非计算功能，采用台积电（TSMC）的成熟制程制造。目前，公司已规划了三类基础小芯片系列：

1.  **TYL.IO（互联系列）**：
    *   **TYL.IO PCIe**：一个32通道的PCIe 7.0 / CXL小芯片，通过UCIe与计算晶片相连。目标是将大型服务器处理器中的PCIe功能解耦出来，让CPU核心可以自由选择先进制程，而I/O部分保持在一个更经济的节点上。
    *   **TYL.IO Scale**：用于机架内扩展互联（如ESUN/UALink标准），提供224G+的SerDes，预计将有约72个通道和约14 TB/s的带宽。
    *   **TYL.IO EIC**：面向共封装光学（co-packaged optics）的电气接口，为未来的光互联做准备。
2.  **TYL.Power（电源管理系列）**：一个基于16nm制程的封装内集成电压调节器（IVR）小芯片。它通过嵌入式无源元件，将电源更靠近计算晶片，利用闭环控制和晶片遥测技术来改善电力输送效率。
3.  **TYL.Mem（内存互联系列）**：规划中的内存控制器和PHY小芯片系列，具体架构和规格尚未披露。

所有这些小芯片都基于UCIe标准进行Die-to-Die连接。TYL.IO系列的首款产品（PCIe Gen7/CXL）的样品预计在2027年下半年提供。

### TYL.Forge：端到端的定制芯片平台

TYL.Forge是TYLsemi平台化战略的核心。它不仅提供标准小芯片，还提供了一条从概念到量产的完整端到端定制芯片服务。该平台旨在服务那些拥有自己的计算架构或计算晶片，但缺乏构建多芯片系统级封装（SiP）和全供应链管理能力的企业。

**工作流程**：
*   客户可以带着自己的**计算RTL代码**、**架构概念**甚至**一个现有的计算晶片**来找TYLsemi。
*   TYLsemi负责其余所有环节：物理实现计算晶片，将其与自身的预验证基础小芯片集成，然后管理流片、封装、组装、测试、认证和量产。
*   **关键优势**：客户可以专注于其差异化的计算架构、软件和系统设计，而将复杂的I/O、电源、封装和供应链问题外包给TYLsemi。

**性能与时间优势**：
*   **开发周期**：TYLsemi估计，与传统定制芯片项目相比，该方法可将开发时间和成本降低高达50%。
*   **具体时间线**：在客户提供成熟且最终的RTL或网表（Netlist）的情况下，TYLsemi预计最快可以在**6到9个月**内完成定制的计算晶片的流片（包括制造、组装、测试和认证）。如果架构已经成熟，从项目启动到获得生产样品，整个过程可能在**大约一年**内完成。
*   **目标市场时间**：TYL.IO和TYL.Power样品在2027年提供给合格客户，而基于其平台设计的客户处理器预计在**2029-2030年**间上市。

### 技术影响与应用场景

TYLsemi的方法将深刻影响AI基础芯片的设计范式：
*   **降低入行门槛**：它使得那些没有深厚芯片设计团队的新兴AI公司或系统公司，也能开发自己的定制AI处理器（XPU），从而加速了AI硬件的创新。
*   **加速产品迭代**：由于小芯片设计比大型单晶片更灵活、更快开发，企业可以更频繁地更新其计算晶片，而无需重新设计整个系统。
*   **增强供应链弹性**：模块化设计允许客户采用多源策略，减少对单一代工厂或封装厂的依赖。

**主要应用场景**包括：
*   **AI加速器**：针对特定超大规模数据中心工作负载的定制化算力芯片。
*   **数据中心CPU**：特别是基于Arm和RISC-V架构的服务器处理器。
*   **高性能计算（HPC）**：需要极高并行计算能力的系统。
*   **网络与电信芯片**：需要大量高速I/O和复杂互联的芯片。
*   **异构SoC**：集成不同功能模块的复杂系统级芯片。

在生态构建上，TYLsemi目前专注于台积电生态系统，但未来也计划探索其他先进封装供应链，如英特尔的EMIB/Foveros和安靠（Amkor）的封装方法。对于是否集成第三方小芯片，TYLsemi持开放态度，但强调UCIe生态仍在成熟中，为了确保兼容性，很多时候需要TYLsemi提供或启用连接两侧的UCIe IP。对于大型战略性客户，公司也考虑提供定制化服务，但前提是这不会打乱其标准产品的路线图。

总之，TYLsemi的诞生，标志着小芯片技术正从理论讨论走向能够大幅降低AI时代定制芯片门槛的工业化实践。通过提供预验证的基础小芯片和一体化的设计服务平台，它正试图填补市场中“纯小芯片公司”的空白，为那些渴望拥抱AI算力红海但受制于高昂芯片开发成本的创新者开辟一条捷径。

items.title: AMD’s 256-core Epyc 9996 ‘Venice’ claims up to a 3.4x jump over Intel Xeon competition, 20% over Nvidia Vera – Zen 6 comes with up to 1024MB of L3, 16-channel memory, and 5GHz+ clock speeds
items.description: After over a year of teases, AMD has finally provided details on its 256-core Venice CPU with the Zen 6 architecture, now known as the Epyc 9996.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter AMD is finally providing some details on its first Zen 6 CPU, which it has been teasing for over a year. The Epyc 9996 is a 256-core / 512-thread chip, packing AMD’s new Zen 6 architecture, and it’s the first to launch in what AMD describes as a “broad portfolio” for Venice. In addition to claiming significant performance advantages over the impending Nvidia Vera and Intel’s Xeon 6, AMD says it will continue to build out the Venice range with bespoke designs over the next year.“It’s not just a single processor,” said AMD’s Ravi Kuppuswany, corporate VP of compute and enterprise solutions.q “It’s a portfolio.” AMD says it has purpose-built solutions, splitting its offerings depending on the application, not dissimilar to how Intel has split its Xeon ranges over the past few generations (nor how AMD has softly segmented its Epyc offerings). The roadmap starts with the main Venice lineup on the SP7 socket, which is what AMD has been teasing for so long. It scales up to 256 cores and 512 threads, 1.6 TB/s of memory bandwidth with fast MRDIMMs, and 128 PCIe 6 lanes in 1P configuration (160 lanes in 2P). Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original Note: When scaling up to 256 cores, AMD uses its Zen 6c “dense” design. With a standard Zen 6 design, AMD says Venice scales up to 128 cores and 256 threads, while high-frequency variations top out at 96 cores.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: In the first half of next year, AMD plans to launch Venice on its SP8 socket, offering as few as eight cores and up to 128, focused on smaller deployments. These chips support eight-channel memory with two DIMMs per channel, and the same 128 PCIe 6 lanes.Venice-X is expected in the second half of 2027, on the SP7 socket. We didn’t see Turin-X, but the last, last-gen Genoa-X came with 96 cores and up to 1152 MB of stacked L3 cache. Those specs haven’t changed (short of the Zen 6 microarchitecture), but AMD says it's able to clock Venice-X up to 5.15 GHz. You may like AMD fires back at Nvidia, claiming 256-core Zen 6 'Venice' CPU beats Vera by 3.3x in rack-level performance AMD begins production ramp of 256-core EPYC Venice AMD’s Venice-X CPU launches in 2027 with 1152 MB of 3D V-Cache, 96 cores, and 5.15 GHz boost clock Finally, Verano should arrive in the second half of next year on the SP8 socket, and it looks like the most direct competitor to Vera (AMD’s Kuppuswamy had some fun with calling it “Vera-No”). It’s optimized to be an AI host node, says AMD, packing up to 72 cores and 5 GHz peak clocks. Critically, it comes with a 24-channel LPDDR5X memory system, leveraging SOCAMM2 modules. (Image credit: AMD)One of the advantages Nvidia claims with its Vera chip is lots of memory bandwidth through the LPDDR5X system. AMD’s approach is different with Venice SP7. It’s scaling up to 16-channel memory with Venice SP7, with support for MRDIMMs running at 12,800 MT/s, or standard DDR5 RDIMMs running at 8000 MT/s.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsIt’s a significant jump over Turin, which uses 12-channel memory, with support for RDIMMs running at 6400 MT/s. AMD claims per-socket bandwidth of 1.6 TB/s, significantly higher than the 1.2 TB/s available on Vera, and nearly triple the 576 GB/s per-socket bandwidth of Turin. Intel recently enabled 8000 MT/s RDIMMs on select Granite Rapids and Clearwater Forest SKUs, and it says support for MRDIMMs with speeds up to 8800 MT/s is coming in Q1 2027.AMD didn’t provide a list of SKUs or detailed specs for Venice. We really only know about the Epyc 9996, the dense 256-core design, and even then there are a lot of holes. We learned a few critical details about Venice more broadly, however, confirming previous rumors and reports.Zen 6 is built on TSMC’s N2 (this has been previously confirmed). AMD confirmed that there are 32 cores on a CCD, along with two IODs. Keep in mind that the 32-core CCD is using Zen 6c, not full Zen 6. There has been plenty of speculation about 32-core CCDs in consumer Zen 6 CPUs, but that seems unlikely. What to read next Intel Xeon 6+ ‘Clearwater Forest’ puts 18A in the data center with up to 288 cores, 576 MB of L3 cache Nvidia offers restricted access to Vera CPU in first round of Linux benchmarks Nvidia deep dives Vera CPU for AI data centers The 256-core configuration comes with a massive 1,024 MB of L3, nearly triple the amount of the Epyc 9965. This isn’t stacked cache, either; that will come with Venice-X. Each CCD has access to 128 MB or L3, or 4 MB per core, double what was available on Turin.Although AMD has focused a lot of its teases on the 256-core Venice, the initial SP7 offerings will also hold a 96-core, high-frequency model that can clock up to 5 GHz.AMD shares first 256-core Epyc ‘Venice’ benchmarks Image 1 of 6 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 6 View Original Image 2 of 6 View Original Image 3 of 6 View Original Image 4 of 6 View Original Image 5 of 6 View Original Image 6 of 6 View Original Unlike the extrapolated performance AMD shared a few weeks back, we have some concrete benchmarks for the Epyc 9996 now. AMD has, unsurprisingly, focused the workloads around agentic AI. However, many of the workloads applicable for agentic AI are applicable elsewhere, as well, including high-concurrency networking tasks, code compilation, and media processing.Note that AMD includes just the Epyc 9965 as a gen-on-gen comparison point in the charts above. This is a “dense” Zen 5 design with 192 cores. Results for the 128-core 9755 are included in the tables below.Starting with front-end operations, AMD claims a 1.2x gen-on-gen improvement and a 2.8x improvement compared to Intel Xeon 6980P, with an NGINX web server using the WRK load generator. Unlike most of these competitive performance figures, AMD included the actual numbers for the benchmarks it ran in the footnotes, which you can see in the table below. Swipe to scroll horizontally Chip Max Request Per Second Intel Xeon 6980P 10,162,179 AWS Graviton5 15,331,108 AMD Epyc 9755 17,906,196 AMD Epyc 9965 24,320,476 AMD Epyc 9996 28,789,170 In data-heavy workloads that are common among AI agents, AMD claims a 1.7x improvement over Turn, and a massive 3.4x over the Xeon 6980P. AMD used the TPCx-AI benchmark to gather these results. The primary metric for this test is AI use cases per minute (AIUCpm), for which AMD shared the median result2. If you’re interested in more about the reporting of this benchmark, Dell has published an extensive breakdown. Swipe to scroll horizontally Chip AIUCpm Intel Xeon 6980P 1,750.36 AWS Graviton5 2,444.8 AMD Epyc 9755 2,704.19 AMD Epyc 9965 3,458.79 AMD Epyc 9996 5,982.91 In vectorized workloads, AMD claims a 1.6x gen-on-gen improvement and 2.3x improvement compared to the Xeon 6980P. For this test, AMD used Meta’s open-source FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) library to search for similar vectors in the siftm1 dataset. The metric here is QPS, or queries processed per second, looking at overall query throughput. Swipe to scroll horizontally Chip QPS Intel Xeon 6980P 316,069 AWS Graviton5 119,179 AMD Epyc 9755 369,252 AMD Epyc 9965 472,079 AMD Epyc 9996 751,453 For its “enterprise tools” benchmarks, AMD ran several tests, including TPC-H, TPC-C, and Redis, and it reports the results as “geomean throughput.” We have actual numbers here, but they’re a geomean representing several different tests rather than a single benchmark. Broadly, however, AMD claims a 1.6x gen-on-gen improvement in these workloads, and a 2.6x improvement compared to Intel. Swipe to scroll horizontally Chip Geomean throughput Intel Xeon 6980P 2,284,701 AWS Graviton5 2,982,203 AMD Epyc 9755 2,546,290 AMD Epyc 9965 3,867,149 AMD Epyc 9996 6,054,748 A lot of agentic workloads are applicable outside of agents, but AMD also tested a few agents directly. It replayed five different agent personas across the chips and, once again, gathered a throughput geomean. We don’t have the metrics here, nor for the previous benchmark, so it’s possible there’s an angle of performance that we’re not seeing with the data provided by AMD.Regardless, the company claims a 1.5x gen-on-gen improvement in this test, and a 2.5x improvement compared to the 6980P. Swipe to scroll horizontally Chip Geomean throughput Intel Xeon 6980P 1.779 AWS Graviton5 2.505 AMD Epyc 9755 2.317 AMD Epyc 9965 2.97 AMD Epyc 9996 4.451 AMD ran these tests earlier in the month. But just a few days ago, Nvidia published its first SPEC CPU 2026 results for Vera. AMD ran some tests of its own using the same compiler for a comparison between Vera and Venice. AMD’s Kuppuswamy says, “everything is apples-to-apples comparison, same compiler.” That’s GNU 15.2, if you’re curious. (Image credit: AMD)In throughput, AMD claims a 2.2x improvement in the SPECrate integer suite, compared to Vera using the dense Venice design with 256 Zen 6c cores. More importantly, AMD claims a 1.2x improvement in per-core performance when comparing Vera to a 96-core “High Frequency” Venice chip. AMD says it used Nvidia’s results as the basis for comparison. With both Venice designs, AMD used a 600W TDP. (Image credit: AMD)In SPEC CPU 2017 (again using SPECrate with integer workloads), AMD has data comparing Venice to Intel’s 6980P and Arm’s new AGI, showing 2x throughput compared to Intel, and 1.3x per-core performance. Note the core counts here for AMD. SPECrate is a throughput test, and AMD stepping down to a 128-core model suggests that performance will likely drop off as the core count increases. Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: AMD) (Image credit: AMD) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original Although AMD wants to focus Venice performance on agentic workloads, it shared a range of what are now being called “legacy” workloads across the cloud and HPC. Some of the results are repeated from the earlier slides, such as Redis and NGINX, but there are some additional data points, including NAMD and SQL. The performance improvements here are large, though not surprising. You can see that across these tests, even Turin beats the competition from Intel and AWS. Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Jake Roach
items.titleZh: AMD 256核Epyc 9996 'Venice'声称比英特尔Xeon竞品快3.4倍，比Nvidia Vera快20%——Zen 6配备高达1024MB L3缓存、16通道内存和5GHz+时钟频率
items.descriptionZh:
AMD 终于公布了其首款 Zen 6 架构 CPU 的详细信息——这款名为 Epyc 9996 的处理器拥有 256 个核心和 512 线程，采用 AMD 全新的 Zen 6 架构，是威尼斯（Venice）系列的首款产品。AMD 表示，这并非单一处理器，而是一个“广泛的产品组合”，旨在应对不同应用场景，类似于英特尔将至强系列按代拆分、以及 AMD 自身对 Epyc 的细分策略。该组合从基于 SP7 插槽的主流威尼斯系列开始，最高可扩展至 256 核/512 线程（使用 Zen 6c 密集设计）、1.6 TB/s 内存带宽（通过高速 MRDIMM 实现）、128 条 PCIe 6.0 通道（单路配置）。标准 Zen 6 设计下，威尼斯可提供 128 核/256 线程，高频版本则止步于 96 核。

**核心技术原理与创新点**  
Zen 6 架构基于台积电 N2 工艺制造（此前已证实）。每个 CCD（核心计算芯片）集成 32 个 Zen 6c 核心（而非完整版 Zen 6），并搭配两个 IOD（输入输出芯片）。256 核配置拥有高达 1024 MB 的 L3 缓存（每个 CCD 128 MB，每核心 4 MB），是上代 Epyc 9965（192 核、384 MB L3）的近三倍，且这部分并非堆叠缓存，堆叠缓存将留给 Venice-X 版本。内存方面，SP7 平台支持 16 通道 DDR5 RDIMM（最高 8000 MT/s）或 MRDIMM（最高 12800 MT/s），单插槽带宽达 1.6 TB/s，显著高于 Nvidia Vera 的 1.2 TB/s 和上代 Turin 的 576 GB/s。PCIe 升级至 6.0 标准，提供 128 条通道（双路 160 条）。Venice-X 预计 2027 年下半年推出，沿用 SP7 插槽，延续 96 核、1152 MB 3D V-Cache 配置，但最高频率可达 5.15 GHz。另外，针对 AI 宿主节点优化的 Verano 系列（2025 年下半年，SP8 插槽）将采用 24 通道 LPDDR5X 内存（基于 SOCAMM2 模块），最高 72 核、5 GHz 加速频率，直接对标 Nvidia Vera 的高带宽内存方案。

**性能参数与对比数据**  
AMD 公布了 Epyc 9996 在多个基准测试中的具体数据，并与上代 Turin（Epyc 9965）、英特尔 Xeon 6980P、AWS Graviton5 以及 Nvidia Vera 进行了对比：  
- **NGINX 前端（WRK 负载）**：Epyc 9996 达到 28,789,170 请求/秒，是 Xeon 6980P 的 2.8 倍，Turin 的 1.2 倍。  
- **TPCx-AI（AI 用例/分钟）**：9996 得分 5,982.91 AIUCpm，是 Turin 的 1.7 倍，Xeon 的 3.4 倍。  
- **FAISS 向量搜索（QPS）**：9996 处理 751,453 QPS，是 Turin 的 1.6 倍，Xeon 的 2.3 倍。  
- **企业工具（TPC-H/TPC-C/Redis 几何平均吞吐量）**：9996 达 6,054,748，是 Turin 的 1.6 倍，Xeon 的 2.6 倍。  
- **AI 代理工作负载（五个代理人设回放）**：9996 几何平均吞吐量 4.451，是 Turin 的 1.5 倍，Xeon 的 2.5 倍。  
- **SPECrate 整数吞吐量（对比 Vera）**：256 核密集版 Venice 比 Vera 快 2.2 倍；96 核高频版单核性能比 Vera 快 1.2 倍（均使用相同的 GNU 15.2 编译器，600W TDP 条件）。  
- **SPEC CPU 2017 整数吞吐量（对比 Intel 和 Arm）**：128 核 Venice 比 Xeon 6980P 快 2 倍，单核性能快 1.3 倍。

**技术影响与应用场景**  
AMD 强调威尼斯系列针对“代理式 AI”（Agentic AI）进行了优化，但实际覆盖范围远超此范畴，包括高并发网络服务（如 NGINX）、代码编译、媒体处理、数据库（Redis、SQL）以及传统 HPC 工作负载。通过提供从 8 核到 256 核的完整产品线（SP7 主流、SP8 低核配置、Venice-X 大缓存、Verano 高带宽 AI 宿主），AMD 试图在数据中心端与 Nvidia Vera 和英特尔 Xeon 6 形成全面竞争。尤其值得注意的是，256 核威尼斯在 AI 推理和数据密集型任务中展现出显著吞吐优势，而内存带宽的翻倍（1.6 TB/s vs Vera 1.2 TB/s）和 PCIe 6.0 的引入将对 AI 集群的互联效率产生直接影响。不过，AMD 尚未公布具体 SKU 列表和完整规格，后续随着威尼斯基于 SP8 平台的低核版本（2025 年上半年）和 Verano 的推出，该系列将进一步完善其数据中心 CPU 生态。

items.title: Geekbench 7 introduces biggest overhaul yet — real-world CPU testing, new media workloads, AI benchmarks, and CUDA support
items.description: The latest update introduces more realistic CPU and GPU workloads, redesigned multi-core testing, AI-focused benchmarks, larger datasets, and CUDA support for Nvidia GPUs.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Primate Labs has released Geekbench 7, the latest version of its popular cross-platform benchmark. The update introduces new and improved workloads designed to better represent and measure the performance of the latest CPUs and GPUs across major platforms, including Android, iOS, Windows, macOS, and Linux. Like previous versions, Geekbench 7 is available free of charge for personal use. Enthusiasts and IT professionals can also opt for Geekbench 7 Pro, which is available at a 20% discount until August 6.Go deeper with TH Premium: CPU (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)CPU scaling with DLSSRyzen to the top: How AMD innovated in the gaming CPU marketHow ARM is working its way into PCsAMD CES 2026 gaming trends press Q&A roundtable transcriptOne of the biggest changes introduced with Geekbench 7 is how the multi-core benchmark behaves. It will now mirror real-world application behavior instead of forcing every workload to use all available CPU cores. For instance, the HTML5 browser test has been excluded from the multi-core suite since web browsers are generally single- or lightly threaded. Geekbench 7 will only run a benchmark in multi-threaded mode if the real application it's modeling also uses multiple threads. As a result, Primate Labs says the new benchmark will deliver a more realistic indication of overall system performance.Geekbench 7 will also feature a new set of media workloads designed to better evaluate how modern CPUs handle everyday multimedia tasks. These include encoding AV1 video for screen sharing during video calls, compressing audio using the Opus codec for applications such as voice recorders and podcasts, and decoding audio and video while simultaneously generating live captions using OpenAI's Whisper speech recognition model. Essentially, these workloads are claimed to better represent tasks such as video conferencing, content streaming, and media consumption.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: A new Game Physics benchmark is also being added, which is based on the Jolt Physics engine used in modern games, to offer a more accurate measurement of CPU performance in physics-heavy gaming scenarios. The update further enhances its Photo Editor workload with more complex editing operations and expands the Photo Library test to include support for newer image formats such as JPEG XL and DNG.The GPU benchmark has also been revamped to go beyond gaming, with greater emphasis on machine learning and content creation workloads. The new tests will measure tasks such as real-time face tracking and filter effects for social media apps, image upscaling using AI, and background blurring during video conferencing. Additionally, the update adds new GPU workloads for RAW image processing, LUT-based video color grading, path tracing, and fluid simulation. You may like CPU Benchmarks and Hierarchy 2026: CPU Rankings New server-focused SPEC CPU 2026 benchmarking suite has results for a Raspberry Pi 5 AMD Ryzen AI 5 435G APU goes head-to-head with the Ryzen 5 8600G in early benchmarks Geekbench 7 is also set to introduce support for Nvidia's CUDA API in addition to OpenCL, Vulkan, and Metal, allowing users to benchmark Nvidia GPUs using the same API that is used in a variety of professional and AI applications.Lastly, in order to keep up with demanding modern workloads, the benchmark datasets have been updated to better reflect the larger and more complex files that users work with today. For instance, the File Compression test now includes a broader mix of archives containing source code, object code, and text documents, while the PDF Viewer workload processes a wider range of files, from park maps to technical documentation and academic papers. The update also introduces more assets and additional image formats across its developer and image processing workloads.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Kunal Khullar
items.titleZh: Geekbench 7 迎来迄今为止最大规模革新——真实场景CPU测试、全新媒体负载、AI基准测试及CUDA支持
items.descriptionZh:
Primate Labs 正式发布了 Geekbench 7，这是其广受欢迎的跨平台基准测试工具的最新版本。此次更新引入了全新和优化的工作负载，旨在更准确地衡量和反映当前主流 CPU 与 GPU 在 Android、iOS、Windows、macOS 及 Linux 等平台上的真实性能。Geekbench 7 个人版继续免费提供，而专业版（Geekbench 7 Pro）在 8 月 6 日前享有 20% 折扣。

**核心变化：多核测试行为向真实应用靠拢**  
Geekbench 7 最显著的变化在于多核基准测试的逻辑调整。过去，所有测试项目都会强制调用所有可用 CPU 核心；新版本则模仿真实应用的行为——只有在实际软件中会使用多线程的任务，才会以多线程模式运行。例如，HTML5 浏览器测试被移出了多核测试套件，因为网页浏览器通常使用单线程或轻量级线程。这一改动使得测试结果更能反映日常使用中的系统整体性能，而非峰值多核吞吐量。

**新增媒体与游戏物理工作负载**  
Geekbench 7 引入了一套全新的媒体工作负载，专门衡量现代 CPU 在处理日常多媒体任务时的能力。具体包括：AV1 视频编码（模拟视频通话中的屏幕共享）、Opus 音频压缩（用于语音记录和播客）、以及通过 OpenAI 的 Whisper 语音识别模型在解码音视频的同时实时生成字幕。这些场景紧密对应视频会议、内容流媒体和媒体消费等常见使用习惯。  

此外，新增的游戏物理测试基于现代游戏常用的 Jolt Physics 引擎，能更精确地评估 CPU 在物理密集型游戏场景中的性能。同时，照片编辑器工作负载升级了更复杂的编辑操作，照片库测试也扩展了对 JPEG XL、DNG 等新图像格式的支持。

**GPU 基准测试重心转向机器学习与内容创作**  
GPU 测试套件同样进行了大幅革新，超越了传统的游戏测试范畴，重点覆盖机器学习和内容创作。新测试包括：实时面部追踪和社交媒体滤镜效果、利用 AI 进行图像放大、视频会议中的背景模糊等。此外还新增了 RAW 图像处理、基于 LUT 的视频调色、路径追踪（path tracing）和流体模拟（fluid simulation）等工作负载。值得一提的是，Geekbench 7 在原有 OpenCL、Vulkan、Metal 基础上，新增了对 NVIDIA CUDA API 的支持，允许用户使用专业及 AI 应用中常见的同一 API 来对 NVIDIA GPU 进行基准测试。

**数据集全面升级，反映现代工作负荷**  
为跟上日益复杂的任务需求，基准测试所用的数据集也进行了更新。例如，文件压缩测试现在包含更广泛的档案混合类型（源代码、目标代码、文本文档等）；PDF 查看器工作负载处理的文件范围也扩大到公园地图、技术文档和学术论文。开发者和图像处理工作负载则引入了更多素材和新的图像格式。

**影响与意义**  
Geekbench 7 并非单纯追求跑分数字的提升，而是通过更贴近现实场景的工作负载设计，帮助用户和专业人士更精准地评估 CPU 与 GPU 在日常使用、内容创作、人工智能及专业应用中的综合表现。对于普通用户而言，测试结果将更真实地反映手机或电脑在视频通话、网页浏览、媒体编辑等常见任务中的响应速度；对于 IT 专业人员和评测机构，CUDA 支持、多核行为优化以及新增的物理、AI 工作负载，则提供了更有深度的横向对比依据。整体来看，Geekbench 7 是一次从“测峰值”到“测现实”的重要迭代。

items.title: OpenAI is making big claims as it rolls out ChatGPT Health to everyone
items.description:
OpenAI is rolling out ChatGPT Health to everyone in the US on Thursday, allowing more people to connect their medical records and health-tracking information to the chatbot. During a briefing, Ashley Alexander, OpenAI's vice president of health product, says the company's models "are now capable of reasoning at levels that are better than clinician level."
When asked for more information about how the performance of OpenAI's models stacks up against human clinicians, OpenAI health lead Karan Singhal says that he would "temper" the claim that they are reasoning at better levels, but that "there have been individual studies that have been poi …
Read the full story at The Verge.
items.content: OpenAI is rolling out ChatGPT Health to everyone in the US on Thursday, allowing more people to connect their medical records and health-tracking information to the chatbot. During a briefing, Ashley Alexander, OpenAI’s vice president of health product, says the company’s models “are now capable of reasoning at levels that are better than clinician level.”When asked for more information about how the performance of OpenAI’s models stacks up against human clinicians, OpenAI health lead Karan Singhal says that he would “temper” the claim that they are reasoning at better levels, but that “there have been individual studies that have been pointing in that direction,” like this one from Harvard and Stanford. On Wednesday, a Florida pastor sued OpenAI over claims ChatGPT provided him with “extremely dangerous medical recommendations,” allegedly leading him to put off medical care for a pulmonary embolism.Image: OpenAIThe broader release of ChatGPT Health comes just weeks after OpenAI launched its new GPT-5.6 Sol, which it claims is its “strongest model yet for health.” ChatGPT Health will run on the company’s latest models.With this update, OpenAI will now allow users to ask questions about their connected medical information in the main chat experience, rather than having them navigate to the dedicated ChatGPT Health tab. ChatGPT will ask for permission before using your health information to personalize its response, adding that “all conversations are encrypted at rest and at transit,” while information connected in Health “receives additional encryption protection.”Image: OpenAIRelatedGiving your healthcare info to a chatbot is, unsurprisingly, a terrible idea‘Clinical-grade AI’: a new buzzy AI word that means absolutely nothingUsers can also review and change the kinds of health data they connect to ChatGPT, including post-visit notes, notes from their care team, lab results, and medications. Currently, users can connect their data from health providers, along with services like Apple Health, Weight Watchers, MyFitnessPal, and Function. OpenAI notes that the ChatGPT Health “supports, not replaces, professional care.” It’s rolling out to all logged-in ChatGPT users aged 18 and older on the web and iOS across free, Go, Plus, and Pro plans.
items.author: Emma Roth
items.titleZh: OpenAI is making big claims as it rolls out ChatGPT Health to everyone
items.descriptionZh:
OpenAI于本周四在美国全面推出ChatGPT Health功能，允许更多用户将自己的医疗记录和健康追踪信息接入聊天机器人。这一举措标志着AI在医疗健康领域的应用进入新阶段。在新闻发布会上，OpenAI健康产品副总裁Ashley Alexander表示，该公司的模型“现在具备的推理能力已经超过了临床医生的水平”。然而，当被问及模型性能与人类临床医生的具体对比数据时，OpenAI健康业务负责人Karan Singhal对此说法进行了“降温”，称“有哈佛和斯坦福等机构进行的个别研究指向了这一方向”，但并非普遍结论。就在此前一天，一名佛罗里达牧师起诉OpenAI，声称ChatGPT向他提供了“极其危险的医疗建议”，导致他延误了对肺栓塞的治疗。

此次ChatGPT Health的全面推出发生在OpenAI发布其最新模型GPT-5.6 Sol仅几周后。OpenAI宣称GPT-5.6 Sol是“迄今为止最强的健康模型”，ChatGPT Health将运行在该公司的最新模型之上。与之前需要用户导航到专门的健康标签页不同，新功能现在允许用户直接在主流聊天体验中询问关于已连接医疗信息的问题。在个性化回复之前，ChatGPT会征求用户许可，并使用户的健康信息被加密存储和传输（“所有对话在静态和传输过程中均加密”），同时通过Health连接的信息还会获得额外的加密保护。

用户现在可以查看和更改连接到ChatGPT的健康数据类型，包括就诊后笔记、护理团队记录、实验室结果和用药信息等。目前支持的数据来源包括医疗机构、Apple Health、Weight Watchers、MyFitnessPal和Function等健康服务。OpenAI强调ChatGPT Health是“支持而非替代专业护理”。该功能面向所有年满18岁、已登录ChatGPT的Web和iOS用户，覆盖免费版、Go版、Plus版和Pro版。

从技术原理看，OpenAI的模型通过大规模预训练和领域微调，学习了海量医学文献、临床指南和真实诊疗数据，从而具备理解复杂医疗术语、分析化验结果、识别药物相互作用等能力。其核心创新在于将通用大语言模型的推理能力与结构化医疗数据（如电子病历、可穿戴设备数据）进行对齐，并通过隐私计算框架实现数据安全。但正如Karan Singhal所承认的，目前尚不能断言模型在所有临床场景下都优于人类医生——个别研究显示其在某些诊断任务（如影像解读、罕见病识别）上表现突出，但整体可靠性仍需大量验证。

此次发布的影响深远：一方面，它降低了普通用户获取健康信息和建议的门槛，尤其适合慢性病管理、药物提醒、症状初筛等场景；另一方面，AI医疗建议的准确性和法律风险引发广泛担忧，尤其是诉讼案例表明，用户可能因盲目依赖AI而延误正规治疗。OpenAI的加密措施和“不支持替代专业护理”的声明，意在平衡创新与责任。未来，随着更多健康数据源接入，ChatGPT Health有望成为个人健康管理的数字助手，但医疗领域的合规性、伦理审查和用户教育仍是其大规模应用的关键障碍。

items.title: AI memory shortage is now increasing the price of cars — GM warns of vast cost increases, BYD hikes driver assistance prices 20%
items.description: GM CFO Paul Jacobson says that the company's costs are expected to increase by $1.5 to $2 billion, primarily due to increasing memory chip costs. The move comes as the RAM shortage is affecting the automotive industry, right as cars are requiring more memory and storage due to infotainment and ADAS features.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 3 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter While PC builders, enthusiasts, and gamers are some of the most heavily affected by the AI-driven memory chip shortage, experts have also been warning that this will eventually hit the automotive sector. It seems that their fears have come true, as Nikkei Asia reports that two major automakers are increasing their prices precisely for this reason. General Motors said that it expects new vehicle prices to increase by 0.3% this year, changing its previous projection of vehicle costs to remain the same or even slightly more affordable. On the other side of the world, Chinese automotive giant BYD has also raised the prices of the driver assistance features by 20%, which it sells separately from its models. South Korea’s Hyundai also called on domestic chip manufacturers to strengthen the local supply chain.GM Chief Financial Officer Paul Jacobson said during the carmaker’s earnings call that GM expects its costs to increase by $1.5 to $2 billion, which is mostly driven by increasing prices of vehicle components, especially DRAM. While most people wouldn’t equate cars with memory chips, the increasing number of features and technologies that buyers expect from new models means that engineers and designers must add more memory compared to models from the past.Micron said in 2023 that the average vehicle used 90GB of DRAM and NAND, and it’s expecting this combined requirement to jump to 278GB by 2026, with high-end models requiring up to 2TB. A British OEM broke down the memory requirements of vehicles released this year — infotainment systems require between 4GB and 16GB, ADAS and safety systems need 8GB to 32GB, while centralized systems often have a minimum requirement of 16GB and could go as high as 64GB or more. And as AI assistants start becoming available on cars, these systems will require at least 256GB of memory. In fact, Micron predicts that cars with level 4 autonomy will need at least 300GB of RAM, as they’re essentially supercomputers on wheels.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Note that automotive memory isn’t just any high-end chip that you can get from wafer production lines. Instead, these are specialized parts that take months or years to be validated for vehicle use. That’s because they must withstand various environmental factors that cars go through, from weather and constant road vibrations to extreme heat and cold.All this means that the current memory chip shortage will have an outsize effect on the automotive industry and push prices even higher. This is bad news for the average car buyer, who is already facing record-high prices for new vehicles. And if the chip shortages continue, we might experience delays in vehicle deliveries and a lack of availability worse than what we experienced in 2021 during the height of port closures due to the pandemic. You may like SK hynix, Samsung, Micron among semiconductor industry group lobbying against government intervention on domestic memory chip supply 2003-era DDR2 memory prices jump up to 60% Samsung and SK hynix warn AI-driven memory shortages could last until 2027 and beyond, as HBM demand explodes Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors
items.author: Jowi Morales
items.titleZh: AI内存短缺正推高汽车价格——通用汽车警告成本大幅上升，比亚迪驾驶辅助提价20%
items.descriptionZh:
AI驱动的内存芯片短缺正在从PC和游戏领域蔓延至汽车行业，两大汽车巨头通用汽车和比亚迪已因此宣布涨价。通用汽车首席财务官保罗·雅各布森在财报电话会议上表示，预计今年新车价格将上涨0.3%，改变了此前价格持平或略微下降的预测，成本增加15亿至20亿美元，主要源于车辆组件特别是DRAM内存涨价。与此同时，中国汽车巨头比亚迪将其单独销售的驾驶辅助功能价格提高了20%。韩国现代也呼吁本土芯片制造商加强供应链建设。

汽车对内存的需求正急剧增长。美光在2023年指出，平均每辆汽车使用90GB的DRAM和NAND闪存，到2026年这一组合需求预计将跃升至278GB，高端车型需求高达2TB。英国一家原始设备制造商分解了今年车型的内存需求：信息娱乐系统需要4GB至16GB，高级驾驶辅助系统和安全系统需要8GB至32GB，中央计算系统最低16GB，最高64GB或更多。随着AI助手开始进入汽车，这些系统将需要至少256GB内存。美光预测，L4级自动驾驶汽车将需要至少300GB RAM，因为本质上它们是轮子上的超级计算机。

汽车级内存并非普通高端芯片，而是需要数月甚至数年验证的特殊部件。它们必须承受各种环境因素——从天气、道路持续振动到极端高温和严寒。这意味着当前内存芯片短缺对汽车行业影响尤为严重，将进一步推高价格。对已在承受创纪录高价的普通购车者而言，这无疑是坏消息。如果芯片短缺持续，我们可能面临比2021年疫情期间港口关闭时更严重的车辆交付延迟和供应短缺问题。

items.title: Inside optical and the battle for scale – how the AI industry is racing to integrate photonic interconnects
items.description: With the limitations of copper looming, the industry is transitioning to photonic interconnects to scale data center capabilities. We spoke to experts such as Lightmatter chief executive Nick Harris about the new scale-out paradigm and the battle for control over emerging standards.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter AI hardware has been a pretty simple game for years: Whose GPU has the most FLOPS, the most memory, or the fastest clock? But as hyperscalers want more performance from the same size of facilities, who controls the fabric between the chips is becoming another question frequently asked – in large part because it can dictate whether AI hardware follows the principles of Moore’s law that have held for decades, or breaks out into a new paradigm.“Moore's Law is deader than a doornail, absolutely just toast,” said Nick Harris, chief executive of photonics firm Lightmatter, in an interview with Tom's Hardware Premium. The way to make a processor faster used to be to pack in more silicon, but there is a physical limit to how big a single chip can be – so performance now depends on networking dozens, then hundreds, of chips together. “Ultimately, networking is the future of computing,” Harris said. “The performance of these AI systems is completely hamstrung and bottlenecked by your ability to achieve low latency and very, very high bandwidth.”It all comes down to scale-up and scale-out. Scale-up is the ultra-low-latency interconnect inside a single server, rack, or pod that makes many accelerators behave like one giant machine. Scale-out is the wider network, combining those machines into clusters. There is a third factor, called scale-across, that links whole data centers together.
