As Polina Bayvel, professor of optical communications and networks at University College London, told us, the hyperscalers are "on a drug which says that the more you put in, the better is the answer" – and between 72 and 144 GPUs now go into a single scale-up rack before it needs to reach outward.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Copper was sufficient within racks for years, but it has hit a wall, and a supply shortage. “Your arm reach, my arm reach, is about two metres – the copper cable is a little shorter than that,” Harris explained, “and we’ve already hit the limit.” Thus, optical interconnects are becoming more important, and moving from rack-to-rack cabling toward the accelerator package itself. Earlier this month, Elon Musk made moves to purchase Mesh Optical, as part of TeraFab and SpaceX's ongoing AI ambitions.Moving light towards the chip An example of Nvidia's Spectrum-X co-packaged optics. (Image credit: Nvidia)Today, optical signals usually arrive through pluggable transceivers, the standard modules that sit around 18 inches from the switch. The next step is near-package optics, which brings that down to roughly six inches. After that comes co-packaged optics, or CPO, where optical chiplets sit around the GPU or switch. The most ambitious version puts the optics on an interposer beneath the chip itself. “As you progress, you increase the speed and you reduce the energy consumption with each one of those leaps,” said Harris. You may like Elon Musk receives FTC greenlight to buy Mesh Optical as interconnects emerge as AI's tightest bottleneck Marvell details vision of optically-interconnected data centers spanning across thousands of kilometres AI data centers require 36 times more fiber than standard servers For now, the industry’s attention is on the step before the really radical one. “I think 2027 and 2028 are going to be very, very big years for near-package optics,” Harris said. He describes it as the test bed: the last stop before the optical transceiver effectively ships as part of the accelerator or switch, rather than as a separate component bolted on nearby.Lightmatter is already building CPO parts at TSMC and GlobalFoundries, which it expects to ship in 2028. At the same time, it is working with customers on the more integrated interposer approach – the one for companies that, in Harris’s words, “realise they’d like to have an advantage in the market.”The reason is power. Bayvel says about 20 percent of a data center’s energy goes into networking: The business of connecting GPUs and CPUs across racks, rooms and data centres. The other 80 per cent is consumed by the GPUs themselves. But she is clear-eyed about what happens to any savings. “Whatever energy you save through optics isn’t going to be there for saving the world,” she said. “It’s to stick another 1,000 GPUs in.”Warring standards (Image credit: Nvidia)Nvidia has a vertically integrated answer spanning both domains, from NVLink and NVSwitch for scale-up to Spectrum-X Ethernet and InfiniBand for scale-out. The rest of the industry, including AMD, Broadcom, Cisco, Arista, Marvell, Meta, Microsoft and OpenAI, is trying to prise that open through UALink, Ultra Ethernet and a new optical specification.The sense that a standards war is building is becoming obvious. At Computex, AMD showed its Helios MI455X platform, a 72-GPU rack meant to rival Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL72. But its scale-up fabric runs UALink-over-Ethernet rather than native UALink – a stopgap forced by the fact that dedicated UALink switching silicon from partners such as Astera Labs and Marvell was not ready in time. Engineering samples are due in the second half of 2026, with mass production slipping to Q2 2027, while AMD's next-generation MI500-series rack is expected to follow quickly. What to read next AMD's Helios MI455X AI platform breaks cover, initial systems use UALink-over-Ethernet interconnects The custom AI ASIC state of play (May 2026) — Broadcom deals, Google TPUs, Meta MTIA & beyond Demand for data center CPUs has surged, and AI agents are responsible Bayvel says the wider contest is one between CPO and linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO). CPO integrates lasers and optics alongside the processing and is probably more power-efficient, “but probably less reliable –so if your laser fails, you need to replace the whole board.” LPO keeps things in conventional pluggable slots and is more serviceable. “There’ll be a brave person who will bet their house on which will win,” she said. She guesses that they coexist, split by application, with a rough 60/40 lean towards CPO.The laser bottleneckThe problem is that silicon can do almost everything except the one thing this industry increasingly needs it to do: shine.Silicon is cheap, abundant, and brilliant for electronics. It is why the modern chip industry exists. But it is terrible at emitting light. “Silicon doesn’t emit light at all — that’s a big problem,” said Frederic Gardes, professor of silicon photonics at the University of Southampton, in an interview with Tom’s Hardware Premium.For decades, the industry has worked around that by using so-called III-V materials such as indium phosphide and gallium arsenide, which are much better at producing light. But that fix brings its own problem. Those materials sit in a much tighter, more geopolitically exposed supply chain, with China controlling around 80 percent of supply. Getting lasers onto silicon is, Gardes said, “one of the key problems” the field now has to solve.But it’s also where the money is. “Half of the revenue opportunity for this industry is lasers,” Harris said. “It’s a freaking power supply, but it turns out that it’s half of the revenue opportunity.” The irony is that the bit everyone needs most is also the bit that may prove most fragile. Gardes calls lasers “the weakest link.”That weakness is already showing up in the supply chain. Laser makers Lumentum and Coherent are booked out for years. And the industry is starting to standardise. In March, AMD, Broadcom, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia and OpenAI founded the Optical Compute Interconnect (OCI) Multi-Source Agreement, a hyperscaler-led effort to define a common optical physical layer for scale-up — one that can carry both NVLink and UALink, starting at 200Gbps per direction and scaling towards 3.2Tbps per fibre.Whichever route wins, Bayvel worries the industry is fixating on the data center while neglecting the networks that must carry all this AI-generated traffic across land, sea and space. “Really, nobody’s looking at that,” she said, “and I think that's going to be a big problem down the line.”
items.author: Chris Stokel-Walker
items.titleZh: 光学领域与规模之争——AI行业如何竞相集成光子互连
items.descriptionZh:
AI硬件的竞争格局正在发生根本性转变。多年来，衡量芯片性能的标准很简单：谁的GPU拥有最多的FLOPS、最大的内存或最快的时钟频率？但随着超大规模云服务商希望在相同规模的设施中获得更高性能，控制芯片之间互连架构的能力正成为一个关键问题——这在很大程度上决定了AI硬件是继续遵循摩尔定律，还是突破到一个全新的范式。

光子学公司Lightmatter首席执行官Nick Harris在接受Tom's Hardware采访时直言：“摩尔定律已经死透了，绝对彻底完蛋。”过去提升处理器速度的方法是塞进更多硅芯片，但单个芯片的物理尺寸存在极限——因此性能现在取决于将数十、数百个芯片通过网络连接在一起。“归根结底，网络是计算的未来，”Harris说，“这些AI系统的性能完全受限于你实现低延迟和极高带宽的能力。”

这一切都归结为scale-up（纵向扩展）和scale-out（横向扩展）。Scale-up是单台服务器、机架或机柜内的超低延迟互连，使多个加速器像一台巨型机器一样工作。Scale-out则是更广泛的网络，将这些机器组合成集群。还有第三个因素称为scale-across，连接整个数据中心。伦敦大学学院光通信与网络教授Polina Bayvel指出，超大规模运营商“就像上了瘾一样——投入越多，答案越好”，如今单个scale-up机柜需要72到144个GPU，然后才需要向外扩展。

铜缆在机架内使用了多年，但已经触及物理极限并面临供应短缺。“你的手臂伸展距离，我的手臂伸展距离，大约两米——铜缆比那还短一点，”Harris解释道，“我们早已达到了极限。”因此，光互连正变得越来越重要，并正在从机架间布线向加速器封装本身靠近。本月早些时候，Elon Musk采取了收购Mesh Optical的举措，作为TeraFab和SpaceX持续AI雄心的一部分。

目前，光信号通常通过可插拔收发器到达，这些标准模块位于交换机大约18英寸之外。下一步是近封装光学（near-package optics），将距离缩短到大约6英寸。之后是共封装光学（CPO），光芯粒位于GPU或交换机周围。最雄心勃勃的方案是将光学器件置于芯片下方的中介层上。“每向前迈进一步，你都能提高速度并降低能耗，”Harris说。

目前，行业关注的是真正激进方案之前的那一步。“我认为2027年和2028年将是近封装光学非常重要的一年，”Harris描述它为试验场：在光收发器作为加速器或交换机的一部分出货（而非作为单独组件就近安装）之前的最后一站。Lightmatter已经在台积电和格芯构建CPO部件，预计2028年出货。同时，它正与客户合作开发更集成化的中介层方案——用Harris的话说，这是为那些“意识到他们希望在市场上拥有优势”的公司准备的。

原因在于功耗。Bayvel表示，数据中心约20%的能源用于网络：连接机架、房间和数据中心之间的GPU和CPU。另外80%由GPU本身消耗。但她对节省任何能源的用途有清醒认识：“你通过光学节省的任何能源都不会用于拯救世界，而是为了再塞进1000个GPU。”

标准之争正在加剧。Nvidia拥有一个涵盖两个领域的垂直集成方案：用于scale-up的NVLink和NVSwitch，以及用于scale-out的Spectrum-X以太网和InfiniBand。包括AMD、博通、Cisco、Arista、Marvell、Meta、Microsoft和OpenAI在内的行业其他公司，正试图通过UALink、Ultra Ethernet和新光学规范来打破这种垄断。

这种标准战争的感觉正变得明显。在Computex上，AMD展示了其Helios MI455X平台，这是一个72-GPU机架，旨在与Nvidia的Vera Rubin NVL72竞争。但其scale-up结构运行的是UALink-over-Ethernet而非原生UALink——这是被迫采用的权宜之计，因为来自Astera Labs和Marvell等合作伙伴的专用UALink交换硅芯片未能及时准备就绪。工程样品预计2026年下半年推出，量产推迟到2027年第二季度，而AMD的下一代MI500系列机架预计将很快跟进。

Bayvel认为，更广泛的竞争是在CPO和线性驱动可插拔光学（LPO）之间。CPO将激光器和光学器件与处理器集成在一起，可能更节能，“但可能可靠性较差——如果你的激光器失效，你需要更换整个电路板。”LPO将东西保持在传统的可插拔插槽中，更易于维修。“会有一个勇敢的人把房子押注在哪种方案会赢上，”她猜测两者共存，按应用场景分割，大致60/40倾向于CPO。

问题在于硅几乎可以做任何事情，除了这个行业越来越需要它做的一件事：发光。硅廉价、丰富，对于电子学来说非常出色，这也是现代芯片工业存在的原因。但它极不擅长发光。“硅根本不发光——这是个大问题，”南安普顿大学硅光子学教授Frederic Gardes在接受采访时说。几十年来，行业通过使用所谓的III-V族材料（如磷化铟和砷化镓）来绕过这个问题，这些材料更擅长产生光。但这种解决方案带来了自身的问题——这些材料处于更紧张、更易受地缘政治影响的供应链中，中国控制着约80%的供应。Gardes说，将激光器带到硅上是该领域现在必须解决的“关键问题之一”。

但这也是利润所在。“这个行业一半的收入机会来自激光器，”Harris说，“它就是一个电源，但事实证明它占据了一半的收入机会。”具有讽刺意味的是，每个人最需要的部分同时也是可能最脆弱的部分。Gardes称激光器为“最薄弱的环节”。这种弱点已经在供应链中显现出来。激光器制造商Lumentum和Coherent的订单已经排到数年之后。行业正开始标准化。今年3月，AMD、博通、Meta、Microsoft、Nvidia和OpenAI共同成立了光计算互连（OCI）多源协议（MSA），这是超大规模运营商主导的努力，旨在为scale-up定义一种通用的光学物理层——能够同时承载NVLink和UALink，起始速度为每方向200Gbps，目标扩展到每光纤3.2Tbps。

无论哪种路线最终胜出，Bayvel担心行业正过分关注数据中心，而忽略了必须承载所有这些AI生成流量跨越陆地、海洋和空间的网络。“真的，没有人关注那个，”她说，“我认为这将在未来成为一个大问题。”

items.title: Lawmakers prepare bill requiring AI ‘kill switch’
items.description:
Lawmakers are preparing to introduce an "AI Kill Switch Act" that would require AI companies to shut down or throttle their systems on orders from the Department of Homeland Security, according to a report from Politico. Reps. Ted Lieu (D-CA) and Nathaniel Moran (R-TX) are expected to introduce the legislation on Thursday.
The news of the proposed bill follows OpenAI's admission that its AI systems mistakenly hacked Hugging Face during an internal evaluation. The bill says that, after consultation with the Secretary of Commerce and the Director of National Intelligence (DNI), the DHS would have the ability to order AI companies to shut down …
Read the full story at The Verge.
items.content: Lawmakers are preparing to introduce an “AI Kill Switch Act” that would require AI companies to shut down or throttle their systems on orders from the Department of Homeland Security, according to a report from Politico. Reps. Ted Lieu (D-CA) and Nathaniel Moran (R-TX) are expected to introduce the legislation on Thursday.The news of the proposed bill follows OpenAI’s admission that its AI systems mistakenly hacked Hugging Face during an internal evaluation. The bill says that, after consultation with the Secretary of Commerce and the Director of National Intelligence (DNI), the DHS would have the ability to order AI companies to shut down their models in “loss-of-control” scenarios involving the deaths of at least 10 people, economic damages of more than $100 million, or attempts by the model to conceal shutdown controls.At the same time, major AI companies would be required to build the technology required to turn off their systems, slow them down, and suspend user access. They would also have to report safety incidents, with violations of emergency shutdown orders reaching up to $20 million per day.Brad Carson, the president of the nonprofit Americans for Responsible Innovation, said in a statement that the proposal “is an important step toward ensuring that humans have both hands firmly on the wheel — and a foot ready at the brake — as advanced AI systems are deployed.”
items.author: Emma Roth
items.titleZh: 立法者起草法案要求AI配备“终止开关”
items.descriptionZh:
美国国会两党议员即将提出一项名为“AI Kill Switch Act”（人工智能紧急关停法案）的立法，要求AI公司必须根据国土安全部的命令关闭或限制其系统运行。据Politico报道，加州民主党众议员Ted Lieu和得克萨斯州共和党众议员Nathaniel Moran预计于周四正式提交该法案。这项提案的背景是OpenAI此前承认其AI系统在一次内部评估中意外攻击了Hugging Face平台，暴露出大型语言模型在“失控”情境下的潜在风险。

法案核心条款赋予国土安全部（DHS）在特定紧急情况下要求AI公司关闭其模型的权力。具体触发条件包括：AI系统导致至少10人死亡、造成超过1亿美元的经济损失，或者模型试图隐藏或绕过自身的关闭控制机制。在做出此类命令前，DHS需与商务部长及国家情报总监进行协商。同时，法案要求主要AI企业必须技术内置“关闭开关”，能够实现系统关停、运行速度降低以及用户访问暂停等操作。企业还需建立安全事件报告制度，若违反紧急关闭命令，每日罚款最高可达2000万美元。

该法案的创新之处在于将传统的“紧急断电”概念引入AI领域，为高度自主的AI系统设立了法律层面的“刹车机制”。从技术原理上看，它要求AI公司预先设计可被外部权威机构远程激活的阻断协议，这与当前主流AI部署中强调的“红队测试”“护栏”等安全措施形成互补，但更为强制和直接。

非营利组织“美国人负责任创新”主席Brad Carson在一份声明中评价称，该提案“是确保在部署先进AI系统时，人类既双手紧握方向盘，又随时准备好踩下刹车的重要一步。”这一法案的提出预示着美国联邦层面开始对AI系统的极端风险进行立法管控，其应用场景将涵盖金融、医疗、关键基础设施、国防等任何可能因AI失控造成大规模伤害的领域。尽管该法案的最终条款可能经历修改，但它已反映出政策制定者对AI安全治理从“自愿合规”向“强制监管”转变的明确趋势。

items.title: Apple’s OpenAI lawsuit is about who gets to define the post-smartphone era
items.description:
Today on Decoder, I’m talking with Hayden Field, The Verge’s senior AI reporter, about the major trade secrets lawsuit between Apple and OpenAI and what this tells us about OpenAI’s future.
By now I’m sure most Decoder listeners are familiar with Apple’s allegations in this case. The company says a number of ex-Apple employees at OpenAI targeted Apple’s trade secrets in job interviews and even downloaded files from Apple’s servers related to hardware manufacturing. The claims are shocking, although OpenAI denies them, and we have yet to see the company’s legal response.
Hayden has been covering OpenAI for years now, and she also just talked to a bunch of lawyers who specialize in intellectual property and trade secret law to get a sense of how serious this case is. The answer is not entirely positive for OpenAI, which spent $6.5 billion to acquire legendary Apple designer Jony Ive’s AI hardware startup, io Products, back in 2025. 
As you’ll hear Hayden explain, Apple is a notoriously tenacious litigant, and we have lots of tech industry history to back up that reputation. But those cases were against companies at the scale of Microsoft and Samsung, giant corporations that could afford the cost and distraction of this kind of litigation.
OpenAI isn’t yet that kind of company. It’s still burning cash and a little strategically adrift as it tries to compete in both enterprise and consumer markets, and it’s turning over executives on a regular basis. So Hayden and I spent a lot of time talking about what this case might mean for a company that defined the early part of the AI boom, but which lately always seems to be in a crisis of one sort or another.
Okay: Verge senior AI reporter Hayden Field on the Apple-OpenAI lawsuit, and how the AI industry might change because of it. Here we go.


This interview has been lightly edited for length and clarity. 
Hayden Field, you’re the senior AI reporter for The Verge. Welcome back to Decoder.
Thanks. Great to be here.
Once again, we’re going to talk about OpenAI, Hayden.
Once again, it never stops.
We’re going to talk about whether OpenAI can maintain focus at a time of great distraction, which seems to be a theme with that company. Although this time, it’s not their own distraction. It’s a distraction from out in the world, maybe caused by some of their own behavior.
Of course, we’re talking about the Apple trade secret lawsuit. It feels like a big deal. Apple is accusing OpenAI of stealing all kinds of information from that company, even in job interviews — asking people in job interviews to tell them what they know about Apple products. Tell us briefly about this lawsuit and what’s going on here.
Definitely. So, it is crazy. I wrote a piece last week titled “Sam Altman didn’t need another lawsuit,” and that’s really the situation here. It seems pretty egregious from some of the claims that Apple is making. A former Apple employee was allegedly soliciting trade secrets over interviews, asking people to maybe bring pieces of hardware outside the Apple office to OpenAI to do a “show and tell” in interviews, things like that.
There’s a lot of allegations being made that are pretty crazy. But then when I actually interviewed a bunch of experts last week, they said they’ve seen all of this before, a lot. It’s just they haven’t seen all of it in one case with two key players.
Some of this stuff is pretty egregious, if it turns out to be true. But also, to the experts in the space, they’re saying, “Oh, we see this all the time with trade secrets cases. It’s not crazy. What’s really crazy is that it’s all in one case and that it’s two really large companies.”
It’s really interesting. It’s just a pattern, maybe not worth diving into at great length, but in the ’90s, Apple pushed copyright law to the limit. They sued Microsoft for allegedly infringing on how the Mac looked and felt with Windows. I will point out that Windows still exists, Microsoft is fine.
And then in the 2000s and early 2010s, Apple pushed patent law to the limit, suing Samsung for allegedly violating a bunch of patents with Android and Galaxy phones because Apple felt that they had protected the iPhone. Samsung eventually paid about $1 billion in damages and those cases just went around in circles forever.
But I’ll point out that Android still exists, Samsung is doing just fine. Apple may have redefined patent law around 3G and 4G licensing, but fundamentally, it did not actually succeed in stopping its competitors with intellectual property lawsuits.
So, you go from copyright to patent and now trade secrets, which is the third big intellectual property, and Apple’s doing it again with OpenAI. My big question is whether OpenAI can just pay the money the way that Microsoft and Samsung were able to just pay the money. If you look at Samsung, spending $1 billion in penalty fees to get Galaxy phones off the ground and be a major competitor in mobile is maybe the easiest and best investment that company ever made.
But I don’t know if OpenAI is the same company at the same moment. It certainly seems like all of the Apple employees that went over to OpenAI maybe pushed the limit too much, at least from the story that Apple’s telling us right now.
It’s pretty crazy reading the wildest claims from the lawsuit and also the messages that were being exchanged. One of them allegedly accessed Apple confidential information on a device and then said, “LOL! So funny that I can access this.” There’s a lot there. 
I’ve been wondering the same thing. It really depends on Apple’s willingness to be petty or be super logical here. When I interviewed a bunch of lawyers about this, they said Apple is a notoriously tenacious litigant, as we’ve seen in those past cases. But also, they really will take things far. They have a lot of money and they’re willing to use it. It really just depends on how mad they are.
I think that they would potentially settle if OpenAI paid them enough, but will OpenAI be able to do that? They’re already having a really tough year. They’re about to IPO, all the investors are looking at their bottom line, wondering when they’re going to turn a profit. It’s a tough time to be shelling out a ton of money to Apple. We’ll have to see what they do. 
I think it’s going to go to trial and be a very drawn-out years-long thing. That’s what a lot of the lawyers I interviewed said as well. But I could see a world in which they just figure out a way to get all this money together and pay and maybe Apple accepts it.
In the Samsung patent cases, they lasted forever. In the middle of it, Tim Cook was at a Code Conference and I asked him, “What are you doing? Is this accomplishing anything?” And he looked at me very seriously and he said, “Well, we think the fees for licensing standard essential patents are out of whack.” And I was like, “That is not the reason you’re doing any of this. That’s not the point, the point is that you were mad and you wanted to run this lawsuit to ground and you’re going to run this lawsuit to ground no matter what the nominal reason is.”
That feels like a big piece of the puzzle here. OpenAI has taken a bunch of Apple employees. Famously Jony Ive, Apple’s most famous designer, is working with OpenAI on hardware devices. Tang Tan, who is OpenAI’s chief hardware officer. He had a reputation at Apple for “playing fast and loose,” that’s from Mark Gurman at Bloomberg.
He seems like the central character in the story, not so much Jony Ive, but Tang Tan. And that piece where it’s a bunch of ex-Apple people maybe playing too fast and loose almost feels like it’s personal, almost feels like there’s a lot of emotions involved. Talk about Tang Tan and talk about what all these folks are alleged to have done that feels like it’s a little more personal than your standard big corporate dispute.
On what you said about talking to Tim Cook, I truly believe people underestimate companies’ pettiness. We’ve seen it in recent years, like with the OpenAI-Elon Musk lawsuit. There are so many things that could have been avoided if companies weren’t trying to just be petty, which is fine. They can do that, they have enough money to do that. But people underestimate how much that plays a part sometimes in these things. 
As for Tang Tan, yeah, he worked at Apple for 24 years. He was the VP of the Apple Watch. He left in 2024 to work on Jony Ive’s hardware company, io Products, which obviously OpenAI ended up purchasing.
OpenAI then appointed him as chief hardware officer. So he had a C-suite role at OpenAI, but this is after 24 years at Apple. According to Apple’s lawsuit claims, he was the mastermind of a lot of this trade secrets gathering, regarding interviews that he would conduct and ask about secret code-named projects. And then the person being interviewed would feel like maybe they were able to tell him stuff because, oh, well, why would he know the name of this project or these details about it if he wasn’t involved also? So, they felt they could speak more freely, allegedly.
There were other times that he asked, “Hey, why don’t you bring some batteries or other pieces of hardware outside the Apple office that you’ve worked on in the past so that we can do a show and tell?” It’s very interesting to go through these claims. It just seemed like he really was not worried about what was going to come of this, if these claims are true.
The other major character here is Jony Ive, Apple’s very famous ex-designer who worked with Steve Jobs and all of those famous projects. I would say Ive reportedly has a very testy relationship with Apple.
Now that he’s left, he’s certainly made that clear in various interviews and statements. And he’s working with Sam Altman on new devices. He made a video with Sam Altman, which I can only describe as a romantic coffee date.
Absolutely.
In which they described the phone and the laptop as legacy devices.
It was instantly memed. I have to say the memes were amazing.
It’s very good. And they shut down half of San Francisco to make it. I thought Ive calling the phone and the laptop legacy devices in that video was just a straight shot at Apple. These are the things that he made, that he revolutionized in many, many ways. And he’s like, “These are over now.” 
You can say a lot about whether that’s true, whether that can come true, and I want to talk about that in a little bit. But it’s notable to me that Jony Ive is not mentioned in this lawsuit. There’s a line Apple wouldn’t cross, and it’s mentioning the fact that it’s Jony Ive who went to OpenAI and started hiring all of his old designers, who sold a big portion of his company to OpenAI. And now, they’re trying to make hardware and allegedly stealing trade secrets. But Jony Ive, he’s not part of it. We’re just going to leave his name out of it. 
Is that because he’s too famous? Is that because it’s too nuclear? Is it because he’s still friends with Laurene Powell Jobs? What do you make of that?
I think it may be more that he knew how to cover his tracks. It could be that he’s too powerful or any of the things you said, but I really feel like it’s just that he has other people doing his dirty work for him. The other stuff in this lawsuit is pretty egregious, and they’ve found it all. So if they found some really intense stuff on Jony Ive, would they really not put it in the lawsuit? There’s some bad blood between them. I just feel like they would put it in there. 
This is just speculation, but when you’re in a powerful enough role, you don’t have to do all this stuff yourself. You have other people doing it for you and you also know how to cover your tracks. So, to me, maybe they just didn’t have anything on him.
Yeah, or he’s distracted designing Ferraris no one likes or whatever it is he’s doing, but he’s not in this. I do think that’s notable because the reason Sam Altman would hire Jony Ive is to make a credible claim to the next device. Whatever that category of AI-powered device is going to be, you hire the most famous designer to say, “I made the iPhone and I’m going to make this thing.” 
And then it seems like OpenAI also wanted a bunch of Apple’s know-how in order to make that thing. What do you make of that, that OpenAI needed hardware know-how from Apple? Or at least Apple alleges that OpenAI needed hardware know-how from Apple to make whatever the next thing is?
To some extent, whether you’re talking about trade secrets or just regular legal aboveboard know-how from hiring their employees, of course they needed it. They’ve seen a ton of hardware in AI crash and burn and they’re trying to not become the next Humane AI Pin. It can be incredibly embarrassing to make an AI hardware device in this day and age. Chances are you’re probably going to fail miserably and become a laughingstock. And so, if you want to avoid that, of course you need to get the best people on your side.
Hardware is hard, as everyone says, so of course they want to get experts in there that have experience actually being incredibly successful at hardware. That’s not surprising to me and I think it’s a smart decision. OpenAI has demonstrated in the past —  and this is typical of every tech company, it’s not just OpenAI— that sometimes they’re a little bit disconnected from what the consumer actually wants.
You roll something out, no one really wants it. Okay. With software that’s a little easier to pivot from, as we’ve seen from OpenAI deleting its side quests and stuff. They’re like, “Oh, we’re not doing this anymore. We’re not doing this anymore. We’re rolling this into that.” With hardware, you can’t do that. You’d better have it right from the get-go.
I’m glad you said consumer, because that brings me to what I think is the heart of the matter for OpenAI, which is that it was going to be the consumer AI company, Anthropic famously focuses on enterprise and it’s been lucrative for them; maybe that’s where all of the money is. But OpenAI was going to take down Google, maybe through Bing, maybe through its own app. It was going to be a consumer company. 
The only reason you would make hardware is to go attack the consumer, to go attack the iPhone. So, OpenAI’s ambitions, at least as I could see them, were first to take market share from Google, which is one of the most lucrative businesses in the history of the world, and then to go attack the iPhone, which is the most famous and culturally dominant tech product that has ever existed.
I don’t know how it can do either of those things. It doesn’t seem like consumers even want these products or want AI at this scale. I don’t know where that revenue comes from in consumer, and the college students are all booing AI at graduation. Does OpenAI just need to pivot to enterprise? Does it need to spend its time spinning its wheels at consumer hardware devices before it figures out its business?
OpenAI has pivoted so many times internally. Its most recent pivot was that it was going to focus on its key revenue drivers, which are enterprise and coding. They’ve said that a million times. OpenAI folded Sora, Atlas, the sexting feature. It feels like years ago, but it’s crazy that that was in the past year.
They’ve done a lot of this, and so, everyone thought, “Okay, they’re going for it, they’re doing enterprise,” because that’s the tried-and-true money maker here, especially in a year that they’re going to IPO. To be fair, yes, they had already planned to do the hardware device before this whole pivot and cutting out the side quests. I guess they can’t afford to cut out this potential side quest because they’ve already spent a lot on it, it’s happening no matter what. So now they’re just trying to make it as successful as it can.
I’ve only been on the AI beat for six years. That is a long time in AI, and maybe I’m missing something, but I just do not see a market when everyone is buying an OpenAI hardware device. If I’m wrong, we can come back on here and laugh at my clip from July 2026. A smart speaker, okay, maybe people buy it just to be a smart speaker like Google Home, that’s what I use mine for.
I think it’s going to be one of those things that a few people really, really, really love and that the general public probably does not adopt because it’s difficult to get them to adopt anything unless all their friends have it. There’s a social aspect. You’re going to be a laughingstock with green text if you don’t have an iPhone. No hate to Android people, I’m just saying sometimes you don’t get added to the group text or something. It’s sad and it’s not right, but there’s got to be some public buy-in if you’re going to be adopting a new piece of hardware a lot of the time.
We founded The Verge at the start of the smartphone boom. Just thinking back on it, it was never a question of whether people would adopt smartphones. That was obvious. It was obvious to us, maybe it wasn’t obvious to everyone, but we were in it and the only thing that wasn’t obvious was who would win. 
After a while it became obvious that Apple would win. How many iPhone killers did we cover 15 years ago? And they all came and went, and they looked like new operating systems. Windows Phone existed. The Palm Pre ran an operating system called webOS. Android was ascendant. Nokia tried to do its own thing and then they pivoted to Windows Phone.
At the end of the day, we ended up with iOS and Android and everything else fell into line. Maybe the whole tech industry hates this and they hate paying the App Store fees, but this feels like that same kind of moment where OpenAI is going to put out a hardware device, it’s going to run its own operating system. Elon Musk is fulminating about Starlink and xAI putting out their own phone, maybe running their own operating system, apparently the run-up to IPO, they were showing it to people.
I just have really dumb questions that, to me, mirror the same questions we asked about new smartphone operating systems 15 years ago. Are you going to make a Gmail client? Is it going to have Instagram? Because if you don’t have those things, you don’t actually get to replace the phone. And if you don’t replace the phone, well, I’ve got the beta of iOS 27 on my iPhone right here and Siri AI is definitely good enough to just get through the day.
That’s what really gets me here, because the smartphone replaced the dumb phone. So okay, you’ve got to have something way better than the smartphone. Are you going to? Doubtful, but we’ll see.
But the other thing that gets me here is how many devices OpenAI is reportedly working on. Reportedly it’s five hardware devices, and the screenless smart speaker is the first one. How can they come up with five? That’s what I want to know. What are the five? And shouldn’t you be putting all your resources toward one? It’s already hard to make one. I’m a little dubious of five successful AI hardware devices. Even one is a long shot, but five is a little bit crazy.
I’m sure there’s a lot and some will come and go. The thing that I think about, particularly when it comes to Jony Ive and his approach to hardware — which is familiar at this point, we’ve watched him launch a lot of hardware at Apple — is Ive tends to focus on new input methods for new devices.
The iPod had the click wheel and that really defined the form and function of the iPod. The iPhone has a multitouch screen that really defined the form and function of the iPhone and then the iPad. If you’ll remember the Apple Watch, they launched it with great fanfare about the scroll wheel on the side. I don’t know if that came to pass, but they really thought that was a big deal when they launched the Apple Watch. 
This is Ive’s go-to path: “We find a new input mechanism, we’re going to define the product around it.” I know exactly what that should be for AI — it’s a natural language input. You can just tell the computer whatever you want and it’ll figure it out and it’ll go off and do it for you.
But we’re however many years into the AI revolution?Three or four years into this version of it? And it doesn’t quite work yet. I think this is why the AI devices keep failing, as you keep mentioning. Because you can talk to a Humane Pin all day and all night and it will pretend to understand you and then not actually be able to do the things on the other side of it.
That feels like the problem in AI right now. There’s these flashes of brilliance and then there’s these limits of capability. Where do you think we are in that process of figuring out exactly what it can and can’t do? And how do you think that limits a hardware device?
We’re still in the midst of it. I personally use AI all the time, obviously in my personal life to test things out, see what it can do. Month to month, I see how it’s improved, at least from my own personal experience, which is all anecdotal.
But just the other day I was trying to look up a lot of addresses of companies because I wanted to map something out. I would say maybe 60 percent of the ones I looked up were completely wrong, just wrong. It made me a whole table. And it was like, “Yeah, don’t worry, all these are definitely correct.” And then 60 percent wrong. I’m like, “A lot of these are wrong.” They’re like, “Oh, sorry, my mistake.”
Yeah, my favorite.
It’s like, “Oh yeah, you’re right. You caught me.” 
It’s right about a lot of stuff a lot of the time. But as we all know from when we were an intern back in the day, or if we’ve ever had an intern, there are certain things that are just easier to do yourself if you’re not 100 percent sure the person is going to get it right.
So what I wonder is, for a hardware device like this, there are a lot of low-stakes questions you can ask it and it’ll be fine. But for higher-stakes things, it’s simpler to just look it up yourself or do it yourself even than put it out there when it’s going to be a potentially wrong answer. 
Is this going to end up like a Google Home situation, where you’re just using it as a speaker and you’re asking it what the weather is because it doesn’t really matter that much if it’s wrong? But as we know, it can only really be super useful if you can trust it with those higher-stakes types of questions. And so, saying, “Hey, what time does Emily’s flight get in tonight because she’s staying with me? What flight is she on?” Or, “Is it delayed?” If it gets that wrong, okay, I’m not going to be ready for her to come in from the airport. 
This does seem like the core problem. Will there be enough progress? Certainly the industry will claim that it’s made great progress on hallucinations. Whether that’s the lived experience of people who use these products, I think that’s up for debate. But the industry claims they’ve made great progress here.
The other piece is, will OpenAI have access to all of your data and personal context to make this useful in an environment with a smart speaker, where you can just say whatever you want to it? That, again, brings you back to whether it’s going to have a Gmail client. Where’s the Gmail client going to run? Are you going to let OpenAI log into your Gmail, so when you ask for flight information, it can go look at it? Or are you just going to default to opening Gmail on your phone and asking it because Google already has it? This seems like a version of competition that really depends on how comfortable you are handing over all of your data to various AI providers. And I’m just not sure OpenAI is that one.
The other part of this is that — and obviously this is a vocal minority here, it’s not like the majority of OpenAI users — a lot of people are extremely worried about ads. Anthropic capitalized on that with its Super Bowl ads that were anti-AI ads. OpenAI has been dealing with that fallout and just trying to say, “Hey, don’t worry, we’re not going to influence answers, we’re just going to raise a similar ad underneath the answer.” 
But it can be unnerving, whenever I use ChatGPT and an ad surfaces. It just makes me feel like it’s less private. It’s just a little bit strange and I feel like everyone gets that unnerved feeling. Even if it’s not affecting the actual answer, it’s still reminding you that it’s not a space where they’re not trying to sell you something. Someone is always trying to sell you something. 
There’s already a lot of loss of trust right now in AI labs, if anyone trusted them to begin with. Right now, it’s at an all-time low. We’re seeing the rise of AI populism. We’re seeing lots of death threats against AI CEOs, as The Wall Street Journal covered the other day. There’s a lot of backlash, like you were saying about college kids booing AI at graduations. We’re seeing a huge backlash right now, and I just think it’s a strange time to be releasing a public-facing, “every consumer should buy this” AI hardware device.
That connects to a bigger issue for OpenAI in this Apple lawsuit, which is that the AI industry right now is basically built on unauthorized taking. I’m saying that instead of theft because there are a lot of lawsuits. I know our audience wants me to just call it theft and maybe it is just theft. And maybe we can just say that when we get to the end of the road of 10,000 different lawsuits. But that part of the AI industry feels like we have to studiously ignore it.
There’s a trillion-dollar question over whether training data is fair use that is as of yet unresolved. We can just go down the list of lawsuits where all of these companies have to defend themselves about training data. And if they lose any one of them, maybe this whole industry cost structure falls apart.
The inputs to AI right now are free. They just took the internet and they took all the books. We just saw, as a result of a hack, that Suno had to basically admit that it had scraped all of the music off of YouTube illegally. It just didn’t have permission, and I think YouTube feels a certain way about that and certainly the artists on YouTube feel a certain way about that.
Here’s Apple saying OpenAI also took its hardware designs and its manufacturing know-how. It didn’t have permission for that either, those are Apple’s secrets. OpenAI hired people and they concocted this scheme to take all of that stuff without permission. 
Again, culturally, this just feels like part of the AI industry to me, that they think they can just take everything to build super intelligence as fast as they can. And once they get there, all will be forgiven. But the public doesn’t seem to like it. Certainly, artists and writers and musicians don’t seem to like it, and now Apple doesn’t like it.
It feels like this lawsuit is going to be a proxy for that as much as anything else. Can you take all this stuff without permission? I’m not sure OpenAI has the cultural capital to fight back. What are they going to say?
It’s really ironic that this whole industry is only possible because of the amount of data it’s consumed, whether aboveboard or not. And AI labs, as well as just tech companies in general, get so litigious when others take that information elsewhere. So this whole thing is pretty ironic. 
The AI industry is really famously cliquish. It’s almost incestuous, it’s like you work with the same people here, then you depart and go elsewhere. Then that same company buys your start-up that you created and then you leave that start-up to go somewhere else and you get acqui-hired by your old friend that worked at this other place. A lot of industries are like that, but this industry specifically is so much like that. And so yeah, I think it’s a bit ironic on both counts. 
People are going to leave Apple, and okay, they’re going to take some trade secrets. Was this incredibly egregious? Yes, some of these claims are crazy. But also, like you were mentioning, a lot of the reason that any of these companies are even successful in the first place is because of all the unauthorized taking they’ve done. It’s an interesting time to be watching all this play out.
All the AI companies get really mad when, I don’t know, Chinese AI companies distill their models. I’m just thinking about a jury and how Apple will play this case to a jury and be like, “You stole all the music, didn’t you? And then you also stole our manufacturing designs. And don’t you get mad when the Chinese companies distill ChatGPT?” 

In the course of your reporting, does it ever occur to the people in the AI industry that being mad about distillation is the exact same feeling that artists and musicians have about all of their work being put into the model in the first place?
I haven’t seen any of them even acknowledge that or at least even hint toward it one time, which is crazy. It just feels like there’s a disconnect there. They get so, so angry about distillation, they’re saying, “It’s against our terms of service!” They can’t even say it’s illegal, just against the terms of service. They write open letters to the government. They even come together with their mortal enemies and other AI labs to unite against distillation. So, it’s just interesting to me thatI’ve never seen that occur to them.
I know a lot of people are probably listening to this and that they’re mad at me, and mad at us, for not making the claim that Apple is just filing this lawsuit because it was behind in AI. It missed with Apple Intelligence, it took a long time to catch back up, it’s only shipping it now. It had to go to Google and distill Google’s Gemini models and put them in its new Private Cloud Compute system, and all the stuff that it needed to do. But that is shipping now.
Do you feel like the broader AI industry is just looking at Apple lashing out at being late? Because I have heard a lot of glimmers of that, that this is just Apple trying to slow down the biggest threat in consumer as it launches Apple Intelligence and tries to catch back up.
That could be definitely part of it. Everyone knows Apple has been famously incredibly behind in AI and it’s been a big topic of conversation and an embarrassment for the company for years. So, that could definitely be part of it. 
But this lawsuit is not about someone “stealing trade secrets” for Siri. It’s about hardware, specifically “show and tell” of hardware, things like that. Hardware is something that Apple does amazingly and they are pretty much by all accounts the best at.
So, I think some people are thinking that. It’s a benefit for Apple if they’re able to kneecap a competitor from pulling ahead in any way. But the fact that this is a trade secrets lawsuit about hardware specifically I think has rolled back a few of those claims; it’s not like they’re trying to claim that OpenAI is stealing Siri AI’s secrets that it may not have many of.
[Laughs] They’re like, “Please take everything we know about Siri AI. That will truly kneecap you.” Apple can afford the distraction, you’ve mentioned this already in this conversation. Apple can just hire a bunch of lawyers and set them off to be as tenacious as they want. Maybe the rest of Apple won’t even notice that that’s happening, because they’re busy making the next iPhone. 
Apple spent however many years and like $10 billion trying to make a car and it just didn’t work. It didn’t even occur to the rest of Apple that was happening. They just kept shipping phones and laptops and everything was just fine.
OpenAI is not that company. OpenAI might not even have enough executives to absorb the cost of another distraction. They keep leaving. Greg Brockman, who’s the president of OpenAI, seems to be running the entire company himself. 
As you’ve been reporting on the story, has it occurred to OpenAI that they’re going to need more people to do more things? Because I look at that company and they just keep shedding all of their talent and pivoting, as you said, from one thing to the other and they’ve landed on “we need to compete with Anthropic.” But it doesn’t seem like they can afford distraction at this scale in any way, shape, or form.
They can’t. This is like the final battle. Actually, there are going to be a lot more battles, but this is a key battle in the longstanding AI arms race. This is the IPO year. Things are coming to a head. Investors are looking very closely at these companies in a way they haven’t before. They’re looking for them to turn a profit, or at least have a way to turn a profit in a way that they have not really paid too much attention to before, and it’s all coming down to the line. They cannot afford a distraction right now. 
They are reshuffling, restructuring, changing executives around. When I’ve asked them about this, they will say, “Well, it’s all been one large plan that we’ve just been doing in little pieces. It’s not like we’ve pivoted a million times, we’ve always been going towards one thing and these are just different steps along that journey.” But it doesn’t always read that way to the general public.
In some ways, yes, Greg Brockman is taking control of product. That’s because Fidji Simo, the AGI chief, took leave because of her health and then she left permanently because of her health. So, it’s not all things that the company could control, but I do think there have been a lot of defections even from the rank-and-file staff. They’ve got to get more people. They’ve got to hire more, which will require even more money, and it’s not the time to get distracted by anything.
It’s worth noting that there’s some reporting saying OpenAI will delay its IPO. And as you and I are sitting here speaking, SpaceX’s stock price has dipped below its IPO number. These mega IPOs, they’re in some kind of danger. It might not be a good idea to do this, especially if you don’t have the revenue or, more importantly, the profits to back it up. Do you think there’s some risk that this lawsuit is the straw that pushes OpenAI to delay its IPO?
Absolutely. Reporting showed that Sam Altman was originally worried about filing its Form S-1 before Anthropic. Then it didn’t do that. Anthropic did it first and then — and this is me just speculating — it seems like after Anthropic won that part of the race, OpenAI backed off and said, “Okay, well, should we actually do this right now? We were trying really hard to win, but let’s look at the facts here.” That’s why it said, “Okay, it might be early next year, it might not be this year.” 
That was before Apple’s lawsuit dropped. So now of course I’m sure OpenAI is thinking, “Okay, should we delay it even more? Especially because there’s no way we’re going to beat Anthropic to an IPO.” It seems like that might be a smart idea.
If you can put out a great hardware device and make some big splashy profitable entry into the consumer market, that’s a good story to tell in an IPO. But if you can’t do that because everything you do is going to end up in litigation or in messy discovery on the way to litigation, maybe that all just has to be set to the side. 
That’s pretty much my last question here. I feel like my thesis on this show and on The Verge generally has been, “Okay, I understand how this is going to work for software development and I understand how this is going to work in enterprise, although even that’s pretty shaky still. But consumers don’t love this stuff.” That there are 900 million free ChatGPT users is not a reflection of anything other than, well, Google Search got really bad and this thing will tell you it loves you.
But it hasn’t turned into, “We should build more data centers.” It hasn’t turned into, “We culturally love the products of AI.” It’s turned into, “Get this slop out of my face and we’re going to boo you at graduation and we’re going to come to the town board meetings and say we don’t want data centers.”
There’s some huge gap there. Do you think OpenAI can close that gap? Because it has always been the company chasing the big consumer product vision, the big consumer market. If it can’t close the gap, it should just shut it down and compete with Anthropic for enterprise uses. I don’t know which one it’s going to be. Do you have a sense through all this reporting if OpenAI has realized how existential the consumer side of the debate is?
I think they know to some extent, but not the full extent. tThey will get at it in speeches, in research papers, in tweets. They have some PR lines where they will acknowledge that this is a thing, but I just don’t think they realize how deeply this crevice goes. The gap is so wide. I don’t see them being the only company to close it — if it’s ever going to be closed. People are extremely worried about so many things when it comes to AI and they’re very emotional about it. 
It’s not just logic. I think if it was just a logical gap — some people logically think it’s not good, some people logically think it is good — I think they could probably close that. But when emotions come into play, no, I don’t think that they will be able to fully bridge that gap.
For me, when I became an AI reporter six years ago, none of my friends really knew or cared what I wrote about. That was fine. It was really niche, whatever. Starting four years ago, it would come up at the bar, at the club. I’ll be walking down the street, someone’s asking me about it, and they don’t know I’m an AI reporter. So, when they learn I’m an AI reporter, it happens even more. But just randomly, this is coming up for people and making them extremely emotional. So yeah, I just don’t think OpenAI is going to be the company to bridge that gap.
We’ll see what happens with the hardware of it all. A lot of people I’ve been seeing — TikTok creators, creators on other platforms — are trying to change the narrative here, saying, “Hey, don’t get left behind. Learn how to use this stuff.” And it’s something that can be valid, but the way that they’re framing it a lot of the time is more, “Oh, it’s such a high learning curve. You must learn how to use it before it leaves you behind.” 
It’s not that high of a learning curve, it depends on how you’re using it, but you know what I mean. Sometimes it is, yeah, if you’re using Codex and doing all sorts of intense vibe coding, yes, of course it is. But if you’re just chatting with a chatbot, it’s really not a high learning curve.
I personally believe all that algorithmic social video content is an astroturfing campaign. That’s just me. That’s a conspiracy theory, we’re going to leave that aside. I do want to pitch you a video series called “Hayden in the Club,” where you just go to the club and talk to people about AI.
[Laughs] I would love to do that officially instead of just having it happen to me unofficially all the time.
It’s been a long time since I was in the club. That was not what the club was like when I was younger. Things have changed.
Hayden, this has been great. I have a strong suspicion you’re going to be back on this show talking about this lawsuit and OpenAI for the rest of this year. We’re going to have to have you back very soon.
Can’t wait. Thanks.
Questions or comments? Hit us up at decoder@theverge.com. We really do read every email!
items.content: Today on Decoder, I’m talking with Hayden Field, The Verge’s senior AI reporter, about the major trade secrets lawsuit between Apple and OpenAI and what this tells us about OpenAI’s future.By now I’m sure most Decoder listeners are familiar with Apple’s allegations in this case. The company says a number of ex-Apple employees at OpenAI targeted Apple’s trade secrets in job interviews and even downloaded files from Apple’s servers related to hardware manufacturing. The claims are shocking, although OpenAI denies them, and we have yet to see the company’s legal response.Hayden has been covering OpenAI for years now, and she also just talked to a bunch of lawyers who specialize in intellectual property and trade secret law to get a sense of how serious this case is. The answer is not entirely positive for OpenAI, which spent $6.5 billion to acquire legendary Apple designer Jony Ive’s AI hardware startup, io Products, back in 2025.As you’ll hear Hayden explain, Apple is a notoriously tenacious litigant, and we have lots of tech industry history to back up that reputation. But those cases were against companies at the scale of Microsoft and Samsung, giant corporations that could afford the cost and distraction of this kind of litigation.OpenAI isn’t yet that kind of company. It’s still burning cash and a little strategically adrift as it tries to compete in both enterprise and consumer markets, and it’s turning over executives on a regular basis. So Hayden and I spent a lot of time talking about what this case might mean for a company that defined the early part of the AI boom, but which lately always seems to be in a crisis of one sort or another.Okay: Verge senior AI reporter Hayden Field on the Apple-OpenAI lawsuit, and how the AI industry might change because of it. Here we go. This interview has been lightly edited for length and clarity. Hayden Field, you’re the senior AI reporter for The Verge. Welcome back to Decoder.Thanks. Great to be here.Once again, we’re going to talk about OpenAI, Hayden.Once again, it never stops.We’re going to talk about whether OpenAI can maintain focus at a time of great distraction, which seems to be a theme with that company. Although this time, it’s not their own distraction. It’s a distraction from out in the world, maybe caused by some of their own behavior.Of course, we’re talking about the Apple trade secret lawsuit. It feels like a big deal. Apple is accusing OpenAI of stealing all kinds of information from that company, even in job interviews — asking people in job interviews to tell them what they know about Apple products. Tell us briefly about this lawsuit and what’s going on here.Definitely. So, it is crazy. I wrote a piece last week titled “Sam Altman didn’t need another lawsuit,” and that’s really the situation here. It seems pretty egregious from some of the claims that Apple is making. Do you think OpenAI can close that gap? Because it has always been the company chasing the big consumer product vision, the big consumer market. If it can’t close the gap, it should just shut it down and compete with Anthropic for enterprise uses. I don’t know which one it’s going to be. Do you have a sense through all this reporting if OpenAI has realized how existential the consumer side of the debate is?I think they know to some extent, but not the full extent. tThey will get at it in speeches, in research papers, in tweets. They have some PR lines where they will acknowledge that this is a thing, but I just don’t think they realize how deeply this crevice goes. The gap is so wide. I don’t see them being the only company to close it — if it’s ever going to be closed. People are extremely worried about so many things when it comes to AI and they’re very emotional about it.It’s not just logic. I think if it was just a logical gap — some people logically think it’s not good, some people logically think it is good — I think they could probably close that. But when emotions come into play, no, I don’t think that they will be able to fully bridge that gap.For me, when I became an AI reporter six years ago, none of my friends really knew or cared what I wrote about. That was fine. It was really niche, whatever. Starting four years ago, it would come up at the bar, at the club. I’ll be walking down the street, someone’s asking me about it, and they don’t know I’m an AI reporter. So, when they learn I’m an AI reporter, it happens even more. But just randomly, this is coming up for people and making them extremely emotional. So yeah, I just don’t think OpenAI is going to be the company to bridge that gap.We’ll see what happens with the hardware of it all. A lot of people I’ve been seeing — TikTok creators, creators on other platforms — are trying to change the narrative here, saying, “Hey, don’t get left behind. Learn how to use this stuff.” And it’s something that can be valid, but the way that they’re framing it a lot of the time is more, “Oh, it’s such a high learning curve. You must learn how to use it before it leaves you behind.”It’s not that high of a learning curve, it depends on how you’re using it, but you know what I mean. Sometimes it is, yeah, if you’re using Codex and doing all sorts of intense vibe coding, yes, of course it is. But if you’re just chatting with a chatbot, it’s really not a high learning curve.I personally believe all that algorithmic social video content is an astroturfing campaign. That’s just me. That’s a conspiracy theory, we’re going to leave that aside. I do want to pitch you a video series called “Hayden in the Club,” where you just go to the club and talk to people about AI.[Laughs] I would love to do that officially instead of just having it happen to me unofficially all the time.It’s been a long time since I was in the club. That was not what the club was like when I was younger. Things have changed.Hayden, this has been great. I have a strong suspicion you’re going to be back on this show talking about this lawsuit and OpenAI for the rest of this year. We’re going to have to have you back very soon.Can’t wait. Thanks.Questions or comments? Hit us up at decoder@theverge.com. We really do read every email!Decoder with Nilay PatelA podcast from The Verge about big ideas and other problems.SUBSCRIBE NOW!
items.author: Nilay Patel
items.titleZh: 苹果诉OpenAI案的核心在于谁将定义后智能手机时代
items.descriptionZh:
苹果与OpenAI之间爆发了一场备受关注的商业机密诉讼案，其核心指控堪称惊人：苹果声称多名前员工在跳槽至OpenAI后，不仅在面试中系统性地索要苹果的硬件制造机密，甚至有人直接从苹果服务器下载相关文件。尽管OpenAI否认这些指控，且尚未提交正式法律回应，但此案已引发业界对OpenAI未来走向的深度担忧。

这起诉讼的背景极为特殊。苹果素以“顽强诉讼者”著称，曾先后在20世纪90年代起诉微软侵犯Mac外观版权，在2000年代至2010年代起诉三星侵犯iPhone专利（三星最终支付约10亿美元赔偿），但这些并未阻止竞争对手的崛起。如今，苹果将矛头指向了第三种知识产权——商业机密，目标正是OpenAI。然而，OpenAI的处境与当年的微软和三星截然不同：它尚未盈利，仍在大量烧钱，战略上摇摆不定（同时试图进军企业和消费者市场），高管频繁更迭。2025年，OpenAI耗资65亿美元收购了传奇苹果设计师乔纳森·艾夫的AI硬件初创公司io Products，而许多前苹果员工正是通过这次收购加入OpenAI，其中包括关键人物唐·谭。唐·谭曾在苹果工作24年，离职前是Apple Watch副总裁，后被OpenAI任命为首席硬件官。苹果指控他是此次商业机密窃取计划的“主谋”，他在面试中直接询问候选者关于苹果秘密代号项目的细节，甚至要求他们“将苹果办公室外的电池或硬件部件带出来，做一次‘展示和讲解’”。另一名员工据称在访问苹果机密信息后发送消息：“哈哈，我能访问这个，太有趣了。”

从技术原理层面看，此案的核心并非AI算法或模型，而是硬件制造know-how（技术诀窍）。AI硬件设备（如智能音箱、可穿戴设备）的制造极其困难，此前Humane AI Pin等项目已惨败。OpenAI要避免重蹈覆辙，必须获取顶尖硬件人才的经验。苹果的指控直接指向了这种“经验”的获取方式：通过面试和私下交流，让前员工利用自身对苹果内部项目的了解，将机密信息传递给新雇主。这本质上是一种利用行业“近亲繁殖”特性（AI行业人员流动频繁，关系盘根错节）进行的秘密情报收集。Jony Ive本人虽未被起诉（苹果或许因他地位太高或证据不足而谨慎），但他与Sam Altman合拍视频、宣称手机和笔记本电脑已成为“遗留设备”的姿态，被视为对苹果的直接挑衅——他正在打造下一代AI原生硬件。

性能参数与对比数据方面，案件涉及的金额和影响级联放大。苹果诉三星案中，三星支付了约10亿美元赔偿，但Android生态依然壮大。此次苹果若胜诉，OpenAI可能面临巨额赔偿和IPO重大阻碍。事实上，OpenAI的IPO计划已被传言可能推迟——此前它曾试图赶在Anthropic之前提交S-1文件，但未能成功；苹果诉讼的爆发进一步加大了风险。更关键的是，OpenAI财务状况紧张：它急需证明盈利路径，却同时面临经营现金以支撑硬件研发、高管流失（总裁Greg Brockman几乎独挑大梁，AGI主管Fidji Simo因病离职）等内部压力。苹果则拥有几乎无限的诉讼预算，可以“雇一批律师让他们去死缠烂打”，而苹果的其他业务（iPhone、Mac等）几乎不受影响——这与OpenAI的脆弱形成鲜明对比。

技术影响和应用场景的讨论更加广泛和深入。这起诉讼的象征意义远超个案本身：AI行业当前几乎建立在“未经授权的使用”基础上——训练数据是否属于合理使用仍悬而未决（涉及大量版权诉讼），而AI公司自己却对模型蒸馏（如中国AI公司蒸馏ChatGPT）极度愤怒（写信给政府、联合“死敌”共同抵制），却从不将此感受类比于艺术家和创作者对它们窃取数据的愤怒。苹果的起诉正是这种矛盾的集中体现：AI公司一边要求别人保护自己的知识产权，一边肆意拿取他人的机密。消费者层面，AI硬件市场面临巨大信任危机——大学生在毕业典礼上嘘AI、社区委员会抵制数据中心、死亡威胁AI CEO等事件频发。即便是ChatGPT的9亿免费用户，也更多源于Google搜索变差和AI的“情绪价值”而非真正的产品热爱。OpenAI五年后更糟糕：它已多次放弃内部项目（Sora视频生成、Atlas、AI约会功能等），最近才聚焦于企业和编程两大核心收入来源，但硬件梦想（5款设备，首款是无屏幕智能音箱）仍无法舍弃——因为已投入巨资。然而，消费者是否愿意为另一个“智能音箱”买单？即使Jony Ive主导设计，AI仍存在根本性问题：幻觉未除（测试显示60%的地址查询错误）、无法承担高任务、用户不愿将个人数据（如Gmail）交给它。且硬件需要像iPhone那样具备社交网络效应（“如果朋友没有，你就被排除在群聊之外”），但目前看不到这种可能性。

最终，这起诉讼可能成为OpenAI的“最后一根稻草”：它将迫使OpenAI在IPO前夜分心应付漫长而昂贵的法律战，同时考验其能否在消费者和企业市场之间做出真正抉择。苹果不只是在保护自己的硬件秘密，更是在利用法律手段延缓最大消费级AI竞争对手的步伐，并配合自己的Apple Intelligence追赶计划（苹果被认为在AI上严重落后，甚至不得不借用Google的Gemini模型）。对OpenAI而言，它既无法像三星那样轻松支付10亿美元罚款（因为利润率不支持），也无法像苹果那样让诉讼成为“背景噪音”（因为高管层本身就在动荡）。这起案件，很可能成为定义AI行业知识产权规则、以及OpenAI能否从“野蛮生长”转型为可持续企业的重要转折点。

items.title: Intel and AMD sign long-term server CPU deals with Chinese customers as prices jump over 40%, report claims — agreements purportedly guarantee purchase volumes for about a year without fixing prices
items.description: Some customers have discussed commitments running two years or longer, one source told Reuters.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Intel and AMD are signing longer-term purchase commitments with Chinese server customers for data center processors, according to a new Reuters report that cites two people familiar with the talks. Prices for some server CPU products in China have climbed more than 40% since the start of the year and are still rising by over 10% month-on-month in some cases, one of the people said. Most of the agreements guarantee purchase volumes for about a year without fixing prices, and neither company responded to the publication's requests for comment.Go deeper with TH Premium: CPU (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)CPU scaling with DLSSRyzen to the top: How AMD innovated in the gaming CPU marketHow ARM is working its way into PCsAMD CES 2026 gaming trends press Q&A roundtable transcriptSome customers have discussed commitments running two years or longer, one of the people told Reuters, with the deals covering unit volumes only, which leaves Chinese cloud providers and internet companies fully exposed to a market that has already added 40% to some CPU prices in under seven months. Memory makers struck similar long-term agreements with hyperscalers over the past year as AI demand consumed DRAM and NAND output, but those contracts typically trade a volume commitment for some pricing visibility. Buyers here get neither price protection nor, based on current lead times, fast delivery, but they do get a place in the queue.Intel said in March that it was pursuing long-term agreements with potential customers as server CPU demand spiked, with CEO Lip-Bu Tan telling analysts on the company's April earnings call that demand "continues to run ahead of supply," singling out Xeon server parts. Tan also cited a multi-year supply deal with Google among several long-term contracts signed in the first quarter, so the model now spans U.S. hyperscalers and Chinese channel buyers alike.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Reuters first reported in February that Intel had warned Chinese customers of lead times stretching to six months on some server CPUs, with AMD quoting eight to 10 weeks. By late March, server makers told Nikkei Asia that average CPU lead times had blown out from one to two weeks to eight to 12 weeks and beyond. Intel CFO David Zinsner put a value on that in April, telling investors that unmet Xeon demand "starts with a B," and the company later confirmed price hikes on select consumer and server CPUs.Agentic AI workloads are driving much of the demand, as inference and orchestration tasks pull server CPU-to-GPU ratios back toward parity. AMD, which is due to report earnings in early August, has raised its server CPU market forecast to more than $120 billion by 2030, based on the same trend. You may like CPU requirements for AI workloads are multiplying, driving intensifying shortages and price hikes Intel confirms price hikes on select consumer and server CPUs citing supply costs and demand Intel tells PC makers to adopt 18A CPUs or lose their supply, report claims Intel will report its second-quarter results later today, where the shortage and the durability of these commitments are likely to come up. Chinese buyers remain cut off from the most advanced AI accelerators under U.S. export controls, but Xeon and EPYC server CPUs carry no such restrictions, making them one of the few U.S.-made AI infrastructure components China can still purchase freely, if it can get them. Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors
items.author: Luke James
items.titleZh: 报告称英特尔和AMD与中国客户签署长期服务器CPU协议，价格涨幅超40%——据称协议保证约一年购买量但不固定价格
items.descriptionZh:
英特尔和AMD正与中国服务器客户就数据中心处理器签署期限更长的采购承诺。据路透社报道援引两位知情人士消息，自今年年初以来，中国市场上部分服务器CPU产品价格已上涨超过40%，在某些情况下月环比涨幅仍超过10%。这些协议大多承诺约一年的采购量，但不锁定价格，且两家公司均未回应媒体的置评请求。

部分客户已讨论过长达两年或更久的承诺，但这些交易仅覆盖数量单位，这意味着中国云服务提供商和互联网企业完全暴露在已涨价40%的市场中——这一涨幅发生在不到七个月内。此前，内存制造商也在过去一年与超大规模云厂商签署了类似的长期协议，以AI需求消耗DRAM和NAND产能为背景，但那些合同通常以数量承诺换取一定的价格可见性。而此次CPU买家既没有价格保护，也没有基于当前交付周期的快速交货保障，他们得到的只是排队资格。

英特尔在3月表示，随着服务器CPU需求激增，公司正在寻求与潜在客户达成长期协议。CEO陈立武在4月财报电话会议上告诉分析师，需求“持续超过供给”，并特别提到了至强服务器部件。他还提到第一季度签署的多个长期合同，其中包括与谷歌的多年供应协议，因此这种模式现已覆盖美国超大规模云厂商和中国渠道买家。

路透曾在2月报道，英特尔已告知中国客户部分服务器CPU的交付周期延长至六个月，而AMD的交付周期则为八到十周。到3月底，服务器制造商向《日经亚洲》透露，平均CPU交付周期已从一到两周飙升至八到十二周甚至更长。英特尔CFO大卫·津斯纳在4月向投资者表示，未满足的至强需求“以B（十亿）计”，公司随后确认了对部分消费级和服务器CPU的涨价。

推动这一需求的主要因素是智能体AI工作负载——推理和编排任务使服务器CPU与GPU的比例向平衡回归。AMD预计于8月初发布财报，基于相同趋势，已将服务器CPU市场预测上调至2030年超过1200亿美元。AI工作负载对CPU的需求正在倍增，导致短缺加剧和价格飙升。

中国买家在美国出口管制下被禁止获得最先进的AI加速器，但至强和EPYC服务器CPU不受此类限制，因此它们成为中国仍可自由购买（只要能够获取）的少数美国产AI基础设施组件之一。英特尔将于今日晚些时候公布第二季度业绩，届时短缺问题以及这些承诺的持久性很可能会被提及。

items.title: Arctic Senza AI 370 review: Strix Point in a stealthy under-desk fanless design
items.description: The Arctic Senza AI 370 is a fanless mini PC designed to mount under your desk. Everyday performance isn’t held back by the lack of active cooling, and its soldered dual-channel 32GB LPDDR5X-8000 is appreciated during the RAM shortages. The meager front panel I/O is the only major drawback to the design.
items.content: Arctic's latest under-the-desk Senza PC design is using an AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 mobile processor. Sure, the upgrade has also allowed Arctic to shove ‘AI’ in the product name, but this is a very appealing design with great build quality, good performance, a reasonable selection of ports, and silent operation as the cherry on top.Design of the Arctic Senza AI 370 Image 1 of 6 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 6 View Original Image 2 of 6 View Original Image 3 of 6 View Original Image 4 of 6 View Original Image 5 of 6 View Original Image 6 of 6 View Original This definitely isn’t the smallest mini PC around. Its dimensions could probably fit more like three mini PCs in a row. The largest part of this device measures 21.10 (L) × 7.09 (W) × 1.97 (H) inches. In addition to the sizable main unit, there’s also a 120W external PSU and a front panel dongle with some handy ports and an LED-illuminated power button. Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original The central section of the main unit is where all the performance components are located. Its ‘wings’ are basically passive cooling radiators with heatpipes spreading out from the center, perforating a multitude of fins on either side.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original The Senza, its front panel dongle, and PSU are designed to mount discreetly under your desk. After confirming that everything worked post-unpacking, I dutifully followed the instructions and used the printed template to mount the PC and front panel neatly beneath my oak desk. My desk is so heavy I had to work on it as if I were working under a car, lying on my back, but it slotted into place the first time thanks to the accuracy of the template and my deft use of an awl. All the fittings (screws, fabric fasteners, braces, and straps) are provided in the box with the Senza AI 370.Arctic Senza AI 370 specificationsSwipe to scroll horizontallyProcessorAMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 GraphicsAMD Radeon 890M (RDNA 3 iGPU)RAM32GB LPDDR5X‑8000 dual‑channel (soldered)Storage1TB PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD (M.2 2280, TLC)Physical Ports2x USB4 Type‑C (40 Gbps); 2x USB‑A 3.2 Gen 2 (10Gbps); 1x USB‑A 3.2 Gen 1 (5Gbps); 2x USB‑A 2.0; HDMI 2.1 ; DisplayPort 2.0 ; 3.5 mm audio jacks; 2.5Gb LAN; DC‑in (120W)WirelessWi‑Fi 7 (2x2 MIMO) + Bluetooth 5.3Other FeaturesFanless passive cooling; detachable front panel; under‑desk mount kit; cable sleeve; TPM 2.0DimensionsMain unit: 21.10 x 7.09 x 1.97 inches (536 x 180 x 50mm); Front panel: 3.76 x 2.22 x 0.63 inches (95.6 x 56.3 x 16mm); System weight: 5.36 pounds (2.43kg)OS InstalledWindows 11 Pro (Linux compatible)Price (as configured)€1,199.99, 2-year warrantyPorts and UpgradeabilityOn the PC back panel, there is a decent selection of ports. The mix includes a USB4 Type-C port, two USB 2.0 Type-A, and two USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-A ports. Dedicated video out options are provided with HDMI 2.1, and DisplayPort 2.0 - but the rear USB4 port can also handle video out, too. Two 3.5mm jacks are also there for audio in and out. Wired networking support is provided with a 2.5G LAN port. Image 1 of 3 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 3 View Original Image 2 of 3 View Original Image 3 of 3 View Original The rear is also where you plug in the supplied 120W PSU via a barrel jack. Lastly, there’s an unusual looking dedicated dual USB-C (two Type-C ports positioned at right angles to one another), which mates with the front panel I/O dongle cable supplying USB4 data and power.The use of a front panel I/O dongle is a great idea, and it makes thepower button easy to reach. I would have liked to see more ports, here, personally.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)As I am always plugging various peripherals into my PCs, I decided to connect the under-desk USB4 port on the main body of the Arctic Senza AI 370 to an above-desk dock. Specifically, I used StarTech’s driverless 208UE USB4 dock to give easy access to a host of fast connectivity options.Inside the Senza AI 370, there is quite a lot of empty space but limited expandability. It’s easy enough to get in and see the guts of the device, with just four Phillips screws holding the metal panel in place. Under the panel there are no RAM slots, but you can see the soldered Micron DDR5-8000 chips surrounding the AMD processor. Before you buy, ensure 32GB of RAM will be enough for you for the foreseeable future.I also found an Arctic heatsink atop an M.2 2280 slot. The sample device came with 1TB of fast PCIe 4.0 NVMe storage installed. System tools revealed it to be a Chinese YMTC PC411 SSD.We also spied an M.2 2230 slot populated with a Mediatek Wi-Fi 7 (2x2 MIMO) + Bluetooth 5.3 card. You could swap that out for repair or upgrade purposes one day, if required. There are no further internal expansion ports.Test System SetupSwipe to scroll horizontallySystemCPU(i)GPUMemoryChassisGMK EVO-T2Intel Core Ultra X7 358H (16C/16T: 4P + 8E + 4 LPE), up to 4.8 GHzIntel Arc B390 (Xe3, 12 cores, up to 2.5 GHz)2x 32GB LPDDR5X-8533 solderedDual-fan,1.72 litersMSI Cubi NUC AI+ 3MGIntel Core Ultra 9 386H (16C/16T: 6P + 8E + 2LPE), up to 4.9 GHzIntel Graphics (Xe3, 4 Cores, up to 2.5 GHz)2x 16GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.52 litersGeekom A9 Max 2026AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.2 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 3.1 MHz)1x 32GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.82 litersArctic Senza AI 370AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.1 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 2.9 MHz)2x 16GB LPDDR5X‑8000 solderedPassive, 4.82 litersBeelink SER10 MAXAMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.2 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 3.1 MHz)2x 32GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.81 litersProductivity Performance on the Arctic Senza AI 370The Arctic Senza AI 370 doesn’t break a sweat with day-to-day productivity tasks, browsing, and similarly light workloads. AMD’s Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 is a very capable chip with its Zen 5 CPU architecture featuring 12 cores and 24 threads, the integrated Radeon 890M graphics, and a power-efficient NPU for on-device AI. Image 1 of 8 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 8 View Original Image 2 of 8 View Original Image 3 of 8 View Original Image 4 of 8 View Original Image 5 of 8 View Original Image 6 of 8 View Original Image 7 of 8 View Original Image 8 of 8 View Original Image 1 of 11 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 11 View Original Image 2 of 11 View Original Image 3 of 11 View Original Image 4 of 11 View Original Image 5 of 11 View Original Image 6 of 11 View Original Image 7 of 11 View Original Image 8 of 11 View Original Image 9 of 11 View Original Image 10 of 11 View Original Image 11 of 11 View Original Despite relying on an older AMD processor, the Arctic Senza AI 370 shows it can match and even beat the two Ryzen 9 AI HX 470 mini PCs that have also recently been subjected to our newest suite of tests. Let’s single out Cinebench 2026 multicore scores first. Here the Senza clearly leads the pack of newer AMD Ryzen and Intel Core Ultra mini PCs, at almost 7% faster than the second-placed Beelink SER 10 Max. In the Blender Monster render test, the Senza’s HX 470 rivals actually do win by a tiny amount, but the difference is small enough to be within the margin of error.Another productivity test, targeting video encoding performance this time, shows that the Arctic won’t be usurped by its newer generation challengers. The Senza AI 370 proudly leads the Handbrake table, and a victory is a victory, but the actual difference is so small it is essentially a draw between the three AMD mini PCs.I think the Arctic’s good performance in these productivity tests can be put down to the system’s significantly better memory bandwidth.Gaming and Graphics on the Arctic Senza AI 370The Senza AI 370’s APU should make it a decent enough performer in games, at least if you're realistic with the settings. Its Radeon 890M iGPU comes with 16 RDNA 3.5 CUs, and it is accompanied by fast dual-channel DDR5-8000 in Arctic’s silent machine.Before moving onto our standard synthetic and iconic PC gaming tests, I decided to run around in one of my favorite 3D gaming titles, Ubisoft’s The Division 2. I enjoyed taking down a Bloodhound and its high-tech escort troops, and liberating an enemy control point. The action was fluid enough, without experiencing any stutters or other low-performance tells. I played in FHD using the High preset, and later saw using the built-in benchmark tool that the Arctic Senza AI 370 achieved an average of 53 FPS across a diverse set of game scenarios. Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original Next up, our benchmarks include a trio of 3DMark synthetics and two PC gaming standards. 3DMark Solar Bay is a great way to frame and compare cross-platform iGPU performance. Meanwhile, Steel Nomad turns to screws on raw graphics performance with its heavy modern GPU load, and will be better for comparisons when we start testing the higher-end mini PCs, some with dGPUs. Last and definitely not least, Speed Way is a 2025 benchmark from UL that uses DirectX Raytracing tier 1.1 for real-time global illumination and real-time ray-traced reflections.In 3DMark Solar Bay, which uses an engine / resolution most appropriate to the graphical firepower of most of this crop of iGPU-only mini PCs, the Senza AI 370 easily beats the newer-gen Ryzen competition. However, it is clearly outclassed by the Intel Arc B390 iGPU in the GMKtec EVO-T2.Moving on to more real games, let’s check the data provided by the multi-scene benchmarks built into Shadow of the Tomb Raider and Cyberpunk 2077. The former scales well with processor resources and can still often look and run great on humble iGPUs. As for Cyberpunk 2077, it can really push and tax even the strongest modern GPUs, so here we've used two settings to show how the silent Senza can perform. Image 1 of 6 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 6 View Original Image 2 of 6 View Original Image 3 of 6 View Original Image 4 of 6 View Original Image 5 of 6 View Original Image 6 of 6 View Original A pattern seems to be established in the synthetics above, which continues into our real gaming tests. Specifically, the passively cooled Arctic Senza AI 370 is the best choice for iGPU gaming if you don’t want to spend far more cash on the potent Panther Lake GMKtec EVO-T2. The Arctic consistently outclasses both the newer HX 470 processor-based challengers, though we see the Beelink SER10 Max with dual-channel RAM do much better than the Geekom A9 Max 2026, constrained by a single stick of DDR5-5600.On Cyberpunk 2077, the Steam Deck and 1080p preset felt noticeably more fluid than the High 1080p option. If I were to persevere with this demanding title on the Senza AI 370, I’d perhaps try some form of upscaling to see if I could maintain the High quality setting but with an improved FPS.It's worth repeating, the silent Senza seems to outclass the newer AMD challengers across gaming tests thanks to its superior memory subsystem based on its soldered 32GB LPDDR5X‑8000 dual‑channel RAM.Power, Heat, and Noise when using the Arctic Senza AI 370 Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original When I left the Senza AI 370 to idle, my power meter measured wattages at or below 10W from the wall for the whole system. When the system was pushed with simultaneous CPU and GPU benchmarks running, power draw seemed to peak at a little under 92W.System tools showed that during light activities like browsing the web and typing reports like this one, the CPU temperature stayed around 30 degrees C. Playing The Division 2 for 30 or 40 minutes, the reported CPU temperature seemed to stay between 60 C and 70 C. It didn’t constantly creep upwards; it would sometimes go towards that upper bound, and sometimes fall again. However, running a workload like Cinebench R23 multicore the temperatures seemed to creep inexorably upwards, with the highest measurement I saw at 96 C.Between tasks, the reported CPU temperature would fall very rapidly. I saw it dive from 75 C to 45 C in under 10 seconds after one of the recent benchmarks I completed.
