According to Apple’s lawsuit claims, he was the mastermind of a lot of this trade secrets gathering, regarding interviews that he would conduct and ask about secret code-named projects. And then the person being interviewed would feel like maybe they were able to tell him stuff because, oh, well, why would he know the name of this project or these details about it if he wasn’t involved also? So, they felt they could speak more freely, allegedly.There were other times that he asked, “Hey, why don’t you bring some batteries or other pieces of hardware outside the Apple office that you’ve worked on in the past so that we can do a show and tell?” It’s very interesting to go through these claims. It just seemed like he really was not worried about what was going to come of this, if these claims are true.The other major character here is Jony Ive, Apple’s very famous ex-designer who worked with Steve Jobs and all of those famous projects. I would say Ive reportedly has a very testy relationship with Apple.Now that he’s left, he’s certainly made that clear in various interviews and statements. And he’s working with Sam Altman on new devices. He made a video with Sam Altman, which I can only describe as a romantic coffee date.Absolutely.In which they described the phone and the laptop as legacy devices.It was instantly memed. I have to say the memes were amazing.It’s very good. And they shut down half of San Francisco to make it. I thought Ive calling the phone and the laptop legacy devices in that video was just a straight shot at Apple. These are the things that he made, that he revolutionized in many, many ways. And he’s like, “These are over now.” You can say a lot about whether that’s true, whether that can come true, and I want to talk about that in a little bit. But it’s notable to me that Jony Ive is not mentioned in this lawsuit. There’s a line Apple wouldn’t cross, and it’s mentioning the fact that it’s Jony Ive who went to OpenAI and started hiring all of his old designers, who sold a big portion of his company to OpenAI. And now, they’re trying to make hardware and allegedly stealing trade secrets. But Jony Ive, he’s not part of it. We’re just going to leave his name out of it. Is that because he’s too famous? Is that because it’s too nuclear? Is it because he’s still friends with Laurene Powell Jobs? What do you make of that?I think it may be more that he knew how to cover his tracks. It could be that he’s too powerful or any of the things you said, but I really feel like it’s just that he has other people doing his dirty work for him. The other stuff in this lawsuit is pretty egregious, and they’ve found it all. So if they found some really intense stuff on Jony Ive, would they really not put it in the lawsuit? There’s some bad blood between them. I just feel like they would put it in there.This is just speculation, but when you’re in a powerful enough role, you don’t have to do all this stuff yourself. You have other people doing it for you and you also know how to cover your tracks. So, to me, maybe they just didn’t have anything on him.Yeah, or he’s distracted designing Ferraris no one likes or whatever it is he’s doing, but he’s not in this. I do think that’s notable because the reason Sam Altman would hire Jony Ive is to make a credible claim to the next device. Whatever that category of AI-powered device is going to be, you hire the most famous designer to say, “I made the iPhone and I’m going to make this thing.” And then it seems like OpenAI also wanted a bunch of Apple’s know-how in order to make that thing. What do you make of that, that OpenAI needed hardware know-how from Apple? Or at least Apple alleges that OpenAI needed hardware know-how from Apple to make whatever the next thing is?To some extent, whether you’re talking about trade secrets or just regular legal aboveboard know-how from hiring their employees, of course they needed it. They’ve seen a ton of hardware in AI crash and burn and they’re trying to not become the next Humane AI Pin. It can be incredibly embarrassing to make an AI hardware device in this day and age. Chances are you’re probably going to fail miserably and become a laughingstock. And so, if you want to avoid that, of course you need to get the best people on your side.Hardware is hard, as everyone says, so of course they want to get experts in there that have experience actually being incredibly successful at hardware. That’s not surprising to me and I think it’s a smart decision. OpenAI has demonstrated in the past — and this is typical of every tech company, it’s not just OpenAI— that sometimes they’re a little bit disconnected from what the consumer actually wants.You roll something out, no one really wants it. Okay. With software that’s a little easier to pivot from, as we’ve seen from OpenAI deleting its side quests and stuff. They’re like, “Oh, we’re not doing this anymore. We’re not doing this anymore. We’re rolling this into that.” With hardware, you can’t do that. You’d better have it right from the get-go.I’m glad you said consumer, because that brings me to what I think is the heart of the matter for OpenAI, which is that it was going to be the consumer AI company, Anthropic famously focuses on enterprise and it’s been lucrative for them; maybe that’s where all of the money is. But OpenAI was going to take down Google, maybe through Bing, maybe through its own app. It was going to be a consumer company. The only reason you would make hardware is to go attack the consumer, to go attack the iPhone. So, OpenAI’s ambitions, at least as I could see them, were first to take market share from Google, which is one of the most lucrative businesses in the history of the world, and then to go attack the iPhone, which is the most famous and culturally dominant tech product that has ever existed.I don’t know how it can do either of those things. It doesn’t seem like consumers even want these products or want AI at this scale. I don’t know where that revenue comes from in consumer, and the college students are all booing AI at graduation. Does OpenAI just need to pivot to enterprise? Does it need to spend its time spinning its wheels at consumer hardware devices before it figures out its business?OpenAI has pivoted so many times internally. Its most recent pivot was that it was going to focus on its key revenue drivers, which are enterprise and coding. They’ve said that a million times. OpenAI folded Sora, Atlas, the sexting feature. It feels like years ago, but it’s crazy that that was in the past year.They’ve done a lot of this, and so, everyone thought, “Okay, they’re going for it, they’re doing enterprise,” because that’s the tried-and-true money maker here, especially in a year that they’re going to IPO. To be fair, yes, they had already planned to do the hardware device before this whole pivot and cutting out the side quests. I guess they can’t afford to cut out this potential side quest because they’ve already spent a lot on it, it’s happening no matter what. So now they’re just trying to make it as successful as it can.I’ve only been on the AI beat for six years. That is a long time in AI, and maybe I’m missing something, but I just do not see a market when everyone is buying an OpenAI hardware device. If I’m wrong, we can come back on here and laugh at my clip from July 2026. A smart speaker, okay, maybe people buy it just to be a smart speaker like Google Home, that’s what I use mine for.I think it’s going to be one of those things that a few people really, really, really love and that the general public probably does not adopt because it’s difficult to get them to adopt anything unless all their friends have it. There’s a social aspect. You’re going to be a laughingstock with green text if you don’t have an iPhone. No hate to Android people, I’m just saying sometimes you don’t get added to the group text or something. It’s sad and it’s not right, but there’s got to be some public buy-in if you’re going to be adopting a new piece of hardware a lot of the time.We founded The Verge at the start of the smartphone boom. Just thinking back on it, it was never a question of whether people would adopt smartphones. That was obvious. It was obvious to us, maybe it wasn’t obvious to everyone, but we were in it and the only thing that wasn’t obvious was who would win. After a while it became obvious that Apple would win. How many iPhone killers did we cover 15 years ago? And they all came and went, and they looked like new operating systems. Windows Phone existed. The Palm Pre ran an operating system called webOS. Android was ascendant. Nokia tried to do its own thing and then they pivoted to Windows Phone.At the end of the day, we ended up with iOS and Android and everything else fell into line. Maybe the whole tech industry hates this and they hate paying the App Store fees, but this feels like that same kind of moment where OpenAI is going to put out a hardware device, it’s going to run its own operating system. Elon Musk is fulminating about Starlink and xAI putting out their own phone, maybe running their own operating system, apparently the run-up to IPO, they were showing it to people.I just have really dumb questions that, to me, mirror the same questions we asked about new smartphone operating systems 15 years ago. Are you going to make a Gmail client? Is it going to have Instagram? Because if you don’t have those things, you don’t actually get to replace the phone. And if you don’t replace the phone, well, I’ve got the beta of iOS 27 on my iPhone right here and Siri AI is definitely good enough to just get through the day.That’s what really gets me here, because the smartphone replaced the dumb phone. So okay, you’ve got to have something way better than the smartphone. Are you going to? Doubtful, but we’ll see.But the other thing that gets me here is how many devices OpenAI is reportedly working on. Reportedly it’s five hardware devices, and the screenless smart speaker is the first one. How can they come up with five? That’s what I want to know. What are the five? And shouldn’t you be putting all your resources toward one? It’s already hard to make one. I’m a little dubious of five successful AI hardware devices. Even one is a long shot, but five is a little bit crazy.I’m sure there’s a lot and some will come and go. The thing that I think about, particularly when it comes to Jony Ive and his approach to hardware — which is familiar at this point, we’ve watched him launch a lot of hardware at Apple — is Ive tends to focus on new input methods for new devices.The iPod had the click wheel and that really defined the form and function of the iPod. The iPhone has a multitouch screen that really defined the form and function of the iPhone and then the iPad. If you’ll remember the Apple Watch, they launched it with great fanfare about the scroll wheel on the side. I don’t know if that came to pass, but they really thought that was a big deal when they launched the Apple Watch. This is Ive’s go-to path: “We find a new input mechanism, we’re going to define the product around it.” I know exactly what that should be for AI — it’s a natural language input. You can just tell the computer whatever you want and it’ll figure it out and it’ll go off and do it for you.But we’re however many years into the AI revolution?Three or four years into this version of it? And it doesn’t quite work yet. I think this is why the AI devices keep failing, as you keep mentioning. Because you can talk to a Humane Pin all day and all night and it will pretend to understand you and then not actually be able to do the things on the other side of it.That feels like the problem in AI right now. There’s these flashes of brilliance and then there’s these limits of capability. Where do you think we are in that process of figuring out exactly what it can and can’t do? And how do you think that limits a hardware device?We’re still in the midst of it. I personally use AI all the time, obviously in my personal life to test things out, see what it can do. Month to month, I see how it’s improved, at least from my own personal experience, which is all anecdotal.But just the other day I was trying to look up a lot of addresses of companies because I wanted to map something out. I would say maybe 60 percent of the ones I looked up were completely wrong, just wrong. It made me a whole table. And it was like, “Yeah, don’t worry, all these are definitely correct.” And then 60 percent wrong. I’m like, “A lot of these are wrong.” They’re like, “Oh, sorry, my mistake.”Yeah, my favorite.It’s like, “Oh yeah, you’re right. You caught me.”It’s right about a lot of stuff a lot of the time. But as we all know from when we were an intern back in the day, or if we’ve ever had an intern, there are certain things that are just easier to do yourself if you’re not 100 percent sure the person is going to get it right.So what I wonder is, for a hardware device like this, there are a lot of low-stakes questions you can ask it and it’ll be fine. But for higher-stakes things, it’s simpler to just look it up yourself or do it yourself even than put it out there when it’s going to be a potentially wrong answer.Is this going to end up like a Google Home situation, where you’re just using it as a speaker and you’re asking it what the weather is because it doesn’t really matter that much if it’s wrong? But as we know, it can only really be super useful if you can trust it with those higher-stakes types of questions. And so, saying, “Hey, what time does Emily’s flight get in tonight because she’s staying with me? What flight is she on?” Or, “Is it delayed?” If it gets that wrong, okay, I’m not going to be ready for her to come in from the airport.This does seem like the core problem. Will there be enough progress? Certainly the industry will claim that it’s made great progress on hallucinations. Whether that’s the lived experience of people who use these products, I think that’s up for debate. But the industry claims they’ve made great progress here.The other piece is, will OpenAI have access to all of your data and personal context to make this useful in an environment with a smart speaker, where you can just say whatever you want to it? That, again, brings you back to whether it’s going to have a Gmail client. Where’s the Gmail client going to run? Are you going to let OpenAI log into your Gmail, so when you ask for flight information, it can go look at it? Or are you just going to default to opening Gmail on your phone and asking it because Google already has it? This seems like a version of competition that really depends on how comfortable you are handing over all of your data to various AI providers. And I’m just not sure OpenAI is that one.The other part of this is that — and obviously this is a vocal minority here, it’s not like the majority of OpenAI users — a lot of people are extremely worried about ads. Anthropic capitalized on that with its Super Bowl ads that were anti-AI ads. OpenAI has been dealing with that fallout and just trying to say, “Hey, don’t worry, we’re not going to influence answers, we’re just going to raise a similar ad underneath the answer.”But it can be unnerving, whenever I use ChatGPT and an ad surfaces. It just makes me feel like it’s less private. It’s just a little bit strange and I feel like everyone gets that unnerved feeling. Even if it’s not affecting the actual answer, it’s still reminding you that it’s not a space where they’re not trying to sell you something. Someone is always trying to sell you something.There’s already a lot of loss of trust right now in AI labs, if anyone trusted them to begin with. Right now, it’s at an all-time low. We’re seeing the rise of AI populism. We’re seeing lots of death threats against AI CEOs, as The Wall Street Journal covered the other day. There’s a lot of backlash, like you were saying about college kids booing AI at graduations. We’re seeing a huge backlash right now, and I just think it’s a strange time to be releasing a public-facing, “every consumer should buy this” AI hardware device.That connects to a bigger issue for OpenAI in this Apple lawsuit, which is that the AI industry right now is basically built on unauthorized taking. I’m saying that instead of theft because there are a lot of lawsuits. I know our audience wants me to just call it theft and maybe it is just theft. And maybe we can just say that when we get to the end of the road of 10,000 different lawsuits. But that part of the AI industry feels like we have to studiously ignore it.There’s a trillion-dollar question over whether training data is fair use that is as of yet unresolved. We can just go down the list of lawsuits where all of these companies have to defend themselves about training data. And if they lose any one of them, maybe this whole industry cost structure falls apart.The inputs to AI right now are free. They just took the internet and they took all the books. We just saw, as a result of a hack, that Suno had to basically admit that it had scraped all of the music off of YouTube illegally. It just didn’t have permission, and I think YouTube feels a certain way about that and certainly the artists on YouTube feel a certain way about that.Here’s Apple saying OpenAI also took its hardware designs and its manufacturing know-how. It didn’t have permission for that either, those are Apple’s secrets. OpenAI hired people and they concocted this scheme to take all of that stuff without permission. Again, culturally, this just feels like part of the AI industry to me, that they think they can just take everything to build super intelligence as fast as they can. And once they get there, all will be forgiven. But the public doesn’t seem to like it. Certainly, artists and writers and musicians don’t seem to like it, and now Apple doesn’t like it.It feels like this lawsuit is going to be a proxy for that as much as anything else. Can you take all this stuff without permission? I’m not sure OpenAI has the cultural capital to fight back. What are they going to say?It’s really ironic that this whole industry is only possible because of the amount of data it’s consumed, whether aboveboard or not. And AI labs, as well as just tech companies in general, get so litigious when others take that information elsewhere. So this whole thing is pretty ironic.The AI industry is really famously cliquish. It’s almost incestuous, it’s like you work with the same people here, then you depart and go elsewhere. Then that same company buys your start-up that you created and then you leave that start-up to go somewhere else and you get acqui-hired by your old friend that worked at this other place. A lot of industries are like that, but this industry specifically is so much like that. And so yeah, I think it’s a bit ironic on both counts.People are going to leave Apple, and okay, they’re going to take some trade secrets. Was this incredibly egregious? Yes, some of these claims are crazy. But also, like you were mentioning, a lot of the reason that any of these companies are even successful in the first place is because of all the unauthorized taking they’ve done. It’s an interesting time to be watching all this play out.All the AI companies get really mad when, I don’t know, Chinese AI companies distill their models. I’m just thinking about a jury and how Apple will play this case to a jury and be like, “You stole all the music, didn’t you? And then you also stole our manufacturing designs. And don’t you get mad when the Chinese companies distill ChatGPT?” In the course of your reporting, does it ever occur to the people in the AI industry that being mad about distillation is the exact same feeling that artists and musicians have about all of their work being put into the model in the first place?I haven’t seen any of them even acknowledge that or at least even hint toward it one time, which is crazy. It just feels like there’s a disconnect there. They get so, so angry about distillation, they’re saying, “It’s against our terms of service!” They can’t even say it’s illegal, just against the terms of service. They write open letters to the government. They even come together with their mortal enemies and other AI labs to unite against distillation. So, it’s just interesting to me thatI’ve never seen that occur to them.I know a lot of people are probably listening to this and that they’re mad at me, and mad at us, for not making the claim that Apple is just filing this lawsuit because it was behind in AI. It missed with Apple Intelligence, it took a long time to catch back up, it’s only shipping it now. It had to go to Google and distill Google’s Gemini models and put them in its new Private Cloud Compute system, and all the stuff that it needed to do. But that is shipping now.Do you feel like the broader AI industry is just looking at Apple lashing out at being late? Because I have heard a lot of glimmers of that, that this is just Apple trying to slow down the biggest threat in consumer as it launches Apple Intelligence and tries to catch back up.That could be definitely part of it. Everyone knows Apple has been famously incredibly behind in AI and it’s been a big topic of conversation and an embarrassment for the company for years. So, that could definitely be part of it.But this lawsuit is not about someone “stealing trade secrets” for Siri. It’s about hardware, specifically “show and tell” of hardware, things like that. Hardware is something that Apple does amazingly and they are pretty much by all accounts the best at.So, I think some people are thinking that. It’s a benefit for Apple if they’re able to kneecap a competitor from pulling ahead in any way. But the fact that this is a trade secrets lawsuit about hardware specifically I think has rolled back a few of those claims; it’s not like they’re trying to claim that OpenAI is stealing Siri AI’s secrets that it may not have many of.[Laughs] They’re like, “Please take everything we know about Siri AI. That will truly kneecap you.” Apple can afford the distraction, you’ve mentioned this already in this conversation. Apple can just hire a bunch of lawyers and set them off to be as tenacious as they want. Maybe the rest of Apple won’t even notice that that’s happening, because they’re busy making the next iPhone. Apple spent however many years and like $10 billion trying to make a car and it just didn’t work. It didn’t even occur to the rest of Apple that was happening. They just kept shipping phones and laptops and everything was just fine.OpenAI is not that company. OpenAI might not even have enough executives to absorb the cost of another distraction. They keep leaving. Greg Brockman, who’s the president of OpenAI, seems to be running the entire company himself. As you’ve been reporting on the story, has it occurred to OpenAI that they’re going to need more people to do more things? Because I look at that company and they just keep shedding all of their talent and pivoting, as you said, from one thing to the other and they’ve landed on “we need to compete with Anthropic.” But it doesn’t seem like they can afford distraction at this scale in any way, shape, or form.They can’t. This is like the final battle. Actually, there are going to be a lot more battles, but this is a key battle in the longstanding AI arms race. This is the IPO year. Things are coming to a head. Investors are looking very closely at these companies in a way they haven’t before. They’re looking for them to turn a profit, or at least have a way to turn a profit in a way that they have not really paid too much attention to before, and it’s all coming down to the line. They cannot afford a distraction right now.They are reshuffling, restructuring, changing executives around. When I’ve asked them about this, they will say, “Well, it’s all been one large plan that we’ve just been doing in little pieces. It’s not like we’ve pivoted a million times, we’ve always been going towards one thing and these are just different steps along that journey.” But it doesn’t always read that way to the general public.In some ways, yes, Greg Brockman is taking control of product. That’s because Fidji Simo, the AGI chief, took leave because of her health and then she left permanently because of her health. So, it’s not all things that the company could control, but I do think there have been a lot of defections even from the rank-and-file staff. They’ve got to get more people. They’ve got to hire more, which will require even more money, and it’s not the time to get distracted by anything.It’s worth noting that there’s some reporting saying OpenAI will delay its IPO. And as you and I are sitting here speaking, SpaceX’s stock price has dipped below its IPO number. These mega IPOs, they’re in some kind of danger. It might not be a good idea to do this, especially if you don’t have the revenue or, more importantly, the profits to back it up. Do you think there’s some risk that this lawsuit is the straw that pushes OpenAI to delay its IPO?Absolutely. Reporting showed that Sam Altman was originally worried about filing its Form S-1 before Anthropic. Then it didn’t do that. Anthropic did it first and then — and this is me just speculating — it seems like after Anthropic won that part of the race, OpenAI backed off and said, “Okay, well, should we actually do this right now? We were trying really hard to win, but let’s look at the facts here.” That’s why it said, “Okay, it might be early next year, it might not be this year.”That was before Apple’s lawsuit dropped. So now of course I’m sure OpenAI is thinking, “Okay, should we delay it even more? Especially because there’s no way we’re going to beat Anthropic to an IPO.” It seems like that might be a smart idea.If you can put out a great hardware device and make some big splashy profitable entry into the consumer market, that’s a good story to tell in an IPO. But if you can’t do that because everything you do is going to end up in litigation or in messy discovery on the way to litigation, maybe that all just has to be set to the side. That’s pretty much my last question here. I feel like my thesis on this show and on The Verge generally has been, “Okay, I understand how this is going to work for software development and I understand how this is going to work in enterprise, although even that’s pretty shaky still. But consumers don’t love this stuff.” That there are 900 million free ChatGPT users is not a reflection of anything other than, well, Google Search got really bad and this thing will tell you it loves you.But it hasn’t turned into, “We should build more data centers.” It hasn’t turned into, “We culturally love the products of AI.” It’s turned into, “Get this slop out of my face and we’re going to boo you at graduation and we’re going to come to the town board meetings and say we don’t want data centers.”There’s some huge gap there. Do you think OpenAI can close that gap? Because it has always been the company chasing the big consumer product vision, the big consumer market. If it can’t close the gap, it should just shut it down and compete with Anthropic for enterprise uses. I don’t know which one it’s going to be. Do you have a sense through all this reporting if OpenAI has realized how existential the consumer side of the debate is?I think they know to some extent, but not the full extent. tThey will get at it in speeches, in research papers, in tweets. They have some PR lines where they will acknowledge that this is a thing, but I just don’t think they realize how deeply this crevice goes. The gap is so wide. I don’t see them being the only company to close it — if it’s ever going to be closed. People are extremely worried about so many things when it comes to AI and they’re very emotional about it.It’s not just logic. I think if it was just a logical gap — some people logically think it’s not good, some people logically think it is good — I think they could probably close that. But when emotions come into play, no, I don’t think that they will be able to fully bridge that gap.For me, when I became an AI reporter six years ago, none of my friends really knew or cared what I wrote about. That was fine. It was really niche, whatever. Starting four years ago, it would come up at the bar, at the club. I’ll be walking down the street, someone’s asking me about it, and they don’t know I’m an AI reporter. So, when they learn I’m an AI reporter, it happens even more. But just randomly, this is coming up for people and making them extremely emotional. So yeah, I just don’t think OpenAI is going to be the company to bridge that gap.We’ll see what happens with the hardware of it all. A lot of people I’ve been seeing — TikTok creators, creators on other platforms — are trying to change the narrative here, saying, “Hey, don’t get left behind. Learn how to use this stuff.” And it’s something that can be valid, but the way that they’re framing it a lot of the time is more, “Oh, it’s such a high learning curve. You must learn how to use it before it leaves you behind.”It’s not that high of a learning curve, it depends on how you’re using it, but you know what I mean. Sometimes it is, yeah, if you’re using Codex and doing all sorts of intense vibe coding, yes, of course it is. But if you’re just chatting with a chatbot, it’s really not a high learning curve.I personally believe all that algorithmic social video content is an astroturfing campaign. That’s just me. That’s a conspiracy theory, we’re going to leave that aside. I do want to pitch you a video series called “Hayden in the Club,” where you just go to the club and talk to people about AI.[Laughs] I would love to do that officially instead of just having it happen to me unofficially all the time.It’s been a long time since I was in the club. That was not what the club was like when I was younger. Things have changed.Hayden, this has been great. I have a strong suspicion you’re going to be back on this show talking about this lawsuit and OpenAI for the rest of this year. We’re going to have to have you back very soon.Can’t wait. Thanks.Questions or comments? Hit us up at decoder@theverge.com. We really do read every email!Decoder with Nilay PatelA podcast from The Verge about big ideas and other problems.SUBSCRIBE NOW!
history.author: Nilay Patel
history.titleZh: 苹果诉OpenAI案的核心在于谁将定义后智能手机时代
history.descriptionZh:
苹果与OpenAI之间爆发了一场备受关注的商业机密诉讼案，其核心指控堪称惊人：苹果声称多名前员工在跳槽至OpenAI后，不仅在面试中系统性地索要苹果的硬件制造机密，甚至有人直接从苹果服务器下载相关文件。尽管OpenAI否认这些指控，且尚未提交正式法律回应，但此案已引发业界对OpenAI未来走向的深度担忧。

这起诉讼的背景极为特殊。苹果素以“顽强诉讼者”著称，曾先后在20世纪90年代起诉微软侵犯Mac外观版权，在2000年代至2010年代起诉三星侵犯iPhone专利（三星最终支付约10亿美元赔偿），但这些并未阻止竞争对手的崛起。如今，苹果将矛头指向了第三种知识产权——商业机密，目标正是OpenAI。然而，OpenAI的处境与当年的微软和三星截然不同：它尚未盈利，仍在大量烧钱，战略上摇摆不定（同时试图进军企业和消费者市场），高管频繁更迭。2025年，OpenAI耗资65亿美元收购了传奇苹果设计师乔纳森·艾夫的AI硬件初创公司io Products，而许多前苹果员工正是通过这次收购加入OpenAI，其中包括关键人物唐·谭。唐·谭曾在苹果工作24年，离职前是Apple Watch副总裁，后被OpenAI任命为首席硬件官。苹果指控他是此次商业机密窃取计划的“主谋”，他在面试中直接询问候选者关于苹果秘密代号项目的细节，甚至要求他们“将苹果办公室外的电池或硬件部件带出来，做一次‘展示和讲解’”。另一名员工据称在访问苹果机密信息后发送消息：“哈哈，我能访问这个，太有趣了。”

从技术原理层面看，此案的核心并非AI算法或模型，而是硬件制造know-how（技术诀窍）。AI硬件设备（如智能音箱、可穿戴设备）的制造极其困难，此前Humane AI Pin等项目已惨败。OpenAI要避免重蹈覆辙，必须获取顶尖硬件人才的经验。苹果的指控直接指向了这种“经验”的获取方式：通过面试和私下交流，让前员工利用自身对苹果内部项目的了解，将机密信息传递给新雇主。这本质上是一种利用行业“近亲繁殖”特性（AI行业人员流动频繁，关系盘根错节）进行的秘密情报收集。Jony Ive本人虽未被起诉（苹果或许因他地位太高或证据不足而谨慎），但他与Sam Altman合拍视频、宣称手机和笔记本电脑已成为“遗留设备”的姿态，被视为对苹果的直接挑衅——他正在打造下一代AI原生硬件。

性能参数与对比数据方面，案件涉及的金额和影响级联放大。苹果诉三星案中，三星支付了约10亿美元赔偿，但Android生态依然壮大。此次苹果若胜诉，OpenAI可能面临巨额赔偿和IPO重大阻碍。事实上，OpenAI的IPO计划已被传言可能推迟——此前它曾试图赶在Anthropic之前提交S-1文件，但未能成功；苹果诉讼的爆发进一步加大了风险。更关键的是，OpenAI财务状况紧张：它急需证明盈利路径，却同时面临经营现金以支撑硬件研发、高管流失（总裁Greg Brockman几乎独挑大梁，AGI主管Fidji Simo因病离职）等内部压力。苹果则拥有几乎无限的诉讼预算，可以“雇一批律师让他们去死缠烂打”，而苹果的其他业务（iPhone、Mac等）几乎不受影响——这与OpenAI的脆弱形成鲜明对比。

技术影响和应用场景的讨论更加广泛和深入。这起诉讼的象征意义远超个案本身：AI行业当前几乎建立在“未经授权的使用”基础上——训练数据是否属于合理使用仍悬而未决（涉及大量版权诉讼），而AI公司自己却对模型蒸馏（如中国AI公司蒸馏ChatGPT）极度愤怒（写信给政府、联合“死敌”共同抵制），却从不将此感受类比于艺术家和创作者对它们窃取数据的愤怒。苹果的起诉正是这种矛盾的集中体现：AI公司一边要求别人保护自己的知识产权，一边肆意拿取他人的机密。消费者层面，AI硬件市场面临巨大信任危机——大学生在毕业典礼上嘘AI、社区委员会抵制数据中心、死亡威胁AI CEO等事件频发。即便是ChatGPT的9亿免费用户，也更多源于Google搜索变差和AI的“情绪价值”而非真正的产品热爱。OpenAI五年后更糟糕：它已多次放弃内部项目（Sora视频生成、Atlas、AI约会功能等），最近才聚焦于企业和编程两大核心收入来源，但硬件梦想（5款设备，首款是无屏幕智能音箱）仍无法舍弃——因为已投入巨资。然而，消费者是否愿意为另一个“智能音箱”买单？即使Jony Ive主导设计，AI仍存在根本性问题：幻觉未除（测试显示60%的地址查询错误）、无法承担高任务、用户不愿将个人数据（如Gmail）交给它。且硬件需要像iPhone那样具备社交网络效应（“如果朋友没有，你就被排除在群聊之外”），但目前看不到这种可能性。

最终，这起诉讼可能成为OpenAI的“最后一根稻草”：它将迫使OpenAI在IPO前夜分心应付漫长而昂贵的法律战，同时考验其能否在消费者和企业市场之间做出真正抉择。苹果不只是在保护自己的硬件秘密，更是在利用法律手段延缓最大消费级AI竞争对手的步伐，并配合自己的Apple Intelligence追赶计划（苹果被认为在AI上严重落后，甚至不得不借用Google的Gemini模型）。对OpenAI而言，它既无法像三星那样轻松支付10亿美元罚款（因为利润率不支持），也无法像苹果那样让诉讼成为“背景噪音”（因为高管层本身就在动荡）。这起案件，很可能成为定义AI行业知识产权规则、以及OpenAI能否从“野蛮生长”转型为可持续企业的重要转折点。

history.title: Intel and AMD sign long-term server CPU deals with Chinese customers as prices jump over 40%, report claims — agreements purportedly guarantee purchase volumes for about a year without fixing prices
history.description: Some customers have discussed commitments running two years or longer, one source told Reuters.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Intel and AMD are signing longer-term purchase commitments with Chinese server customers for data center processors, according to a new Reuters report that cites two people familiar with the talks. Prices for some server CPU products in China have climbed more than 40% since the start of the year and are still rising by over 10% month-on-month in some cases, one of the people said. Most of the agreements guarantee purchase volumes for about a year without fixing prices, and neither company responded to the publication's requests for comment.Go deeper with TH Premium: CPU (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)CPU scaling with DLSSRyzen to the top: How AMD innovated in the gaming CPU marketHow ARM is working its way into PCsAMD CES 2026 gaming trends press Q&A roundtable transcriptSome customers have discussed commitments running two years or longer, one of the people told Reuters, with the deals covering unit volumes only, which leaves Chinese cloud providers and internet companies fully exposed to a market that has already added 40% to some CPU prices in under seven months. Memory makers struck similar long-term agreements with hyperscalers over the past year as AI demand consumed DRAM and NAND output, but those contracts typically trade a volume commitment for some pricing visibility. Buyers here get neither price protection nor, based on current lead times, fast delivery, but they do get a place in the queue.Intel said in March that it was pursuing long-term agreements with potential customers as server CPU demand spiked, with CEO Lip-Bu Tan telling analysts on the company's April earnings call that demand "continues to run ahead of supply," singling out Xeon server parts. Tan also cited a multi-year supply deal with Google among several long-term contracts signed in the first quarter, so the model now spans U.S. hyperscalers and Chinese channel buyers alike.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Reuters first reported in February that Intel had warned Chinese customers of lead times stretching to six months on some server CPUs, with AMD quoting eight to 10 weeks. By late March, server makers told Nikkei Asia that average CPU lead times had blown out from one to two weeks to eight to 12 weeks and beyond. Intel CFO David Zinsner put a value on that in April, telling investors that unmet Xeon demand "starts with a B," and the company later confirmed price hikes on select consumer and server CPUs.Agentic AI workloads are driving much of the demand, as inference and orchestration tasks pull server CPU-to-GPU ratios back toward parity. AMD, which is due to report earnings in early August, has raised its server CPU market forecast to more than $120 billion by 2030, based on the same trend. You may like CPU requirements for AI workloads are multiplying, driving intensifying shortages and price hikes Intel confirms price hikes on select consumer and server CPUs citing supply costs and demand Intel tells PC makers to adopt 18A CPUs or lose their supply, report claims Intel will report its second-quarter results later today, where the shortage and the durability of these commitments are likely to come up. Chinese buyers remain cut off from the most advanced AI accelerators under U.S. export controls, but Xeon and EPYC server CPUs carry no such restrictions, making them one of the few U.S.-made AI infrastructure components China can still purchase freely, if it can get them. Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors
history.author: Luke James
history.titleZh: 报告称英特尔和AMD与中国客户签署长期服务器CPU协议，价格涨幅超40%——据称协议保证约一年购买量但不固定价格
history.descriptionZh:
英特尔和AMD正与中国服务器客户就数据中心处理器签署期限更长的采购承诺。据路透社报道援引两位知情人士消息，自今年年初以来，中国市场上部分服务器CPU产品价格已上涨超过40%，在某些情况下月环比涨幅仍超过10%。这些协议大多承诺约一年的采购量，但不锁定价格，且两家公司均未回应媒体的置评请求。

部分客户已讨论过长达两年或更久的承诺，但这些交易仅覆盖数量单位，这意味着中国云服务提供商和互联网企业完全暴露在已涨价40%的市场中——这一涨幅发生在不到七个月内。此前，内存制造商也在过去一年与超大规模云厂商签署了类似的长期协议，以AI需求消耗DRAM和NAND产能为背景，但那些合同通常以数量承诺换取一定的价格可见性。而此次CPU买家既没有价格保护，也没有基于当前交付周期的快速交货保障，他们得到的只是排队资格。

英特尔在3月表示，随着服务器CPU需求激增，公司正在寻求与潜在客户达成长期协议。CEO陈立武在4月财报电话会议上告诉分析师，需求“持续超过供给”，并特别提到了至强服务器部件。他还提到第一季度签署的多个长期合同，其中包括与谷歌的多年供应协议，因此这种模式现已覆盖美国超大规模云厂商和中国渠道买家。

路透曾在2月报道，英特尔已告知中国客户部分服务器CPU的交付周期延长至六个月，而AMD的交付周期则为八到十周。到3月底，服务器制造商向《日经亚洲》透露，平均CPU交付周期已从一到两周飙升至八到十二周甚至更长。英特尔CFO大卫·津斯纳在4月向投资者表示，未满足的至强需求“以B（十亿）计”，公司随后确认了对部分消费级和服务器CPU的涨价。

推动这一需求的主要因素是智能体AI工作负载——推理和编排任务使服务器CPU与GPU的比例向平衡回归。AMD预计于8月初发布财报，基于相同趋势，已将服务器CPU市场预测上调至2030年超过1200亿美元。AI工作负载对CPU的需求正在倍增，导致短缺加剧和价格飙升。

中国买家在美国出口管制下被禁止获得最先进的AI加速器，但至强和EPYC服务器CPU不受此类限制，因此它们成为中国仍可自由购买（只要能够获取）的少数美国产AI基础设施组件之一。英特尔将于今日晚些时候公布第二季度业绩，届时短缺问题以及这些承诺的持久性很可能会被提及。

history.title: Arctic Senza AI 370 review: Strix Point in a stealthy under-desk fanless design
history.description: The Arctic Senza AI 370 is a fanless mini PC designed to mount under your desk. Everyday performance isn’t held back by the lack of active cooling, and its soldered dual-channel 32GB LPDDR5X-8000 is appreciated during the RAM shortages. The meager front panel I/O is the only major drawback to the design.
history.content: Arctic's latest under-the-desk Senza PC design is using an AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 mobile processor. Sure, the upgrade has also allowed Arctic to shove ‘AI’ in the product name, but this is a very appealing design with great build quality, good performance, a reasonable selection of ports, and silent operation as the cherry on top.Design of the Arctic Senza AI 370 Image 1 of 6 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 6 View Original Image 2 of 6 View Original Image 3 of 6 View Original Image 4 of 6 View Original Image 5 of 6 View Original Image 6 of 6 View Original This definitely isn’t the smallest mini PC around. Its dimensions could probably fit more like three mini PCs in a row. The largest part of this device measures 21.10 (L) × 7.09 (W) × 1.97 (H) inches. In addition to the sizable main unit, there’s also a 120W external PSU and a front panel dongle with some handy ports and an LED-illuminated power button. Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original The central section of the main unit is where all the performance components are located. Its ‘wings’ are basically passive cooling radiators with heatpipes spreading out from the center, perforating a multitude of fins on either side.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Image 1 of 4 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 4 View Original Image 2 of 4 View Original Image 3 of 4 View Original Image 4 of 4 View Original The Senza, its front panel dongle, and PSU are designed to mount discreetly under your desk. After confirming that everything worked post-unpacking, I dutifully followed the instructions and used the printed template to mount the PC and front panel neatly beneath my oak desk. My desk is so heavy I had to work on it as if I were working under a car, lying on my back, but it slotted into place the first time thanks to the accuracy of the template and my deft use of an awl. All the fittings (screws, fabric fasteners, braces, and straps) are provided in the box with the Senza AI 370.Arctic Senza AI 370 specificationsSwipe to scroll horizontallyProcessorAMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 GraphicsAMD Radeon 890M (RDNA 3 iGPU)RAM32GB LPDDR5X‑8000 dual‑channel (soldered)Storage1TB PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD (M.2 2280, TLC)Physical Ports2x USB4 Type‑C (40 Gbps); 2x USB‑A 3.2 Gen 2 (10Gbps); 1x USB‑A 3.2 Gen 1 (5Gbps); 2x USB‑A 2.0; HDMI 2.1 ; DisplayPort 2.0 ; 3.5 mm audio jacks; 2.5Gb LAN; DC‑in (120W)WirelessWi‑Fi 7 (2x2 MIMO) + Bluetooth 5.3Other FeaturesFanless passive cooling; detachable front panel; under‑desk mount kit; cable sleeve; TPM 2.0DimensionsMain unit: 21.10 x 7.09 x 1.97 inches (536 x 180 x 50mm); Front panel: 3.76 x 2.22 x 0.63 inches (95.6 x 56.3 x 16mm); System weight: 5.36 pounds (2.43kg)OS InstalledWindows 11 Pro (Linux compatible)Price (as configured)€1,199.99, 2-year warrantyPorts and UpgradeabilityOn the PC back panel, there is a decent selection of ports. The mix includes a USB4 Type-C port, two USB 2.0 Type-A, and two USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-A ports. Dedicated video out options are provided with HDMI 2.1, and DisplayPort 2.0 - but the rear USB4 port can also handle video out, too. Two 3.5mm jacks are also there for audio in and out. Wired networking support is provided with a 2.5G LAN port. Image 1 of 3 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 3 View Original Image 2 of 3 View Original Image 3 of 3 View Original The rear is also where you plug in the supplied 120W PSU via a barrel jack. Lastly, there’s an unusual looking dedicated dual USB-C (two Type-C ports positioned at right angles to one another), which mates with the front panel I/O dongle cable supplying USB4 data and power.The use of a front panel I/O dongle is a great idea, and it makes thepower button easy to reach. I would have liked to see more ports, here, personally.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)As I am always plugging various peripherals into my PCs, I decided to connect the under-desk USB4 port on the main body of the Arctic Senza AI 370 to an above-desk dock. Specifically, I used StarTech’s driverless 208UE USB4 dock to give easy access to a host of fast connectivity options.Inside the Senza AI 370, there is quite a lot of empty space but limited expandability. It’s easy enough to get in and see the guts of the device, with just four Phillips screws holding the metal panel in place. Under the panel there are no RAM slots, but you can see the soldered Micron DDR5-8000 chips surrounding the AMD processor. Before you buy, ensure 32GB of RAM will be enough for you for the foreseeable future.I also found an Arctic heatsink atop an M.2 2280 slot. The sample device came with 1TB of fast PCIe 4.0 NVMe storage installed. System tools revealed it to be a Chinese YMTC PC411 SSD.We also spied an M.2 2230 slot populated with a Mediatek Wi-Fi 7 (2x2 MIMO) + Bluetooth 5.3 card. You could swap that out for repair or upgrade purposes one day, if required. There are no further internal expansion ports.Test System SetupSwipe to scroll horizontallySystemCPU(i)GPUMemoryChassisGMK EVO-T2Intel Core Ultra X7 358H (16C/16T: 4P + 8E + 4 LPE), up to 4.8 GHzIntel Arc B390 (Xe3, 12 cores, up to 2.5 GHz)2x 32GB LPDDR5X-8533 solderedDual-fan,1.72 litersMSI Cubi NUC AI+ 3MGIntel Core Ultra 9 386H (16C/16T: 6P + 8E + 2LPE), up to 4.9 GHzIntel Graphics (Xe3, 4 Cores, up to 2.5 GHz)2x 16GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.52 litersGeekom A9 Max 2026AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.2 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 3.1 MHz)1x 32GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.82 litersArctic Senza AI 370AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.1 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 2.9 MHz)2x 16GB LPDDR5X‑8000 solderedPassive, 4.82 litersBeelink SER10 MAXAMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 (12C / 24T: 4x Zen 5 , 8x Zen 5c), up to 5.2 GHzRadeon 890M (RDNA 3.5, 16 CUs, up to 3.1 MHz)2x 32GB DDR5-5600 SodimmSingle fan, 0.81 litersProductivity Performance on the Arctic Senza AI 370The Arctic Senza AI 370 doesn’t break a sweat with day-to-day productivity tasks, browsing, and similarly light workloads. AMD’s Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 is a very capable chip with its Zen 5 CPU architecture featuring 12 cores and 24 threads, the integrated Radeon 890M graphics, and a power-efficient NPU for on-device AI. Image 1 of 8 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 8 View Original Image 2 of 8 View Original Image 3 of 8 View Original Image 4 of 8 View Original Image 5 of 8 View Original Image 6 of 8 View Original Image 7 of 8 View Original Image 8 of 8 View Original Image 1 of 11 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 11 View Original Image 2 of 11 View Original Image 3 of 11 View Original Image 4 of 11 View Original Image 5 of 11 View Original Image 6 of 11 View Original Image 7 of 11 View Original Image 8 of 11 View Original Image 9 of 11 View Original Image 10 of 11 View Original Image 11 of 11 View Original Despite relying on an older AMD processor, the Arctic Senza AI 370 shows it can match and even beat the two Ryzen 9 AI HX 470 mini PCs that have also recently been subjected to our newest suite of tests. Let’s single out Cinebench 2026 multicore scores first. Here the Senza clearly leads the pack of newer AMD Ryzen and Intel Core Ultra mini PCs, at almost 7% faster than the second-placed Beelink SER 10 Max. In the Blender Monster render test, the Senza’s HX 470 rivals actually do win by a tiny amount, but the difference is small enough to be within the margin of error.Another productivity test, targeting video encoding performance this time, shows that the Arctic won’t be usurped by its newer generation challengers. The Senza AI 370 proudly leads the Handbrake table, and a victory is a victory, but the actual difference is so small it is essentially a draw between the three AMD mini PCs.I think the Arctic’s good performance in these productivity tests can be put down to the system’s significantly better memory bandwidth.Gaming and Graphics on the Arctic Senza AI 370The Senza AI 370’s APU should make it a decent enough performer in games, at least if you're realistic with the settings. Its Radeon 890M iGPU comes with 16 RDNA 3.5 CUs, and it is accompanied by fast dual-channel DDR5-8000 in Arctic’s silent machine.Before moving onto our standard synthetic and iconic PC gaming tests, I decided to run around in one of my favorite 3D gaming titles, Ubisoft’s The Division 2. I enjoyed taking down a Bloodhound and its high-tech escort troops, and liberating an enemy control point. The action was fluid enough, without experiencing any stutters or other low-performance tells. I played in FHD using the High preset, and later saw using the built-in benchmark tool that the Arctic Senza AI 370 achieved an average of 53 FPS across a diverse set of game scenarios. Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original Next up, our benchmarks include a trio of 3DMark synthetics and two PC gaming standards. 3DMark Solar Bay is a great way to frame and compare cross-platform iGPU performance. Meanwhile, Steel Nomad turns to screws on raw graphics performance with its heavy modern GPU load, and will be better for comparisons when we start testing the higher-end mini PCs, some with dGPUs. Last and definitely not least, Speed Way is a 2025 benchmark from UL that uses DirectX Raytracing tier 1.1 for real-time global illumination and real-time ray-traced reflections.In 3DMark Solar Bay, which uses an engine / resolution most appropriate to the graphical firepower of most of this crop of iGPU-only mini PCs, the Senza AI 370 easily beats the newer-gen Ryzen competition. However, it is clearly outclassed by the Intel Arc B390 iGPU in the GMKtec EVO-T2.Moving on to more real games, let’s check the data provided by the multi-scene benchmarks built into Shadow of the Tomb Raider and Cyberpunk 2077. The former scales well with processor resources and can still often look and run great on humble iGPUs. As for Cyberpunk 2077, it can really push and tax even the strongest modern GPUs, so here we've used two settings to show how the silent Senza can perform. Image 1 of 6 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 6 View Original Image 2 of 6 View Original Image 3 of 6 View Original Image 4 of 6 View Original Image 5 of 6 View Original Image 6 of 6 View Original A pattern seems to be established in the synthetics above, which continues into our real gaming tests. Specifically, the passively cooled Arctic Senza AI 370 is the best choice for iGPU gaming if you don’t want to spend far more cash on the potent Panther Lake GMKtec EVO-T2. The Arctic consistently outclasses both the newer HX 470 processor-based challengers, though we see the Beelink SER10 Max with dual-channel RAM do much better than the Geekom A9 Max 2026, constrained by a single stick of DDR5-5600.On Cyberpunk 2077, the Steam Deck and 1080p preset felt noticeably more fluid than the High 1080p option. If I were to persevere with this demanding title on the Senza AI 370, I’d perhaps try some form of upscaling to see if I could maintain the High quality setting but with an improved FPS.It's worth repeating, the silent Senza seems to outclass the newer AMD challengers across gaming tests thanks to its superior memory subsystem based on its soldered 32GB LPDDR5X‑8000 dual‑channel RAM.Power, Heat, and Noise when using the Arctic Senza AI 370 Image 1 of 2 (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) (Image credit: Tom's Hardware) Image 1 of 2 View Original Image 2 of 2 View Original When I left the Senza AI 370 to idle, my power meter measured wattages at or below 10W from the wall for the whole system. When the system was pushed with simultaneous CPU and GPU benchmarks running, power draw seemed to peak at a little under 92W.System tools showed that during light activities like browsing the web and typing reports like this one, the CPU temperature stayed around 30 degrees C. Playing The Division 2 for 30 or 40 minutes, the reported CPU temperature seemed to stay between 60 C and 70 C. It didn’t constantly creep upwards; it would sometimes go towards that upper bound, and sometimes fall again. However, running a workload like Cinebench R23 multicore the temperatures seemed to creep inexorably upwards, with the highest measurement I saw at 96 C.Between tasks, the reported CPU temperature would fall very rapidly. I saw it dive from 75 C to 45 C in under 10 seconds after one of the recent benchmarks I completed. That’s pretty impressive given that there’s no fan.In my testing before installing it under the desk, oddly enough, I could actually hear a sound when the YMTC PC411 SSD was being accessed. I'm unsure what it was, but I did notice it.Software and Warranty on the Arctic Senza AI 370Arctic’s Senza AI 370 ships with a clean copy of Windows 11 Pro installed. That's it. There were no bundled utilities, dashboards, or custom monitoring and tuning software. Arctic sells the Senza AI 370 with a two-year warranty.Configurations of the Arctic Senza AI 370Arctic sells just a single Senza AI 370 configuration: the one we tested with a AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370,32GB LPDDR5X-8000 memory and a 1TB SSD. Windows 11 Pro is included, but Arctic says that the machine is Linux compatible.Those in Europe can head directly to the official company website and snag a Senza AI 370 for the MSRP of €1,199.99. Meanwhile, UK buyers can grab a device from Arctic’s official Amazon web store for £910.49. Compared with many of the other mini PC prices we are seeing, that seems very reasonable for a fully populated mini PC with fast memory and storage plus unique silent / passive appeal. Those German and UK prices include VAT at 19% and 20%, respectively.I asked Arctic about the availability of the Senza AI 370 in the U.S. and was told that no official distributor had been set up for this device. The company is seeding the device to U.S. media, however, to gauge interest in devices like this.Bottom Line (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)If you want a mini PC that you can't see or hear, the Arctic Senza AI 370 is for you. Many will be attracted by mini PCs for their minimal physical footprint, but Combine that with its silence, well-behaved thermals, plus solid performance, and there's really not much to complain about.Concerning performance, Arctic’s choice of soldered 32GB LPDDR5X-8000 memory and a fast SSD gave this device equal or better application and gamingi performance compared to its newer-gen Ryzen 9 AI 470 HX equipped mini PC rivals we have tested recently. It is noticeably cheaper, too, but sadly not easy to grab if you live stateside right now.Arctic Senza AI 370: Price Comparison No price informationCheck Amazon No price informationCheck Best BuyWe check over 250 million products every day for the best pricespowered by
history.author: Mark Tyson
history.titleZh: Arctic Senza AI 370 评测：采用隐蔽式桌面下无风扇设计的Strix Point
history.descriptionZh:
Arctic 最新推出的桌下安装式 Senza AI 370 迷你 PC 采用了 AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 移动处理器。虽然升级让 Arctic 顺势在名称中加入了“AI”，但这款产品真正的亮点在于其出色的设计、扎实的做工、稳定的性能、合理的接口配置，以及最吸引人的——完全无风扇的静音运行。

**设计与核心技术创新**

这款设备并非传统意义上的超小型迷你 PC，其尺寸相当可观。主机部分长 21.10 英寸（约 536mm）、宽 7.09 英寸（约 180mm）、厚 1.97 英寸（约 50mm），体积约为 4.82 升。此外，还配备了一个 120W 的外置电源适配器和一个可分离的前面板连接器（上面带有一些实用接口和带 LED 灯光的电源按钮）。主机中央区域是核心性能部件，两侧的“翅膀”实际上是大型被动散热鳍片，通过热管从中央延伸而出，将热量传导至两侧密布的散热鳍片上。这套无风扇被动散热系统是 Arctic 的核心创新，实现了完全静音运行。

整机设计为可隐蔽地安装在桌子下方。Arctic 在包装内提供了完整的安装套件（包括螺丝、织物固定件、支架和绑带）以及精确的安装模板，用户可按照指示轻松将主机和前面板固定在桌底，安装过程如同在车下工作一般，但模板的精准度确保了首次安装即可完美就位。

**性能参数与接口配置**

Arctic Senza AI 370 的核心配置为：AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 处理器（12核24线程：4个Zen 5核心 + 8个Zen 5c核心，最高频率5.1GHz），集成 Radeon 890M 显卡（RDNA 3.5 架构，16个计算单元，最高频率2.9GHz），32GB LPDDR5X-8000 双通道内存（板载焊接，不可扩展），1TB PCIe 4.0 NVMe M.2 2280 SSD（实测为国产YMTC PC411），以及 MediaTek Wi-Fi 7 无线网卡和蓝牙 5.3。系统预装 Windows 11 Pro，同时兼容 Linux。

接口方面，主机背部提供了：1个USB4 Type-C（40Gbps，支持视频输出）、2个USB 2.0 Type-A、2个USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-A（10Gbps）、HDMI 2.1、DisplayPort 2.0、3.5mm音频输入输出接口、2.5Gbps网口，以及DC电源输入接口。前面板连接器通过一根特殊设计的双USB-C线缆（两个Type-C端口成直角排列）与主机相连，提供USB4数据和供电。前面板本身提供了额外的USB端口和电源按钮，极大方便了日常操作。用户也可将主机上的USB4端口连接至桌面扩展坞（如StarTech的208UE USB4免驱扩展坞）以获得更多高速接口。

**性能测试与对比分析**

在生产力测试中，Arctic Senza AI 370 表现优异，甚至能够击败搭载更新的 Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 处理器的竞品（如 Beelink SER10 Max 和 Geekom A9 Max 2026）。在 Cinebench 2026 多核测试中，Senza 领先第二名 Beelink SER10 Max 约 7%；在 Blender Monster 渲染和 Handbrake 视频编码测试中，虽然与 HX 470 机型差距极小（近乎误差范围内），但依然位居前列。这种优势主要归功于其采用的板载 LPDDR5X-8000 双通道内存带来的更高内存带宽。

在游戏性能方面，Senza AI 370 同样表现不俗。在《全境封锁2》中以1080p高画质运行，内置基准测试平均帧率可达 53 FPS，全程流畅无卡顿。在 3DMark Solar Bay 跨平台 iGPU 测试中，Senza 轻松击败了新一代 Ryzen 机型；在《古墓丽影：暗影》和《赛博朋克2077》等真实游戏测试中，其表现同样优于 HX 470 竞品（但被搭载 Intel Arc B390 的 GMKtec EVO-T2 超越）。在《赛博朋克2077》中，以 Steam Deck 预设（约1080p低画质）运行时，游戏流畅度明显优于高画质预设。重复测试表明，其稳定的游戏性能优势再次源自高速双通道内存子系统。

**功耗、散热与噪声**

在闲置状态下，整机功耗低于10W；同时运行CPU和GPU烤机时，峰值功耗低于92W。轻负载（如网页浏览）下CPU温度维持在30°C左右；运行《全境封锁2》30-40分钟后，CPU温度在60°C-70°C之间波动；运行Cinebench R23多核测试时，温度缓慢攀升至最高96°C。任务停止后，温度下降迅速——例如从75°C降至45°C仅需不到10秒，鉴于无风扇设计，这一热表现令人印象深刻。测试中唯一可察觉的噪声来自SSD读写时的轻微声音，但整体上机器完全静音。

**软件与质保**

Arctic 为该设备提供了干净的 Windows 11 Pro 系统，未预装任何多余的监控或调优软件。保修期为两年。

**定价与市场可用性**

目前 Arctic Senza AI 370 仅提供单一配置（Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 + 32GB + 1TB），欧洲官网建议零售价为 1,199.99 欧元（含19%增值税），英国亚马逊售价 910.49 英镑（含20%增值税）。相较于其他同级别迷你PC，这一价格非常合理，尤其考虑到其静音和被动散热特性。遗憾的是，该产品目前尚未在美国设立官方分销渠道，Arctic 正在通过向媒体提供样机来评估美国市场需求。

**总结与影响**

Arctic Senza AI 370 是一款专为追求“看不见也听不见”的用户设计的迷你 PC。它将紧凑的桌下安装、完全无风扇的静音运行、良好的散热控制以及稳定的日常与游戏性能融为一体，且价格合理。通过采用高速板载内存，它在多项测试中甚至超越更新的Ryzen 9 AI HX 470竞品。主要不足在于内存和存储无法后续升级（内存为焊接，SSD为M.2 2280可换但无额外插槽），且目前在美国购买不便。对于欧洲用户而言，这是一款极具吸引力的静音办公与轻度游戏解决方案，尤其适合对噪音敏感的环境（如卧室、图书馆或开放式办公室）。其成功展示了被动散热与高性能移动处理器的结合潜力，为未来桌面计算形态提供了新的思路。

history.title: The right-wing boomers protesting data centers have a lot in common with the left
history.description:
A person holds signs during a nationwide protest against AI data center expansion outside Peace Hall in New Port Richey, Florida. | AFP via Getty Images	

On a gray, humid Saturday morning in central Florida, a little under a dozen people gathered outside the Spring Hill Branch Library to protest the construction of a hyperscale data center in their community. There was no immediate threat - the Hernando County commission had unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on such developments in June - but the organizers weren't satisfied. A temporary pause wouldn't be enough. They wanted a ban.
"We are going to fight, fight, fight," said a 71-year-old woman named Carol, who was manning a table with flyers and a petition the group, No Data Centers Hernando County, planned on presenting to the loc …
Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: On a gray, humid Saturday morning in central Florida, a little under a dozen people gathered outside the Spring Hill Branch Library to protest the construction of a hyperscale data center in their community. There was no immediate threat — the Hernando County commission had unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on such developments in June — but the organizers weren’t satisfied. A temporary pause wouldn’t be enough. They wanted a ban.“We are going to fight, fight, fight,” said a 71-year-old woman named Carol, who was manning a table with flyers and a petition the group, No Data Centers Hernando County, planned on presenting to the local government. Carol, a retired country music radio host, is a staunch conservative and a proud Floridian. The American flag on her red T-shirt was made up of little sea turtles. A white visor, bedazzled with red and blue rhinestones, shielded her face from the late-morning sun. “How would you like to have one by you? If you live within 15 minutes of a data center, night and day you’re going to hear: EEEEENNNNNNHHHHHHHHHHHH.”Beyond the noise pollution and environmental destruction — the frequencies emitted by data centers, she told me, hurt bees and other pollinators — Carol was concerned about the social cost of mass AI adoption. She told me about a woman she knows who cut off all her friends because “ChatGPT told her to.”“She consults with ChatGPT what time she should wake up in the morning, what time she should go to bed, who should be her friends,” she said.RelatedThe fight against AI data centers is just beginningNew York becomes the first state to enact a data center moratoriumThe biggest data center ever is becoming a huge problem in UtahThe small rally in this Tampa exurb was one of nearly 150 protests affiliated with Humans First, which describes itself as a “conservative social movement that is dedicated to ensuring that the future of AI is in the hands of everyday people.”Carol recited a litany of harms caused by data centers, some of them more dubious than others. Cows raised on ranches near data centers, she claimed, were giving birth to stillborn calves. Hyperscale facilities source their parts from China, an American adversary, threatening national security. “We’re setting ourselves up,” Carol told me. “Do you know what’s in those chips?” I told her I do not. “We’re blindly trusting the Chinese?” she continued, incredulous. “To give us chips?” (Conservative proponents of data centers, meanwhile, claim China is funding anti-AI campaigns in the United States.)Communities across the country are pushing back on data center construction. A recent Gallup poll found that seven in 10 Americans oppose construction of data centers in their area. Nearly two-thirds of Republicans and 75 percent of Democrats surveyed said they objected to local data center construction. Americans may be more polarized than ever, but the backlash to data centers is bipartisan — and it may cement new, surprising political alliances. The concerns animating the conservative backlash are similar to those motivating liberals: the environmental costs of data center construction and maintenance; the ethical problems of AI, ranging from its use for mass government surveillance to its effects on users’ mental health; and fears that having a data center nearby will hurt their quality of life. But there are also some partisan anxieties behind the right-wing data center backlash, namely the threat of Chinese infiltration of US technology.Humans First, a once-bipartisan offshoot of the Center for AI Safety, is helmed by Amy Kremer, a longtime conservative organizer who is arguably one of the people most responsible for the Republican Party’s populist turn. Kremer was an early leader of the Tea Party and a cofounder of both Women for Trump and Women for America First, the latter of which hosted the Stop the Steal rally in Washington, DC, that devolved into the January 6th riots. (Kremer was not among those who stormed the Capitol.)Despite Humans First’s partisan bent and Kremer’s political bona fides, the concerns at the Humans First-affiliated rally in Hernando County were decidedly local. A firefighter running for agricultural commissioner dropped off signs; a Republican candidate for county commissioner stopped by. The gathering ended with a lecture from a land use expert who explained how to change zoning codes to prevent the construction of hyperscale data centers — those that occupy at least 10,000 square feet and have a minimum energy draw of 100 megawatts. Before the lecture, No Data Center Hernando County member Kim Belanger, who works in IT, pulled up an article on data centers from the progressive website More Perfect Union. Even though the room skewed conservative, the article, she said, was “very informative.”Patti Zirhut, the organizer, told me that she found out about Humans First on Facebook. Participating in the national day of action was a no-brainer, since Zirhut and her friends had already started No Data Center Hernando County to thwart data center construction in their community.“I am very conservative and I don’t believe in all the stuff that Democrats believe in at all,” Zirhut told me. But she also feels betrayed by Florida’s Republican leadership. She was a big fan of Gov. Ron DeSantis — at first. But in his second term, she said, “he’s been nothing but pro-development, just inviting everybody here, tearing up our state.”DeSantis has positioned himself as an AI skeptic and signed a law in May requiring hyperscale data centers to cover their own utility costs and letting local governments set water use regulations for data center projects. But his administration has given data center developers millions in tax breaks, according to the Tampa Bay Times. The governor reportedly pushed to alter the state’s tax exemption for data centers so it would only apply to hyperscale facilities.As they tabled outside the library, Zirhut and her friends discussed DeSantis’ prospective successors. “This is probably the most important governor’s race of your lifetime,” Belanger told me. She’s also concerned about a DeSantis-backed ballot amendment that would eliminate property taxes for most homeowners, since it would deprive county governments of much-needed revenue — potentially making developments like data centers more enticing.“If Byron Donalds wins the primary, I’m voting Democrat. I will not vote for him.”Zirhut and the others were disappointed by Rep. Byron Donalds, the Republican front-runner in the gubernatorial primary and a vocal supporter of data center construction in Florida. “I just don’t understand why Trump would back him,” one of them said.“I’m not anti-data center. I’m pro having a plan,” Donalds said in February. “Data centers are going to be a function of American life going forward.” Donalds has said he wants to bring more outside investment to Florida — a comment that has since come back to haunt him. “My vision is to make Florida the financial capital of the world,” he said at an event hosted by The Hill.“That is not what we want here in Florida at all,” Zirhut told me.Donalds’ support for data centers is now costing him supporters from his own party. Recent attack ads have focused on the support he’s received from the tech industry, including more than $5 million in donations from Leading the Future, a $100 million pro-AI super PAC whose funders include Andreessen Horowitz and Joe Lonsdale of Palantir, according to the Tallahassee Democrat, as well as $500,000 from American Mission, which is itself funded by Leading the Future. Donalds appears to be staking out a middle ground between the industry that supports him and the voters whose support he needs to win the primary. On Monday, he announced legislation that would prevent data centers from driving up utility costs.Opposition to data centers is widespread throughout the state. More than 20 counties and municipalities throughout the state have discussed or passed moratoria on data center construction, Politico reports. In some communities, the backlash has been strong enough to deter developers. In June, the sponsor behind a controversial data center proposal in Citrus County withdrew its plan to build an 800-acre data center in the community after the county commission approved a one-year moratorium on rezoning applications for data centers.“Unfortunately, local moratoriums on data centers would send a signal that the area is closed for business, both for data centers and for other significant economic development projects,” Dan Diorio, executive vice president of state policy and government affairs at the Data Center Coalition, an industry lobbying group, told The Verge. But that’s exactly what Zirhut and other organizers want.Zirhut explained that she’s not anti-growth, but she doesn’t want the government to give handouts to major developers who have no ties to the community. “If we could keep it local, with local builders, that would be fine because they know what to do with Florida,” she said. “Even our agriculture commissioner” — Wilton Simpson, a Republican — “is having people come in and use herbicides and pesticides, spraying our lakes and killing all our vegetation.” She’s concerned about how AI can fuel mass surveillance, an issue that became especially salient after the Hernando County Sheriff’s Office started installing them on local roads.“We don’t want people to lose jobs, and our country to be run by AI, and live like people in China, where our money is controlled and everything we do is looked at,” she said.Though Zirhut is a lifelong Republican, the data center issue — and the bigger question of development — has led to some unexpected political compromises for her.“If Byron Donalds wins the primary, I’m voting Democrat. I will not vote for him,” she told me, adding that she’d also be willing to vote for an independent candidate who opposed data centers. “I think a lot of people are like that. It’s not about the party. It’s not. It’s about human life.”
history.author: Gaby Del Valle
history.titleZh: 抗议数据中心的右翼婴儿潮一代与左派有许多共同之处
history.descriptionZh: 一个潮湿阴沉的周六早晨，在佛罗里达州中部，不到十二个人聚集在春山图书馆分馆外，抗议在他们社区建造超大规模数据中心。尽管没有立即威胁——赫南多县委员会已于6月全票通过了为期一年的此类项目暂停令——但组织者并不满足。临时暂停还不够，他们想要彻底禁止。“我们要战斗、战斗、战斗，”71岁的卡罗尔说，她正守着一张摆满传单和请愿书的桌子，这些请愿书将由“赫南多县无数据中心”团体提交给地方政府。卡罗尔是一名退休乡村音乐电台主持人，坚定的保守派，自豪的佛罗里达人。她红色T恤上的美国国旗由小海龟图案组成，一顶镶有红蓝水钻的白色遮阳帽为她遮挡着上午晚些时候的阳光。“你会希望家旁边有一个数据中心吗？如果你住在数据中心15分钟车程内，日日夜夜你都会听到：EEEEEENNNNNNHHHHHHHHHHHH。”除了噪音污染和环境破坏——她告诉我数据中心发出的频率会伤害蜜蜂和其他传粉昆虫——卡罗尔还担心大规模采用AI的社会成本。她讲了一个认识的女性因为“ChatGPT告诉她”而断绝所有朋友关系的故事。“她向ChatGPT咨询她应该几点起床、几点睡觉、谁该做她的朋友。”这场位于坦帕郊区的集会，是近150场与“人类优先”组织相关的抗议活动之一。该组织自称是“致力于确保AI未来掌握在普通人手中的保守派社会运动”。卡罗尔列举了数据中心造成的一系列危害，其中有些说法比其他更可疑。她声称，数据中心附近牧场养的奶牛产下了死胎。超大规模设施从美国的对手中国采购零部件，威胁国家安全。“我们在自掘坟墓，”卡罗尔告诉我，“你知道那些芯片里有什么吗？”我说我不知道。“我们盲目信任中国人？”她难以置信地继续说，“让他们给我们芯片？”（与此同时，支持数据中心的保守派则声称中国正在资助美国的反AI运动。）全美各地的社区都在抵制数据中心建设。最近的盖洛普民调发现，七成美国人反对在自家附近建造数据中心。近三分之二的共和党人和75%的受访民主党人表示反对本地数据中心建设。美国人可能比以往更加两极分化，但对数据中心的抵制是跨党派的——而且可能巩固新的、令人惊讶的政治联盟。激发保守派抵制的担忧与激励自由派的担忧相似：数据中心建设和维护的环境成本；AI的伦理问题，从用于大规模政府监控到对用户心理健康的影响；以及担心附近有数据中心会损害生活质量。但右翼对数据中心的抵制背后也存在一些党派焦虑，主要是中国渗透美国技术的威胁。“人类优先”组织曾是AI安全中心的一个跨党派分支，现由艾米·克雷默领导。克雷默是一位长期从事保守派组织工作的活动家，可以说是最对共和党民粹主义转向负责的人之一。她是茶党运动的早期领袖，也是“女性支持特朗普”和“美国优先女性”的联合创始人，后者在华盛顿特区主办的“停止偷窃”集会演变成了1月6日的骚乱（克雷默并未参与冲击国会大厦）。尽管“人类优先”有党派倾向，克雷默也有政治资历，但这次在赫南多县举行的“人类优先”附属集会上，人们的关切显然是地方性的。一名竞选农业专员的消防员留下了标语牌；一名竞选县委员的共和党人也顺道拜访。集会以一场土地使用专家的讲座结束，他解释了如何修改分区法规来防止建造超大规模数据中心——即那些至少占地1万平方英尺、最低能耗100兆瓦的设施。在讲座之前，从事IT工作的“赫南多县无数据中心”成员金·贝朗格调出了一篇来自进步网站《更完美联盟》的关于数据中心的文章。尽管房间里的人偏保守，但这篇文章，她说，“信息量很大”。组织者帕蒂·齐赫特告诉我，她是在脸书上发现“人类优先”的。参加全国行动日是不假思索的选择，因为齐赫特和她的朋友们已经成立了“赫南多县无数据中心”来阻止社区内的数据中心建设。“我非常保守，完全不认同民主党相信的那一套，”齐赫特告诉我。但她也感到被佛罗里达州的共和党领导层背叛了。她曾是州长罗恩·德桑蒂斯的忠实粉丝——起初是这样——但在他第二个任期内，她说，“他一心只顾开发，邀请所有人来我们这里，毁掉我们的州。”德桑蒂斯将自己定位为AI怀疑论者，并于5月签署了一项法律，要求超大规模数据中心自行承担公用事业成本，并允许地方政府为数据中心项目制定用水规定。但据《坦帕湾时报》报道，他的政府给了数据中心开发商数百万美元的税收减免。据报道，州长曾推动修改该州的数据中心税收豁免政策，使其只适用于超大规模设施。在图书馆外设置宣传摊位时，齐赫特和她的朋友们讨论了德桑蒂斯的潜在继任者。“这可能是你一生中最重要的州长选举，”贝朗格告诉我。她还担心德桑蒂斯支持的一项投票修正案，该修正案将免除大多数房主的财产税，因为这会使县政府失去急需的收入——可能使数据中心这样的开发项目更具吸引力。“如果拜伦·唐纳德斯赢得初选，我就投民主党。我不会投给他。”齐赫特和其他人对共和党州长初选领跑者、众议员拜伦·唐纳德斯感到失望，因为他公开支持佛罗里达州的数据中心建设。“我真不明白特朗普为什么会支持他，”其中一人说。“我不反对数据中心。我支持有计划地建设，”唐纳德斯在2月表示。“数据中心将成为未来美国生活的一部分。”唐纳斯说他想为佛罗里达带来更多外部投资——这句话此后一直困扰着他。“我的愿景是让佛罗里达成为世界金融之都，”他在《国会山报》主办的一场活动上说。“这根本不是我们在佛罗里达想要的，”齐赫特告诉我。唐纳德斯对数据中心的支持现在正在让他失去本党支持者。最近的攻击广告聚焦于他获得的技术行业支持，包括来自“引领未来”的超过500万美元捐款，这是一个1亿美元的支持AI的超级政治行动委员会，其资助者包括安德森·霍洛维茨和帕兰提尔的乔·朗斯代尔，据《塔拉哈西民主党人报》报道，另外还有来自“美国使命”组织的50万美元，该组织本身也由“引领未来”资助。唐纳德斯似乎在支持他的行业和赢得初选所需的选民之间寻求中间立场。周一，他宣布了一项立法，旨在防止数据中心推高公用事业成本。在全州范围内，对数据中心的反对很普遍。据Politico报道，全州超过20个县市已讨论或通过了数据中心建设暂停令。在一些社区，反对声浪已足以吓退开发商。今年6月，锡特勒斯县一个有争议的数据中心提案的发起者在县委员会批准对数据中心重新分区申请实施一年暂停令后，撤回了在该社区建造800英亩数据中心的计划。“不幸的是，地方上的数据中心暂停令会发出一个信号，表明该地区对商业关闭大门，不仅是对数据中心，也包括其他重大经济发展项目，”行业游说团体数据中心联盟的州政策与政府事务执行副总裁丹·迪奥里奥告诉The Verge。但这正是齐赫特和其他组织者想要的结果。齐赫特解释说，她并非反对发展，但她不希望政府向与社区毫无联系的大开发商发放补贴。“如果能保持本地化，由本地建筑商来建，那没问题，因为他们知道如何在佛罗里达做事，”她说。“连我们的农业专员”——威尔顿·辛普森，共和党人——“也在让人进来使用除草剂和杀虫剂，喷洒我们的湖泊，杀死所有植被。”她担心AI如何助长大规模监控，这个问题在赫南多县警长办公室开始在本地道路上安装摄像头后变得尤其突出。“我们不希望人们失业，不希望我们的国家被AI统治，不希望像在中国那样生活，钱被控制，所做的一切都被监视，”她说。尽管齐赫特是终身共和党人，但数据中心问题——以及更大的发展问题——导致她做出了一些意想不到的政治妥协。“如果拜伦·唐纳德斯赢得初选，我就投民主党的票。我不会投给他，”她告诉我，并补充说她也愿意投票给反对数据中心的独立候选人。“我认为很多人都是这样。这不是党派问题。不是。这是关于人类生命的问题。”
